title: Short title, extent and commencement
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: This Act may be called the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023. It extends as provided under the Act and shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may appoint by notification in the Official Gazette.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the name of the law, specifies where it applies, and states that the Central Government decides when it comes into force.
examples: BNSS became operational on a date notified by the Central Government.
The Act is officially known as Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.

faqs.question: What is the official name of BNSS?
faqs.answer: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: Defines various terms used in the Sanhita including bail, bailable offence, cognizable offence, complaint, investigation, police station, victim, warrant-case and other expressions.
simpleExplanation: This section provides meanings of important legal terms used throughout BNSS.
examples: A cognizable offence allows police to arrest without warrant.
A victim includes the legal heir of a deceased victim.

faqs.question: Why is this section important?
faqs.answer: It explains the meaning of legal terms used throughout BNSS.

title: Construction of references
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: References to Magistrates in laws shall be construed according to the nature of functions involved and the provisions of this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how references to Magistrates should be interpreted in different laws.
examples: Judicial functions are exercised by Judicial Magistrates.
Administrative functions may be exercised by Executive Magistrates.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of this section?
faqs.answer: To clarify which type of Magistrate should perform specific functions.

title: Trial of offences under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and other laws
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: All offences under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and other laws shall be investigated, inquired into, tried and otherwise dealt with according to the provisions of this Sanhita unless otherwise provided.
simpleExplanation: This section states that criminal cases are generally handled according to BNSS procedures.
examples: A theft case under BNS follows BNSS procedure.
Special laws may provide different procedures.

faqs.question: Does BNSS apply to all criminal cases?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless another law provides a different procedure.

title: Saving
chapter: Chapter I - Preliminary
officialText: Nothing in this Sanhita affects any special or local law, special jurisdiction, power, or special procedure prescribed by any other law unless specifically provided.
simpleExplanation: BNSS does not override special laws unless it specifically says so.
examples: Special anti-corruption laws continue to apply.
Special courts operate under their own procedures where applicable.

faqs.question: Does BNSS override special laws?
faqs.answer: No, unless BNSS specifically provides otherwise.

title: Classes of Criminal Courts
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: There shall be Courts of Session, Judicial Magistrates of the first class, Judicial Magistrates of the second class and Executive Magistrates in every State.
simpleExplanation: This section lists the different criminal courts recognized under BNSS.
examples: A Sessions Court tries serious offences.
Magistrates handle less serious criminal matters.

faqs.question: Which criminal courts exist under BNSS?
faqs.answer: Sessions Courts, Judicial Magistrates and Executive Magistrates.

title: Territorial divisions
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: States shall consist of sessions divisions, districts and sub-divisions as determined according to the provisions of this section.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how criminal administration areas are divided geographically.
examples: A State may contain multiple districts.
Districts may be divided into sub-divisions.

faqs.question: Who determines sessions divisions and districts?
faqs.answer: The State Government after consultation with the High Court.

title: Court of Session
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government shall establish a Court of Session for every sessions division. The High Court appoints Sessions Judges and Additional Sessions Judges.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with the establishment and functioning of Sessions Courts.
examples: Murder cases are usually tried by Sessions Courts.
Additional Sessions Judges may be appointed.

faqs.question: Who appoints a Sessions Judge?
faqs.answer: The High Court.

title: Courts of Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Courts of Judicial Magistrates of the first class and second class shall be established at places specified by the State Government after consultation with the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the establishment of Magistrate Courts.
examples: Judicial Magistrates hear criminal complaints and conduct trials.
Special Magistrate Courts may be established.

faqs.question: Who establishes Magistrate Courts?
faqs.answer: The State Government in consultation with the High Court.

title: Chief Judicial Magistrate and Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The High Court shall appoint a Chief Judicial Magistrate in every district and may appoint Additional Chief Judicial Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for the appointment and powers of Chief Judicial Magistrates.
examples: Every district has a Chief Judicial Magistrate.
Additional Chief Judicial Magistrates may assist in administration.

faqs.question: Who appoints the Chief Judicial Magistrate?
faqs.answer: The High Court.

title: Special Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The High Court may, on the request of the Central Government or State Government, confer upon qualified persons all or any powers of a Judicial Magistrate of the first class or second class in respect of particular cases or classes of cases.
simpleExplanation: The High Court can appoint Special Judicial Magistrates with limited judicial powers for specific cases or areas.
examples: A retired judicial officer is appointed as a Special Judicial Magistrate.
A Special Judicial Magistrate handles specific categories of cases.

faqs.question: Who appoints Special Judicial Magistrates?
faqs.answer: The High Court.

title: Local Jurisdiction of Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The Chief Judicial Magistrate may define the local limits within which Magistrates may exercise powers under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: This section determines the geographical area where a Judicial Magistrate can exercise authority.
examples: A Magistrate may be assigned jurisdiction over a specific district.
Jurisdiction limits can be modified by competent authority.

faqs.question: Who determines a Magistrate's local jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: The Chief Judicial Magistrate subject to BNSS provisions.

title: Subordination of Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: Chief Judicial Magistrates are subordinate to the Sessions Judge and other Judicial Magistrates are subordinate to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the hierarchy among Judicial Magistrates.
examples: A Judicial Magistrate follows administrative directions from the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
The Sessions Judge supervises the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

faqs.question: Who supervises Judicial Magistrates?
faqs.answer: The Chief Judicial Magistrate and Sessions Judge.

title: Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may appoint Executive Magistrates in every district and appoint one of them as District Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for appointment of Executive Magistrates and District Magistrates.
examples: A District Magistrate oversees executive functions in a district.
Sub-Divisional Magistrates may also be appointed.

faqs.question: Who appoints Executive Magistrates?
faqs.answer: The State Government.

title: Special Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may appoint Special Executive Magistrates for specific areas or functions and confer upon them powers of Executive Magistrates.
simpleExplanation: Special Executive Magistrates can be appointed for special administrative or law-and-order duties.
examples: A Special Executive Magistrate is appointed for election duties.
Special powers are granted for a specific area.

faqs.question: Why are Special Executive Magistrates appointed?
faqs.answer: To perform specific functions or serve specific areas.

title: Local Jurisdiction of Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The District Magistrate may define the local limits within which Executive Magistrates exercise powers under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: This section determines where Executive Magistrates can exercise authority.
examples: An Executive Magistrate is assigned a specific subdivision.
Jurisdiction can extend across a district.

faqs.question: Who defines the jurisdiction of Executive Magistrates?
faqs.answer: The District Magistrate.

title: Subordination of Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: All Executive Magistrates are subordinate to the District Magistrate and, where applicable, to the Sub-Divisional Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the administrative hierarchy among Executive Magistrates.
examples: Executive Magistrates work under the District Magistrate.
Sub-Divisional Magistrates supervise local executive work.

faqs.question: To whom are Executive Magistrates subordinate?
faqs.answer: The District Magistrate and, in some cases, the Sub-Divisional Magistrate.

title: Public Prosecutors
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The Central Government or State Government shall appoint Public Prosecutors and Additional Public Prosecutors for conducting prosecutions, appeals and proceedings before courts.
simpleExplanation: This section governs appointment and qualifications of Public Prosecutors.
examples: A Public Prosecutor represents the State in criminal trials.
A Special Public Prosecutor may be appointed for specific cases.

faqs.question: Who appoints Public Prosecutors?
faqs.answer: The Central Government or State Government, depending on the case.

title: Assistant Public Prosecutors
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government shall appoint Assistant Public Prosecutors for conducting prosecutions in Magistrates' Courts.
simpleExplanation: Assistant Public Prosecutors mainly conduct criminal cases before Magistrate Courts.
examples: An Assistant Public Prosecutor conducts a trial in a Magistrate Court.
The Government appoints APPs for districts.

faqs.question: What is the role of an Assistant Public Prosecutor?
faqs.answer: To conduct prosecutions before Magistrate Courts.

title: Directorate of Prosecution
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may establish a Directorate of Prosecution consisting of a Director of Prosecution and other officers to supervise and monitor prosecutions.
simpleExplanation: This section creates the administrative structure for managing criminal prosecutions in a State.
examples: The Director of Prosecution supervises serious criminal cases.
Deputy Directors monitor prosecution performance.

faqs.question: What is the Directorate of Prosecution?
faqs.answer: A State-level body responsible for supervision and management of prosecutions.

title: Directorate of Prosecution
chapter: Chapter II - Constitution of Criminal Courts and Offices
officialText: The State Government may establish a Directorate of Prosecution consisting of a Director of Prosecution and Deputy Directors of Prosecution to supervise prosecutions in the State.
simpleExplanation: This section creates a Directorate of Prosecution to supervise and improve criminal prosecutions.
examples: The Director of Prosecution oversees prosecution policies across the State.
Deputy Directors assist in monitoring criminal cases.

faqs.question: What is the Directorate of Prosecution?
faqs.answer: A State-level body responsible for supervising and coordinating prosecutions.

title: Courts by Which Offences are Triable
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: Offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and other laws shall be tried by the High Court, Court of Session, or other Courts as specified under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies which criminal courts can try different offences.
examples: Serious offences may be tried by a Sessions Court.
Minor offences may be tried by Magistrates.

faqs.question: Which court will try a criminal offence?
faqs.answer: The court depends on the nature and seriousness of the offence.

title: Sentences Which High Courts and Sessions Judges May Pass
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: A High Court may pass any sentence authorised by law. A Sessions Judge or Additional Sessions Judge may pass any sentence authorised by law, subject to confirmation requirements where applicable.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the sentencing powers of High Courts and Sessions Courts.
examples: A Sessions Court may award life imprisonment.
Certain sentences may require confirmation by the High Court.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Court award life imprisonment?
faqs.answer: Yes, if authorized by law.

title: Sentences Which Magistrates May Pass
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: Judicial Magistrates may pass sentences within the limits prescribed by this Sanhita according to their class and authority.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the maximum punishments that Magistrates can impose.
examples: A Chief Judicial Magistrate can impose higher sentences than a Judicial Magistrate of the Second Class.
Sentencing powers vary by rank.

faqs.question: Can all Magistrates award the same sentence?
faqs.answer: No. Sentencing powers depend on the class of Magistrate.

title: Sentence of Imprisonment in Default of Fine
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: A Magistrate may award imprisonment in default of payment of fine within the limits prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: If a convicted person does not pay a court-imposed fine, imprisonment may be ordered according to law.
punishment: Imprisonment in default of payment of fine.
examples: A person fails to pay a court fine and serves the default sentence.
Default imprisonment is separate from the main punishment.

faqs.question: What happens if a court fine is not paid?
faqs.answer: The court may order imprisonment as permitted by law.

title: Sentence in Cases of Conviction of Several Offences at One Trial
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: When a person is convicted of multiple offences at one trial, the Court may impose separate punishments and direct whether they run concurrently or consecutively, subject to statutory limits. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
simpleExplanation: If a person is convicted of multiple offences in the same trial, the Court decides how the sentences will operate.
examples: A person convicted of theft and forgery receives separate sentences.
The Court may order sentences to run together or one after another.

faqs.question: Can a Court impose multiple punishments in one trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, for each offence proved.

title: Mode of Conferring Powers
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: The High Court or State Government may confer powers under this Sanhita on persons by name, office, or class of officials. Such orders take effect upon communication. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
simpleExplanation: This section explains how legal powers are formally granted to officials.
examples: The State Government grants powers to a class of officers.
The High Court authorizes specific judicial functions.

faqs.question: Who can confer powers under BNSS?
faqs.answer: The High Court or State Government, depending on the provision.

title: Powers of Officers Appointed
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: An officer appointed to an equal or higher office shall continue to exercise powers previously conferred unless otherwise directed. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: Officials promoted or transferred to equivalent positions generally retain their legal powers.
examples: A promoted officer continues exercising previously granted powers.
Existing authority remains unless withdrawn.

faqs.question: Do powers continue after promotion?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, unless otherwise directed.

title: Withdrawal of Powers
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: The High Court, State Government, Chief Judicial Magistrate, or District Magistrate may withdraw powers previously conferred under this Sanhita. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
simpleExplanation: Authorities that grant powers may also withdraw them.
examples: Special powers granted to an officer are revoked.
Administrative authority is withdrawn by competent authority.

faqs.question: Can powers granted under BNSS be withdrawn?
faqs.answer: Yes, by the authority that granted them.

title: Powers of Judges and Magistrates Exercisable by Successors-in-Office
chapter: Chapter III - Powers of Courts
officialText: The powers and duties of a Judge or Magistrate may be exercised by his successor-in-office. Questions regarding succession shall be determined by the competent authority. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
simpleExplanation: When a Judge or Magistrate is transferred, retires, or is replaced, the successor can continue the work.
examples: A new Magistrate continues a pending proceeding.
A successor Judge completes a case started by another Judge.

faqs.question: Can a new Judge continue an old case?
faqs.answer: Yes, the successor-in-office can exercise the same powers.

title: Powers of Superior Officers of Police
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Police officers superior in rank to an officer in charge of a police station may exercise the same powers throughout the local area to which they are appointed as may be exercised by such officer within the limits of his station. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
simpleExplanation: Senior police officers can exercise the same powers as a Station House Officer (SHO) within their jurisdiction.
examples: A Superintendent of Police may exercise powers available to a Station House Officer.
A Deputy Commissioner of Police can supervise and perform police functions within their area.

faqs.question: Can senior police officers exercise SHO powers?
faqs.answer: Yes, within their jurisdiction.

title: Public When to Assist Magistrates and Police
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Every person is bound to assist a Magistrate or police officer reasonably demanding aid in making arrests, preventing escapes, suppressing breaches of peace, or preventing injury to public property. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
simpleExplanation: Citizens must help police or Magistrates when lawfully asked to assist in maintaining law and order.
punishment: Failure may attract consequences under applicable laws.
examples: Helping police stop a suspect from escaping.
Assisting authorities during a riot.

faqs.question: Am I legally required to assist police?
faqs.answer: Yes, when lawfully required under this section.

title: Aid to Person Executing Warrant
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: When a warrant is directed to a person other than a police officer, any other person may assist in executing the warrant if the person to whom it is directed is present and acting in its execution. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: People may help a person who is legally executing a warrant.
examples: Citizens assist in serving a lawful warrant.
Officials receive assistance during execution of a court order.

faqs.question: Can a private person assist in executing a warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes, under lawful supervision.

title: Public to Give Information of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Every person aware of the commission or intention to commit certain serious offences must provide information to the nearest Magistrate or police officer.
simpleExplanation: Citizens have a legal duty to report serious crimes and planned offences.
punishment: Failure may attract penalties under applicable law.
examples: Reporting knowledge of a planned murder.
Informing police about a kidnapping.

faqs.question: Must I report serious crimes?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you have relevant information.

title: Duty of Officers Employed in Connection with Affairs of a Village
chapter: Chapter IV - Powers of Superior Officers of Police and Aid to the Magistrates and the Police
officialText: Village officers and persons connected with village administration shall communicate information regarding crimes, suspicious activities, and offenders to authorities.
simpleExplanation: Village officials must help law enforcement by reporting crimes and suspicious activities.
examples: A village officer reports the presence of a proclaimed offender.
Suspicious movements are reported to police.

faqs.question: What is the duty of village officials under BNSS?
faqs.answer: They must assist authorities by reporting crime-related information.

title: Arrest on Refusal to Give Name and Residence
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A person who commits a non-cognizable offence in the presence of a police officer and refuses to provide name or address, or provides false details, may be arrested.
simpleExplanation: Police may arrest a person who refuses to identify themselves after committing an offence.
punishment: Detention until identity is established, subject to law.
examples: A person caught committing a minor offence refuses to provide their name.
False identity information is given to police.

faqs.question: Can police arrest me for refusing to identify myself?
faqs.answer: Yes, in circumstances specified by law.

title: Arrest by Private Person and Procedure on Such Arrest
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A private person may arrest an individual who commits a non-bailable and cognizable offence in their presence and shall immediately hand over such person to the police.
simpleExplanation: Citizens may make a lawful arrest in limited circumstances and must promptly hand the person to police.
examples: A citizen restrains a robber caught in the act.
The arrested person is handed to police immediately.

faqs.question: Can a citizen arrest someone?
faqs.answer: Yes, in limited circumstances allowed by law.

title: Arrest by Magistrate
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A Magistrate may arrest or order the arrest of a person committing an offence in the Magistrate's presence and may commit the person to custody.
simpleExplanation: Magistrates have authority to arrest offenders or direct their arrest in appropriate situations.
examples: An offence occurs inside a courtroom.
A Magistrate orders immediate arrest.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate personally order an arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes, under circumstances provided by law.

title: Protection of Members of Armed Forces from Arrest
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Members of the Armed Forces are protected from arrest for acts done in discharge of official duties except with the consent of the Central Government where required.
simpleExplanation: Special safeguards apply before arresting Armed Forces personnel for official actions.
examples: Permission is sought before prosecuting a soldier for official acts.
Military personnel receive statutory protection.

faqs.question: Can Armed Forces personnel be arrested for official acts?
faqs.answer: Only according to the safeguards provided by law.

title: Arrest How Made
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer or other person making an arrest shall actually touch or confine the body of the person to be arrested unless there is submission to custody by word or action.
simpleExplanation: This section explains the legal procedure for carrying out an arrest.
examples: A suspect voluntarily surrenders to police.
Police physically detain a person resisting arrest.

faqs.question: Is physical contact always required for arrest?
faqs.answer: Not if the person voluntarily submits to custody.

title: Arrest by Magistrate
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When any offence is committed in the presence of a Magistrate, whether Executive or Judicial, within his local jurisdiction, he may himself arrest or order any person to arrest the offender and may commit the offender to custody subject to the provisions relating to bail. A Magistrate may also arrest or direct the arrest of any person for whose arrest he is competent to issue a warrant. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate can directly arrest a person or order their arrest if an offence is committed in the Magistrate's presence.
examples: A person creates a disturbance inside a courtroom and is arrested on the Magistrate's order.
A Magistrate directs police to arrest a person for whom a warrant could legally be issued.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate personally order an arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. BNSS gives Magistrates authority to arrest or direct arrest in specified situations.

title: Protection of Members of Armed Forces from Arrest
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: No member of the Armed Forces of the Union shall be arrested for anything done or purported to be done in the discharge of official duties except after obtaining the consent of the Central Government. The State Government may extend similar protection to specified forces maintaining public order. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
simpleExplanation: Members of the Armed Forces receive legal protection from arrest for actions performed while carrying out official duties.
examples: Prior government approval may be required before arresting a soldier for an act done during official duty.
Special protections apply to certain security forces.

faqs.question: Can Armed Forces personnel be arrested?
faqs.answer: Yes, but special legal safeguards and approvals may apply.

title: Arrest How Made
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: In making an arrest, the police officer or other person making the arrest shall actually touch or confine the body of the person unless there is submission to custody by word or action. Reasonable force may be used where necessary to effect the arrest. Special safeguards apply to the arrest of women. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: This section explains the legal method of making an arrest and the use of reasonable force.
examples: A suspect voluntarily surrenders to police custody.
Police use reasonable force when a suspect attempts to escape.

faqs.question: Is physical contact always required for arrest?
faqs.answer: No. If a person voluntarily submits to custody, physical contact may not be necessary.

title: Search of Place Entered by Person Sought to Be Arrested
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: If a person sought to be arrested enters or is within a place, persons residing in or in charge of that place shall permit lawful entry and search. Police may break open doors or windows where necessary after complying with the requirements of law. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
simpleExplanation: Police can enter and search premises where a wanted person is believed to be hiding.
punishment: Obstruction may attract legal consequences under applicable laws.
examples: Police search a house where an accused is hiding.
Authorities enter a locked building to execute a lawful arrest.

faqs.question: Can police enter a house to arrest a suspect?
faqs.answer: Yes, when authorized by law and following prescribed procedures.

title: Pursuit of Offenders into Other Jurisdictions
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer may pursue into any place in India a person whom he is authorized to arrest without warrant. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
simpleExplanation: Police can continue chasing and arresting a suspect even if the suspect crosses district or State boundaries.
examples: Police pursue a suspect from Karnataka into Kerala.
A wanted accused is arrested in another State.

faqs.question: Can police chase a suspect into another State?
faqs.answer: Yes. BNSS permits pursuit throughout India.

title: No Unnecessary Restraint
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: The person arrested shall not be subjected to more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}
simpleExplanation: An arrested person should not be restrained more than necessary.
examples: Excessive restraints should not be used without necessity.
Only reasonable measures may be taken to prevent escape.

faqs.question: Can police use excessive restraints?
faqs.answer: No. Only the minimum necessary restraint is permitted.

title: Person Arrested to Be Informed of Grounds of Arrest and of Right to Bail
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Every police officer or other person arresting any person without warrant shall forthwith communicate the grounds of arrest. Where the offence is bailable, the arrested person shall also be informed of the right to be released on bail. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}
simpleExplanation: Anyone arrested must be told why they are being arrested and informed of their right to bail where applicable.
examples: Police explain the charges at the time of arrest.
An accused in a bailable offence is informed about bail.

faqs.question: Must police tell me why I am being arrested?
faqs.answer: Yes. The grounds of arrest must be communicated immediately.

title: Obligation of Person Making Arrest to Inform About Arrest to Relative or Friend
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Every person making an arrest shall inform a relative, friend, or nominated person about the arrest and place of detention. The arrested person must be informed of this right, and appropriate records must be maintained. The Magistrate shall ensure compliance. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}
simpleExplanation: Police must notify a relative, friend, or nominated person when someone is arrested.
examples: Police inform the spouse of an arrested person.
A friend nominated by the accused receives notification.

faqs.question: Can my family be informed if I am arrested?
faqs.answer: Yes. BNSS requires notification of a relative, friend, or nominated person.

title: Search of Arrested Person
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer may search an arrested person and place in safe custody articles found upon him, other than necessary wearing apparel. A receipt of seized articles shall be provided. Searches of females must be conducted by another female with strict regard to decency. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}
simpleExplanation: Police can search an arrested person and secure items found during the search.
examples: A mobile phone recovered during arrest is documented and seized.
A female detainee is searched only by a female officer.

faqs.question: Can police search me after arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. A lawful search may be conducted following arrest.

title: Power to Seize Offensive Weapons
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: The police officer or other person making an arrest may take from the arrested person any offensive weapons found on him and shall deliver them to the appropriate Court or authority. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}
simpleExplanation: Weapons found on an arrested person may be confiscated for safety and evidence purposes.
examples: A knife recovered during arrest is seized.
A firearm found on the accused is handed over to authorities.

faqs.question: Can police confiscate weapons during arrest?
faqs.answer: Yes. Offensive weapons may be seized during a lawful arrest.

title: Examination of Accused by Medical Practitioner at Request of Police Officer
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a person is arrested and there are reasonable grounds for believing that examination of the person will afford evidence relating to the offence, a registered medical practitioner may examine the accused at the request of a police officer. Female examinations shall be conducted only by or under the supervision of a female registered medical practitioner. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
simpleExplanation: Police can request a medical examination of an accused person if it may provide evidence related to the crime.
examples: Collection of blood samples in a drunk driving case.
Recording injury marks on an accused person.

faqs.question: Can police require a medical examination of an accused?
faqs.answer: Yes, when it may provide evidence relating to the offence.

title: Examination of Person Accused of Rape by Medical Practitioner
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: When a person is arrested on a charge of rape or attempt to commit rape, examination by a registered medical practitioner may be conducted for the purpose of collecting evidence relating to the offence.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the procedure for medical examination of a person accused of rape.
examples: DNA evidence is collected from an accused.
Medical findings are documented and reported.

faqs.question: Why is a medical examination conducted in rape cases?
faqs.answer: To collect scientific evidence relevant to the investigation.

title: Examination of Arrested Person by Medical Officer
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Every arrested person may be medically examined by a government medical officer or registered medical practitioner. The examination report shall record injuries or marks of violence and a copy shall be provided to the arrested person. Female arrested persons shall be examined only by or under the supervision of a female medical officer or practitioner. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
simpleExplanation: This section protects arrested persons by ensuring medical examination and documentation of injuries.
examples: Injuries present at the time of arrest are recorded.
A copy of the medical report is provided to the accused.

faqs.question: Can an arrested person obtain a medical report?
faqs.answer: Yes, a copy of the report must be furnished.

title: Identification of Person Arrested
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Where identification of an arrested person is necessary for investigation, the Court may direct the person to submit to identification. Identification involving persons with disabilities shall be conducted under Magistrate supervision and recorded by audio-video means. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: Courts may order identification procedures such as test identification parades.
examples: A witness identifies a robbery suspect.
An identification parade is conducted under supervision.

faqs.question: Who authorizes identification of an accused?
faqs.answer: The Court having jurisdiction.

title: Procedure When Police Officer Deputes Subordinate to Arrest Without Warrant
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: Where a police officer directs a subordinate officer to make an arrest without warrant, a written order specifying the person to be arrested and grounds of arrest shall be provided. simpleExplanation: A subordinate police officer must carry proper authorization before making an arrest on behalf of a superior officer.
examples: A Sub-Inspector arrests an accused based on written instructions from the SHO.

title: Health and Safety of Arrested Person
chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: It shall be the duty of the person having custody of an accused to take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
simpleExplanation: Authorities must ensure the safety and well-being of persons in custody.

Ensuring safe conditions in custody.

chapter: Chapter V - Arrest of Persons
officialText: A police officer making an arrest without warrant shall, without unnecessary delay and subject to the provisions relating to bail, take or send the arrested person before a Magistrate having jurisdiction or before the officer in charge of a police station. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

title: Person Arrested Not to Be Detained More Than Twenty-Four Hours

officialText: No police officer shall detain a person arrested without warrant for more than twenty-four hours, excluding travel time, unless authorized by a Magistrate under law. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

title: Police to Report Apprehensions

Arrest reports are maintained for oversight.

title: Power, on Escape, to Pursue and Retake

title: Arrest to Be Made Strictly According to Sanhita

title: Form of Summons

title: Service When Persons Summoned Cannot Be Found
chapter: Chapter VI - Processes to Compel Appearance
officialText: Where the person summoned cannot, despite due diligence, be found, the summons may be served by leaving a duplicate with an adult member of the family residing with that person. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

title: Proof of Service in Such Cases and When Serving Officer Not Present

title: Service of Summons on Witness

title: Form of Warrant of Arrest and Duration

officialText: If a Court has reason to believe that a person against whom a warrant has been issued has absconded or is concealing himself so that the warrant cannot be executed, the Court may publish a written proclamation requiring such person to appear at a specified place and time, not less than thirty days from the date of publication.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate personally oversee a search?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Power to Impound Document, etc., Produced
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Any Court may, if it thinks fit, impound any document or thing produced before it under this Sanhita. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}
simpleExplanation: Courts may retain documents or items produced before them when necessary.
examples: A forged document is retained by the Court.
Evidence records are impounded.

faqs.question: Can a Court keep documents submitted before it?
faqs.answer: Yes, by impounding them.

title: Reciprocal Arrangements Regarding Processes
chapter: Chapter VII - Processes to Compel Production of Things
officialText: Courts may send summonses, warrants and search warrants for service or execution in other States, Union Territories or foreign countries having reciprocal arrangements with India. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}
simpleExplanation: This section enables cross-border and inter-state execution of criminal process.
examples: A summons is served in another country under a treaty arrangement.
A search warrant is executed through reciprocal legal assistance.

faqs.question: Can Indian courts send warrants abroad?
faqs.answer: Yes, where reciprocal arrangements exist.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: In this Chapter, unless the context otherwise requires,— (a) 'contracting State' means any country or place outside India in respect of which arrangements have been made by the Central Government with the Government of such country through a treaty or otherwise; (b) 'identifying' includes establishment of a proof that the property was derived from, or used in, the commission of an offence; (c) 'proceeds of crime' means any property derived or obtained directly or indirectly, by any person as a result of criminal activity (including crime involving currency transfers) or the value of any such property; (d) 'property' means property and assets of every description whether corporeal or incorporeal, movable or immovable, tangible or intangible and deeds and instruments evidencing title to, or interest in, such property or assets derived or used in the commission of an offence and includes property obtained through proceeds of crime; (e) 'tracing' means determining the nature, source, disposition, movement, title or ownership of property.
simpleExplanation: This section defines important terms used in the chapter dealing with international cooperation and criminal property forfeiture.
examples: Money earned from fraud is considered proceeds of crime.
Property purchased using criminal funds can be treated as criminal property.

faqs.question: What is a contracting State?
faqs.answer: A foreign country with which India has entered into a treaty or arrangement for legal assistance.

title: Letter of Request to Competent Authority for Investigation in a Country or Place Outside India
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: If, during investigation of an offence, it appears that evidence may be available in a country or place outside India, any Criminal Court may issue a letter of request to a Court or authority in that country or place competent to deal with such request for examination of persons, production of documents or things, and forwarding of evidence to the Court issuing the request. The letter shall be transmitted in the manner specified by the Central Government. Evidence received shall be deemed to have been collected during investigation under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: Indian courts can seek evidence from foreign countries through an official letter of request.
examples: A bank account located abroad is investigated through a foreign authority.
Witness statements are recorded in another country and sent to India.

faqs.question: Can Indian courts obtain evidence from another country?
faqs.answer: Yes, through a letter of request under this section.

title: Letter of Request from a Country or Place Outside India to a Court or an Authority for Investigation in India
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Upon receipt of a letter of request from a Court or authority in a country or place outside India for examination of a person or production of a document or thing relating to an offence under investigation in that country, the Central Government may forward it to the appropriate Judicial Magistrate or police officer for execution in India. The investigation shall be carried out as if the offence had been committed within India.
simpleExplanation: India may assist foreign countries in collecting evidence for criminal investigations.
examples: A foreign court requests examination of a witness residing in India.
Indian authorities collect documents requested by a foreign government.

faqs.question: Can foreign countries seek assistance from India?
faqs.answer: Yes, through letters of request sent under reciprocal arrangements.

title: Assistance in Securing Transfer of Persons
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Provides for securing the transfer of persons in custody for giving evidence or assisting investigations pursuant to reciprocal arrangements with foreign States.
simpleExplanation: Persons in custody may be transferred between countries for criminal investigations or court proceedings.
examples: A prisoner is temporarily transferred to provide testimony.
A detained witness is produced before a foreign court.

faqs.question: Can a prisoner be transferred for investigation purposes?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to legal safeguards and reciprocal arrangements.

title: Assistance in Relation to Orders of Attachment or Forfeiture of Property
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where a Court in India or a contracting State seeks execution of attachment or forfeiture orders concerning criminal property, assistance may be provided in accordance with reciprocal arrangements and directions of the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: India and foreign countries can help each other seize criminal assets.
punishment: Property may be attached or forfeited.
examples: Assets hidden abroad are frozen.
Foreign authorities assist in recovering criminal proceeds.

faqs.question: Can India recover criminal assets located abroad?
faqs.answer: Yes, through reciprocal legal assistance mechanisms.

title: Identifying Unlawfully Acquired Property
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The Court shall direct a police officer not below the rank of Sub-Inspector to take steps necessary for tracing and identifying property. Such steps may include inquiries, investigations and surveys relating to persons, places, assets, documents, bank accounts or financial institutions.
simpleExplanation: Authorities may investigate and trace assets suspected to be linked to criminal activity.
examples: Investigators trace proceeds of fraud.
Bank records are examined to locate criminal assets.

faqs.question: Who identifies criminal property?
faqs.answer: A police officer acting under directions of the Court.

title: Seizure or Attachment of Property
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where an officer has reason to believe that property under investigation is likely to be concealed, transferred or disposed of, he may seize such property or attach it, subject to confirmation by the Court within thirty days.
simpleExplanation: Property suspected to be linked to crime can be seized or frozen to prevent disposal.
punishment: Property may remain under attachment pending Court orders.
examples: A suspect's bank account is frozen.
Land purchased using criminal proceeds is attached.

faqs.question: Can property be attached before conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the law's conditions are satisfied.

title: Management of Properties Seized or Forfeited Under this Chapter
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The Court may appoint the District Magistrate or another nominated officer as Administrator to receive, manage and dispose of property attached or forfeited under this Chapter in accordance with directions of the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: An Administrator manages property seized or forfeited during criminal proceedings.
examples: A District Magistrate manages attached land.
Forfeited property is disposed of according to law.

faqs.question: Who manages attached property?
faqs.answer: An Administrator appointed by the Court.

title: Notice of Forfeiture of Property
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where the Court has reason to believe that properties are proceeds of crime, it may issue a notice requiring the affected person to explain the source of income, earnings or assets and show cause why the property should not be declared proceeds of crime and forfeited to the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: The owner is given an opportunity to explain the lawful source of the property.
punishment: Failure to establish lawful ownership may result in forfeiture.
examples: A suspect receives notice regarding unexplained assets.
The Court seeks proof of lawful income.

faqs.question: Why is a forfeiture notice issued?
faqs.answer: To allow the affected person to explain the source of the property.

title: Forfeiture of Property in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: After considering the explanation and evidence, the Court may record a finding that the property is proceeds of crime. Such property shall stand forfeited to the Central Government free from all encumbrances. Where shares in a company are forfeited, the company shall register the Central Government as transferee.
simpleExplanation: If property is proven to be proceeds of crime, it can be permanently confiscated by the Government.
examples: Illegal assets are confiscated by the Government.
Shares acquired through criminal activity are transferred to the Central Government.

faqs.question: When does property get forfeited?
faqs.answer: When the Court finds that it represents proceeds of crime.

title: Fine in lieu of forfeiture
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Where the Court makes a declaration that property stands forfeited under section 120 and only part of the property's source remains unproved, the Court may give the affected person an option to pay a fine equal to the market value of that part. After hearing the person, and upon payment of the fine, the Court may revoke the forfeiture declaration and release the property. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: Instead of losing the entire property, a person may be allowed to pay a fine equal to the value of the unexplained portion.
punishment: Fine equal to market value of unexplained portion of property.
examples: Only part of a property's funding source cannot be explained.
The Court allows payment of a fine instead of forfeiture.

faqs.question: Can forfeiture be avoided?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Court allows payment of a fine under this section.

title: Certain transfers to be null and void
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Any transfer of property made after an attachment order under section 117 or a notice under section 119 shall be ignored for proceedings under this Chapter. If the property is later forfeited, such transfer shall be deemed null and void. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
simpleExplanation: A person cannot avoid forfeiture by transferring property after attachment or notice proceedings begin.
examples: Property is transferred to a relative after attachment.
Sale of property after forfeiture notice is ignored.

faqs.question: Can I transfer property after receiving a forfeiture notice?
faqs.answer: Such transfers may be treated as void.

title: Procedure in respect of letter of request
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: Every letter of request, summons or warrant received from or transmitted to a contracting State shall be transmitted in such form and manner as specified by the Central Government through notification. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
simpleExplanation: This section specifies how international legal requests and warrants are exchanged.
examples: A request for evidence is sent to a foreign country.
A foreign summons is received through official channels.

faqs.question: Who decides the format of international legal requests?
faqs.answer: The Central Government.

title: Application of Chapter
chapter: Chapter VIII - Reciprocal Arrangements for Assistance in Certain Matters and Procedure for Attachment and Forfeiture of Property
officialText: The Central Government may direct that the provisions of this Chapter, in relation to a contracting State, shall apply subject to conditions, exceptions, or qualifications specified in a notification. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}
simpleExplanation: The Government may customize how these international cooperation provisions apply to specific countries.
examples: Special conditions apply to cooperation with a particular country.
A notification modifies procedural requirements.

faqs.question: Can the Government modify how this Chapter applies?
faqs.answer: Yes, through official notifications.

title: Security for keeping the peace on conviction
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a Court of Session or Magistrate of the First Class convicts a person of specified offences and considers it necessary to secure future peace, it may require execution of a bond or bail bond for keeping the peace for a period not exceeding three years. If the conviction is set aside, the bond becomes void. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}
simpleExplanation: Courts may require convicted persons to give a security bond promising to maintain peace.
punishment: Execution of bond up to three years.
examples: A person convicted of criminal intimidation is ordered to execute a peace bond.
A bond becomes void if the conviction is overturned.

faqs.question: What is a peace bond?
faqs.answer: A legal undertaking to maintain peace and good conduct.

title: Security for keeping the peace in other cases
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a Magistrate receives information that a person is likely to commit a breach of peace or disturb public tranquillity, the Magistrate may require that person to show cause why he should not execute a bond for keeping the peace.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate may act before violence or public disorder occurs.
examples: Two groups are preparing for a violent clash.
A person repeatedly threatens public disorder.

faqs.question: Can action be taken before an offence occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes, to prevent breaches of peace.

title: Security for good behaviour from persons disseminating seditious matters
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: Where a person disseminates material that may disturb public order or encourage offences against the State, a Magistrate may require security for good behaviour.
simpleExplanation: Authorities may require a bond from persons spreading harmful or dangerous material.
examples: Distribution of material promoting violence.
Publication encouraging public disorder.

faqs.question: What is security for good behaviour?
faqs.answer: A bond requiring lawful and peaceful conduct.

title: Security for good behaviour from suspected persons
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: A Magistrate may require security for good behaviour from a person reasonably suspected of concealing his presence with a view to committing a cognizable offence.
simpleExplanation: Suspicious persons believed to be preparing crimes may be required to provide security.
examples: A person is found hiding near a target location.
Authorities suspect preparation for a crime.

faqs.question: Can a suspected offender be required to furnish security?
faqs.answer: Yes, under preventive provisions.

title: Security for good behaviour from habitual offenders
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: A Magistrate may require a habitual offender, receiver of stolen property, habitual cheat, or similar offender to furnish security for good behaviour.
simpleExplanation: Repeat offenders may be required to provide a bond ensuring future lawful conduct.
examples: A habitual thief is ordered to furnish security.
A repeat fraud offender is placed under bond.

faqs.question: Who is a habitual offender?
faqs.answer: A person repeatedly involved in criminal activities.

title: Order to be made
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a Magistrate acts under sections 126 to 129, an order in writing shall be made setting forth the substance of the information received, the amount of bond required, the term of the bond, and the number and character of sureties, if any.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate must formally record the reasons and conditions before requiring a security bond.
examples: The order specifies bond amount and duration.
Surety requirements are recorded in writing.

faqs.question: What details must a Magistrate include in the order?
faqs.answer: Information received, bond amount, duration, and surety requirements.

title: Procedure in Respect of Person Present in Court
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If the person in respect of whom an order under section 130 is made is present in Court, the Magistrate shall read over the order to him, or if necessary explain its substance.
simpleExplanation: When the person is already before the Magistrate, the order must be read or explained to them.
examples: A person present in Court is informed about the security proceedings.
The Magistrate explains the bond requirements.

faqs.question: Must the order be explained to the person?
faqs.answer: Yes, if necessary.

title: Summons or Warrant in Case of Person Not So Present
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If the person is not present in Court, the Magistrate may issue a summons requiring attendance, or, where immediate arrest is necessary, a warrant.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate may call the person to Court or order arrest in appropriate cases.
examples: A summons is issued for appearance.
A warrant is issued due to risk of absconding.

faqs.question: Can a warrant be issued directly?
faqs.answer: Yes, where justified by circumstances.

title: Copy of Order to Accompany Summons or Warrant
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: Every summons or warrant issued under section 132 shall be accompanied by a copy of the order made under section 130, and such copy shall be delivered to the person served or arrested. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
simpleExplanation: A person must receive a copy of the order along with the summons or warrant.
examples: A copy of the Magistrate's order accompanies a summons.
An arrested person receives the order.

faqs.question: Must a copy of the order be supplied?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Power to Dispense with Personal Attendance
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: The Magistrate may, if sufficient cause is shown, dispense with the personal attendance of a person and permit appearance through an advocate. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
simpleExplanation: In suitable cases, personal attendance may not be required.
examples: A sick person appears through counsel.
The Magistrate exempts personal attendance.

faqs.question: Can a lawyer appear instead of the person?
faqs.answer: Yes, with permission of the Magistrate.

title: Inquiry as to Truth of Information
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When the person appears or is brought before the Magistrate, the Magistrate shall inquire into the truth of the information upon which action has been taken and take such further evidence as necessary. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate must verify whether preventive action is justified.
examples: Witnesses are examined regarding a threat to public peace.
Evidence is recorded before requiring security.

faqs.question: Is an inquiry mandatory?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Order to Give Security
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If upon inquiry the Magistrate finds sufficient grounds, the person may be ordered to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour.
simpleExplanation: After inquiry, a bond may be ordered if preventive action is justified.
examples: A person likely to disturb peace is ordered to furnish security.
Sureties are required for future good behaviour.

faqs.question: When can security be ordered?
faqs.answer: After the Magistrate is satisfied on inquiry.

title: Discharge of Person Informed Against
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If the Magistrate finds that there is no sufficient ground for proceeding, the person shall be discharged.
simpleExplanation: The case ends if allegations are not proved.
examples: Evidence fails to support the complaint.
The Magistrate closes proceedings.

faqs.question: What if allegations are false?
faqs.answer: The person is discharged.

title: Commencement of Period for Which Security is Required
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: The period for which security is required shall commence from the date of execution of the bond unless otherwise provided.
simpleExplanation: The bond period generally starts when the bond is executed.
examples: A one-year bond begins on the date it is signed.

faqs.question: When does the bond period start?
faqs.answer: From the execution of the bond.

title: Contents of Bond
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: Every bond executed under this Chapter shall specify the conditions, amount, duration and other particulars prescribed by law.
simpleExplanation: The bond must clearly mention its obligations and terms.
examples: The bond specifies two sureties and a one-year duration.

faqs.question: What must a bond contain?
faqs.answer: Terms, amount, duration and conditions.

title: Power to Reject Sureties
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: A Magistrate may refuse to accept a surety if the surety is unsuitable or does not satisfy legal requirements.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate can reject unreliable or ineligible sureties.
examples: A surety with insufficient means is rejected.
A person with a conflict of interest is not accepted.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate reject a surety?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the surety is not suitable.

title: Imprisonment in Default of Security
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: If a person ordered to give security does not furnish the required bond or sureties, the Magistrate may commit such person to prison for the period prescribed by law or until the security is furnished.
simpleExplanation: A person who fails to provide the required bond may be detained in prison.
punishment: Imprisonment until security is furnished or for the period specified by law.
examples: A person refuses to execute a peace bond.
Required sureties are not produced.

faqs.question: What happens if security is not furnished?
faqs.answer: The person may be imprisoned according to law.

title: Power to Release Persons Imprisoned for Failing to Give Security
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: The Chief Judicial Magistrate or other competent authority may release a person imprisoned for failure to furnish security if sufficient reasons exist.
simpleExplanation: A person jailed for not providing security may later be released.
examples: A person subsequently furnishes the required bond.
The authority finds continued detention unnecessary.

faqs.question: Can a person imprisoned for failure to furnish security be released?
faqs.answer: Yes, under conditions specified by law.

title: Security for Unexpired Period of Bond
chapter: Chapter IX - Security for Keeping the Peace and for Good Behaviour
officialText: When a person is released from imprisonment under this Chapter, security may still be required for the remaining unexpired portion of the original bond period.
simpleExplanation: Release from prison does not automatically remove the obligation to furnish security.
punishment: Execution of bond for the remaining period.
examples: A person released after six months must still furnish security for the remaining term.

faqs.question: Does release cancel the security requirement?
faqs.answer: No, security may still be required.

title: Commencement of Proceedings
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Proceedings under this Chapter may be initiated by the Executive Magistrate upon receiving information regarding unlawful obstruction, nuisance, danger, disturbance, or threats to public tranquillity.
simpleExplanation: This chapter begins procedures to address public nuisances and maintain order.
examples: A complaint is received regarding a dangerous obstruction on a public road.

faqs.question: Who can initiate proceedings under this chapter?
faqs.answer: An Executive Magistrate.

title: Conditional Order for Removal of Nuisance
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: An Executive Magistrate may issue a conditional order requiring a person to remove an unlawful obstruction, nuisance, dangerous structure, trade, or activity affecting public safety.
simpleExplanation: Authorities can order removal of public nuisances.
punishment: Failure to comply may lead to enforcement action.
examples: Removal of an illegal obstruction on a public road.
Closure of a hazardous operation affecting the public.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate order removal of a nuisance?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Service or Notification of Order
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The conditional order shall be served on the affected person or published in the prescribed manner when personal service is not practicable.
simpleExplanation: The affected person must be informed of the Magistrate's order.
examples: The order is personally served.
The order is publicly displayed.

faqs.question: How is a nuisance order communicated?
faqs.answer: Through service or public notification.

title: Person to Whom Order Is Addressed to Obey or Show Cause
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: A person against whom a conditional order is made must either comply with it or appear before the Magistrate and show cause against the order.
simpleExplanation: The person can obey the order or challenge it.
punishment: Failure may result in further proceedings.
examples: A shop owner removes an obstruction.
A person contests the order before the Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can a person challenge the Magistrate's order?
faqs.answer: Yes, by appearing and showing cause.

title: Consequence of Failure to Show Cause
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If the person fails to comply with the order or show sufficient cause, the order may be made absolute and enforced.
simpleExplanation: Ignoring the Magistrate's order can result in enforcement action.
examples: An obstruction is removed by authorities after non-compliance.

faqs.question: What happens if no cause is shown?
faqs.answer: The order may become final.

title: Procedure Where Existence of Public Right Is Denied
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Where a person denies the existence of a public right in respect of a way, river, channel or place, the Magistrate shall inquire into the claim and proceed according to law.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate examines disputes about public rights before making a final decision.
examples: A person claims a public pathway is private property.

faqs.question: What if someone disputes a public right?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate conducts an inquiry.

title: Procedure Where Person Against Whom Order Is Made Appears to Show Cause
chapter: Chapter X - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: When a person appears and contests the order, the Magistrate shall take evidence and conduct an inquiry before deciding whether the order should be confirmed, modified or cancelled.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate must hear evidence before making a final decision.
examples: Witnesses are examined regarding an alleged nuisance.

faqs.question: Will evidence be heard before a final order is passed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Protection against prosecution for acts done under sections 148, 149 and 150
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Provides protection from prosecution for Executive Magistrates, police officers, armed forces personnel and others acting in good faith under sections 148, 149 and 150. Prosecution requires prior sanction of the Central Government or State Government, as applicable.
simpleExplanation: Officials acting in good faith while dispersing unlawful assemblies are protected from legal action unless proper government sanction is obtained.
examples: A police officer disperses an unlawful assembly using lawful force.
An armed forces officer acts under orders to restore public order.

faqs.question: Can an officer be prosecuted for actions taken while dispersing a riot?
faqs.answer: Only with the required government sanction.

title: Conditional order for removal of nuisance
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Empowers a District Magistrate, Sub-Divisional Magistrate or specially empowered Executive Magistrate to issue a conditional order for removal of public nuisances, dangerous buildings, dangerous animals, unlawful obstructions, hazardous trades, unsafe excavations, or similar dangers affecting the public.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate can order a person to remove a public nuisance or appear and explain why the order should not become final.
punishment: Non-compliance may lead to proceedings under subsequent sections.
examples: Removal of a dangerous tree likely to fall.
Closure of a hazardous business affecting public health.

faqs.question: What is a public nuisance?
faqs.answer: An activity or condition that causes danger, obstruction, or inconvenience to the public.

title: Service or notification of order
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The order shall be served on the concerned person. If service is not practicable, it shall be notified through proclamation and publication in the manner prescribed by the State Government.
simpleExplanation: The person must be informed about the nuisance removal order.
examples: Order delivered personally.
Order published through public notification.

faqs.question: What if the person cannot be found?
faqs.answer: The order can be notified publicly.

title: Person to whom order is addressed to obey or show cause
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The person must either comply with the order within the specified time or appear before the Magistrate and show cause. Appearance may be through audio-video conferencing.
simpleExplanation: A person can obey the order or challenge it before the Magistrate.
punishment: Failure may attract consequences under section 155.
examples: A shop owner removes an obstruction.
A property owner contests the order.

faqs.question: Can appearance be through video conference?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Penalty for failure to comply with section 154
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If the person neither complies with the order nor appears to show cause, he becomes liable to punishment under section 223 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, and the order becomes absolute.
simpleExplanation: Ignoring the Magistrate's order makes it final and may result in punishment.
examples: Failure to remove a dangerous obstruction.
Ignoring a Magistrate's notice.

faqs.question: What happens if I ignore the order?
faqs.answer: The order becomes final and legal penalties may apply.

title: Procedure where existence of public right is denied
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Where a person denies the existence of a public right over a way, river, channel or place, the Magistrate shall inquire into the matter. If reliable evidence supports the denial, proceedings are stayed until a competent Court decides the issue.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate must verify disputes relating to public rights before proceeding.
examples: A person claims a public pathway is private property.

faqs.question: What if public ownership is disputed?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate conducts an inquiry and may stay proceedings.

title: Procedure where person against whom order is made under section 152 appears to show cause
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The Magistrate shall record evidence as in a summons case. If satisfied that the order is reasonable and proper, it shall be made absolute, with or without modification. Proceedings should ordinarily be completed within ninety days and may be extended up to one hundred and twenty days with recorded reasons.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate hears evidence before making a final decision.
examples: Evidence is examined regarding a nuisance complaint.

faqs.question: How long should the inquiry take?
faqs.answer: Normally within 90 days, extendable to 120 days.

title: Power of Magistrate to direct local investigation and examination of an expert
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: The Magistrate may order a local investigation or summon and examine an expert for the purposes of inquiry under sections 156 or 157.
simpleExplanation: Experts or investigators can be used to help determine facts.
examples: An engineer inspects a dangerous building.
A veterinary expert examines a dangerous animal.

faqs.question: Can experts be appointed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Power of Magistrate to furnish written instructions, etc.
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Where a local investigation is directed, the Magistrate may issue written instructions, determine who bears the expenses, and use the investigator's report as evidence. The Magistrate may also decide who pays the costs of expert examination.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate controls how investigations are conducted and who bears the costs.
examples: Costs of inspection are imposed on a party.
An expert report is admitted as evidence.

faqs.question: Can investigation expenses be recovered?
faqs.answer: Yes, as directed by the Magistrate.

title: Procedure on order being made absolute and consequences of disobedience
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: When an order becomes absolute, the Magistrate shall require compliance within a specified time. If the act is not performed, the Magistrate may cause it to be performed and recover the costs through sale or attachment of property. No suit shall lie for acts done in good faith under this section.
simpleExplanation: If a person still does not comply, authorities can carry out the work and recover the expenses.
punishment: Recovery of costs and liability under applicable law.
examples: Authorities demolish a dangerous structure and recover expenses.
A public obstruction is removed at the owner's cost.

faqs.question: Can the Government recover costs of removal?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Injunction Pending Inquiry
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If a Magistrate making an order under section 152 considers that immediate measures should be taken to prevent imminent danger or injury of a serious kind to the public, he may issue an injunction to the person against whom the order was made, as is required to obviate or prevent such danger or injury pending determination of the matter. In default of compliance, the Magistrate may use or cause to be used such means as he thinks fit to prevent the danger or injury. No suit shall lie for anything done in good faith under this section. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate can issue an immediate temporary order to prevent serious danger while the inquiry is still pending.
punishment: Non-compliance may lead to direct action by the Magistrate.
examples: A dangerous building is ordered to be vacated immediately.
A hazardous activity is temporarily stopped pending inquiry.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate take urgent action before the inquiry ends?
faqs.answer: Yes, through an injunction under this section.

title: Magistrate May Prohibit Repetition or Continuance of Public Nuisance
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: A District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or any other Executive Magistrate or Deputy Commissioner of Police empowered in this behalf, may order any person not to repeat or continue a public nuisance as defined in the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, or any special or local law. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
simpleExplanation: Authorities can order a person to stop continuing or repeating a public nuisance.
examples: Stopping repeated illegal dumping of waste.
Preventing recurring noise nuisance.

faqs.question: Can a nuisance be permanently prohibited?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Magistrate can prohibit its repetition or continuation.

title: Power to Issue Order in Urgent Cases of Nuisance or Apprehended Danger
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: In urgent cases where immediate prevention or speedy remedy is desirable, a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or specially empowered Executive Magistrate may issue written orders directing a person to abstain from certain acts or take specified action regarding property to prevent obstruction, annoyance, injury, danger to life, health or safety, disturbance of public tranquillity, riot or affray. Orders may be passed ex parte in emergencies and ordinarily remain in force for not more than two months, subject to extension by the State Government. :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}
simpleExplanation: This is the BNSS equivalent of Section 144 powers used to prevent urgent threats to public order.
punishment: Violation may attract penal consequences under applicable laws.
