examples: Imposition of prohibitory orders during communal tension.
Restrictions on public gatherings during emergencies.

faqs.question: Can orders be issued without hearing the affected person first?
faqs.answer: Yes, in emergencies through ex parte orders.

title: Procedure Where Dispute Concerning Land or Water Is Likely to Cause Breach of Peace
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever an Executive Magistrate is satisfied that a dispute likely to cause a breach of peace exists concerning any land, water or boundaries, he shall issue a written order requiring the parties to appear and submit written statements regarding actual possession. The Magistrate shall inquire into possession and may declare possession rights until determined by a competent Court. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate determines who is in possession of disputed property to prevent violence.
examples: Boundary disputes between neighbours.
Disputes regarding possession of agricultural land.

faqs.question: Does the Magistrate decide ownership?
faqs.answer: No, only possession for maintaining peace.

title: Power to Attach Subject of Dispute and Appoint Receiver
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: If the Magistrate considers the case to be one of emergency, or is unable to determine possession under section 164, he may attach the disputed property until a competent Court determines rights of the parties and may appoint a receiver to manage the property. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
simpleExplanation: In emergencies, the disputed property can be taken under official control until the Court decides the matter.
examples: Attachment of disputed agricultural land.
Appointment of a receiver for disputed property.

faqs.question: What is a receiver?
faqs.answer: A person appointed to manage disputed property.

title: Dispute Concerning Right of Use of Land or Water
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Where a dispute likely to cause breach of peace exists regarding any alleged right of use of land or water, the Executive Magistrate may conduct an inquiry and, if satisfied that such right exists, prohibit interference with the exercise of that right and order removal of obstructions. :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate can protect rights such as easements, access roads, or water usage.
examples: Dispute over access to a pathway.
Dispute regarding use of irrigation water.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate protect a right of way?
faqs.answer: Yes, under this section.

title: Local Inquiry
chapter: Chapter XI - Maintenance of Public Order and Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever a local inquiry is necessary for purposes of sections 164, 165 or 166, a District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may depute a subordinate Magistrate to conduct the inquiry and submit a report. The report may be read as evidence and costs may be apportioned among parties. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate may appoint another officer to conduct a local inspection and inquiry.
examples: Inspection of disputed land.
Local inquiry regarding a pathway dispute.

faqs.question: Can a subordinate Magistrate conduct the inquiry?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Police to Prevent Cognizable Offences
chapter: Chapter XII - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: Every police officer may interpose for the purpose of preventing, and shall to the best of his ability prevent, the commission of any cognizable offence. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}
simpleExplanation: Police officers have a duty to prevent cognizable offences before they occur.
examples: Police intervene to prevent a planned assault.
Police stop a robbery before it occurs.

faqs.question: Do police have preventive powers?
faqs.answer: Yes, they must act to prevent cognizable offences.

title: Information of Design to Commit Cognizable Offences
chapter: Chapter XII - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: Every police officer receiving information of a design to commit a cognizable offence shall communicate such information to his superior officer and to any other officer whose duty it is to prevent or take cognizance of such offence. :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}
simpleExplanation: Police must share intelligence about planned crimes with appropriate authorities.
examples: Information about a planned robbery is reported.
A tip-off regarding a violent offence is communicated.

faqs.question: What should a police officer do after receiving information about a planned crime?
faqs.answer: Immediately communicate it to the appropriate authorities.

title: Arrest to Prevent Commission of Cognizable Offences
chapter: Chapter XII - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: A police officer knowing of a design to commit any cognizable offence may arrest, without orders from a Magistrate and without a warrant, the person so designing if the offence cannot otherwise be prevented. A person so arrested shall not be detained for more than twenty-four hours unless further detention is authorised by law. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}
simpleExplanation: Police can make a preventive arrest to stop a cognizable offence from being committed.
punishment: Preventive detention up to 24 hours unless otherwise authorised.
examples: Police arrest a person preparing to commit a violent offence.
A planned armed robbery is prevented through arrest.

faqs.question: Can police arrest someone before a crime is committed?
faqs.answer: Yes, if necessary to prevent a cognizable offence.

title: Prevention of Injury to Public Property
chapter: Chapter XII - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: A police officer may of his own authority interpose to prevent any injury attempted to be committed in his view to any public property, movable or immovable, or the removal or injury of any public landmark, buoy or other mark used for navigation.
simpleExplanation: Police can immediately act to protect public property from damage.
examples: Stopping vandalism of a government building.
Preventing damage to a public monument.

faqs.question: Can a police officer stop damage to public property without a court order?
faqs.answer: Yes. A police officer may immediately intervene to prevent damage to public property.

faqs.question: What types of property are protected under this section?
faqs.answer: Public buildings, monuments, roads, navigation markers, and other public property.

title: Persons Bound to Conform to Lawful Directions of Police
chapter: Chapter XII - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: All persons shall be bound to conform to the lawful directions of a police officer given in fulfilment of duties under this Chapter. A police officer may detain or remove any person resisting or refusing to comply and may produce such person before a Magistrate or release him within twenty-four hours in petty cases.
simpleExplanation: People must obey lawful police directions relating to preventive policing.
examples: A crowd obeys police instructions during a public disturbance.
Police detain a person refusing lawful directions during preventive action.

faqs.question: Must citizens obey lawful directions of the police?
faqs.answer: Yes. All persons are legally bound to comply with lawful directions issued under this Chapter.

faqs.question: What happens if someone refuses to comply?
faqs.answer: The police may detain, remove, or produce the person before a Magistrate.

title: Information in Cognizable Cases
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Every information relating to the commission of a cognizable offence may be given orally or by electronic communication and shall be recorded in accordance with law. Special provisions exist for offences against women and persons with disabilities.
simpleExplanation: This is the FIR provision. Police must record information relating to cognizable offences.
examples: A victim reports a robbery at a police station.
An FIR is filed electronically for a cognizable offence.

faqs.question: What is an FIR?
faqs.answer: An FIR is the first information recorded by police regarding a cognizable offence.

faqs.question: Can an FIR be filed electronically?
faqs.answer: Yes. BNSS permits information relating to cognizable offences to be given electronically.

faqs.question: Are there special provisions for women victims?
faqs.answer: Yes. Special procedures exist for offences against women and persons with disabilities.

title: Information as to Non-Cognizable Cases and Investigation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Information regarding a non-cognizable offence shall be entered in the prescribed register and the informant referred to the Magistrate. Investigation requires Magistrate's order.
simpleExplanation: Police generally cannot investigate non-cognizable offences without permission from a Magistrate.
examples: A minor dispute is recorded and referred to a Magistrate.
Police seek Magistrate approval before investigating a non-cognizable offence.

faqs.question: Can police investigate a non-cognizable offence on their own?
faqs.answer: No. Police generally require permission from a Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens when a non-cognizable complaint is received?
faqs.answer: The information is entered in the prescribed register and the informant is referred to a Magistrate.

title: Police Officer's Power to Investigate Cognizable Case
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: An officer in charge of a police station may investigate any cognizable case without the order of a Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Police have independent authority to investigate cognizable offences.
examples: Police immediately investigate a theft complaint.
An SHO begins investigation without seeking court permission.

faqs.question: Do police need a Magistrate's order to investigate a cognizable offence?
faqs.answer: No. Police may investigate a cognizable offence without prior permission.

faqs.question: Who can investigate a cognizable case?
faqs.answer: The officer in charge of a police station or an authorized investigating officer.

title: Procedure for Investigation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Police receiving information of a cognizable offence shall send a report to the Magistrate and proceed to investigate. For offences punishable with seven years or more, forensic experts shall visit the crime scene and collect forensic evidence.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the investigation process and introduces mandatory forensic collection for serious offences.
examples: Police visit a murder scene and collect forensic samples.
A Magistrate receives an investigation report after registration of a serious offence.

faqs.question: When is forensic evidence collection mandatory?
faqs.answer: For offences punishable with imprisonment of seven years or more.

faqs.question: Why are forensic experts required at the crime scene?
faqs.answer: To scientifically collect, preserve and document evidence for investigation and trial.

faqs.question: Must police inform the Magistrate about the investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Police are required to send a report to the Magistrate and proceed with the investigation.

title: Report How Submitted
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Reports sent to a Magistrate may be routed through superior police officers appointed by the State Government.
simpleExplanation: Allows supervisory review and transmission of investigation reports through senior police officers.
examples: An investigation report is reviewed by a Superintendent of Police before being forwarded.
A senior officer transmits the report to the Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can investigation reports be reviewed by senior police officers?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reports may be routed through superior officers designated by the State Government.

faqs.question: Why are reports sent through superior officers?
faqs.answer: To ensure supervision, accountability and proper monitoring of investigations.

title: Power to Hold Investigation or Preliminary Inquiry
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Upon receiving a report, the Magistrate may direct investigation, hold a preliminary inquiry, or otherwise dispose of the case as provided in the Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate may supervise the matter, order further investigation, or conduct a preliminary inquiry where necessary.
examples: A Magistrate orders further investigation after reviewing a police report.
A preliminary inquiry is conducted before proceeding further.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate order further investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may direct further investigation if required.

faqs.question: What is a preliminary inquiry?
faqs.answer: It is an initial examination of facts to determine the appropriate course of action.

faqs.question: Does a Magistrate supervise police investigations?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate has oversight powers under this provision.

title: Police Officer's Power to Require Attendance of Witnesses
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: A police officer investigating a case may require attendance of persons acquainted with the facts. Women, children below fifteen years, senior citizens above sixty years, disabled persons and persons suffering from acute illness shall not be required to attend at any place other than their residence.
simpleExplanation: Police may require witnesses to assist the investigation, but certain vulnerable persons receive special protection.
examples: A witness is asked to appear before the investigating officer.
An elderly witness is examined at home instead of being called to the police station.

faqs.question: Can police require a witness to attend for questioning?
faqs.answer: Yes. Police may require attendance of persons acquainted with the facts of the case.

faqs.question: Are women and senior citizens required to attend the police station?
faqs.answer: No. Protected categories may be examined at their residence.

faqs.question: Who receives special protection under this section?
faqs.answer: Women, children below fifteen years, persons above sixty years, disabled persons and persons suffering from acute illness.

title: Examination of Witnesses by Police
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Police may orally examine persons acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case and may record their statements, including through audio-video electronic means.
simpleExplanation: Police can question witnesses and record their statements during the course of an investigation.
examples: A witness provides information about a theft incident.
A witness statement is recorded through video conferencing.

faqs.question: Can police record witness statements electronically?
faqs.answer: Yes. Statements may be recorded through audio-video electronic means.

faqs.question: Is a witness required to answer police questions during investigation?
faqs.answer: Generally, yes, except where the answer may expose the witness to criminal liability.

faqs.question: Can witness statements recorded by police be used directly as evidence at trial?
faqs.answer: Such statements are primarily used for investigation and may be used in accordance with evidentiary rules prescribed by law.

title: Making or Possessing Instruments or Materials for Forging or Counterfeiting Coin, Government Stamp, Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter XIII - Preventive Action of the Police
officialText: Whoever makes or mends, or performs any part of the process of making or mending, or buys or sells or disposes of, or has in his possession, any machinery, die, instrument or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for forging or counterfeiting any coin, stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, currency-note or bank-note, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
simpleExplanation: It is an offence to make, repair, buy, sell, possess, or deal in machinery, tools, dies, or materials intended for producing counterfeit coins, Government revenue stamps, currency notes, or bank notes.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A person manufactures printing plates specifically designed for producing fake currency notes.
An individual possesses specialised dies intended to create counterfeit coins.
A workshop repairs machinery knowing that it will be used for producing fake bank notes.

faqs.question: Is actual counterfeiting required for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. Possessing, making, repairing, selling, or dealing in equipment intended for counterfeiting is sufficient.

faqs.question: What items are covered under this section?
faqs.answer: Machinery, dies, instruments, and materials intended for forging or counterfeiting coins, Government stamps, currency notes, or bank notes.

faqs.question: Does knowledge of the intended use matter?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or have reason to believe that the equipment or material is intended for counterfeiting.

title: No Inducement to be Offered
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: No police officer or person in authority shall offer any inducement, threat or promise to obtain a statement. However, no police officer shall prevent a person from making a voluntary statement of his own free will during investigation.
simpleExplanation: Police cannot force, threaten, bribe, or promise benefits to obtain statements. At the same time, they cannot stop someone from voluntarily giving information.
punishment: No specific punishment prescribed under this section.
examples: A police officer cannot promise release from custody in exchange for a confession.
A witness may voluntarily provide information without being pressured.

faqs.question: Can police threaten someone to obtain a statement?
faqs.answer: No. Threats, inducements, and promises are prohibited.

faqs.question: Can a person voluntarily provide information?
faqs.answer: Yes. Voluntary statements are permitted.

title: Recording of Confessions and Statements
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Any Magistrate may record any confession or statement made during investigation. Before recording a confession, the Magistrate must explain that the person is not bound to confess and that the confession may be used as evidence against him. The Magistrate must ensure that the confession is voluntary. Confessions may also be recorded through audio-video electronic means in the presence of the accused's advocate.
simpleExplanation: Confessions are valid only when recorded voluntarily before a Magistrate after proper safeguards are explained to the accused.
punishment: No punishment prescribed; procedural provision governing admissibility of confessions.
examples: An accused voluntarily confesses before a Magistrate after being informed of his rights.
A rape victim's statement is recorded by a Magistrate shortly after the offence is reported.

faqs.question: Can police record a confession?
faqs.answer: No. A confession must generally be recorded by a Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can confessions be video recorded?
faqs.answer: Yes. Audio-video recording is permitted.

title: Medical Examination of Victim of Rape
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Where rape or attempted rape is under investigation, the victim shall be medically examined by a registered medical practitioner with her consent or the consent of a competent person on her behalf. The examination must be conducted within twenty-four hours of receiving information about the offence and the medical report must be forwarded within seven days.
simpleExplanation: The law requires prompt medical examination of rape victims to preserve evidence and assist investigation.
punishment: No punishment prescribed; procedural safeguard for investigation.
examples: A rape survivor is examined by a government doctor within 24 hours of reporting the offence.
DNA samples are collected and documented in the medical report.

faqs.question: Is consent required for examination?
faqs.answer: Yes. Examination cannot be conducted without valid consent.

faqs.question: What information is included in the report?
faqs.answer: Injuries, DNA samples, age, mental condition, and other relevant medical findings.

title: Search by Police Officer
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: A police officer investigating an offence may search any place within his jurisdiction if he has reasonable grounds to believe that evidence necessary for investigation is located there and cannot otherwise be obtained without undue delay. Grounds must be recorded in writing and searches should be videographed wherever practicable.
simpleExplanation: Police may conduct searches without a warrant in urgent situations during an investigation, subject to legal safeguards.
examples: Police search a suspect's house to recover stolen property.
Investigators seize documents relevant to a fraud case.

faqs.question: Can police search without a warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes, under specified circumstances during investigation.

title: When Officer in Charge of Police Station May Require Another to Issue Search-Warrant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: An officer in charge of a police station or a police officer not below the rank of Sub-Inspector making an investigation may require the officer in charge of another police station to conduct a search. If delay may result in evidence being concealed or destroyed, the investigating officer may directly conduct the search in another police station's jurisdiction and must notify the local police station and Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Police officers can request another police station to conduct a search in its jurisdiction. In urgent situations, they may directly search outside their jurisdiction to prevent loss of evidence.
punishment: No punishment prescribed; procedural provision relating to investigation.
examples: A Delhi police officer requests Mumbai police to search a suspect's residence located in Mumbai.
An officer directly conducts a search in a neighboring district when delay could result in destruction of evidence.

faqs.question: Can police search outside their jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes. They may either request the local police station or conduct the search directly in urgent situations.

faqs.question: Must the local police station be informed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Notice of the search must be sent to the officer in charge of the local police station.

title: Procedure When Investigation Cannot Be Completed in Twenty-Four Hours
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When an investigation cannot be completed within twenty-four hours and there are sufficient grounds for believing the accusation is well-founded, the accused shall be forwarded to a Magistrate. The Magistrate may authorize detention subject to statutory limits, including a maximum of ninety days for offences punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment of ten years or more, and sixty days for other offences.
simpleExplanation: If police need more than twenty-four hours to investigate, they must produce the accused before a Magistrate who decides whether continued custody is justified.
punishment: No punishment prescribed; procedural provision governing remand and custody.
examples: A murder investigation requires further evidence collection, so the accused is produced before a Magistrate for remand.
An accused is released on default bail after expiry of the statutory investigation period.

faqs.question: What is default bail?
faqs.answer: If the investigation is not completed within the prescribed period and the accused is willing to furnish bail, release on bail becomes a statutory right.

faqs.question: Can police keep an accused beyond twenty-four hours without a Magistrate's approval?
faqs.answer: No. Further detention requires authorization by a Magistrate.

title: Report of Investigation by Subordinate Police Officer
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When any subordinate police officer has made an investigation under this Chapter, he shall report the result of such investigation to the officer in charge of the police station.
simpleExplanation: A subordinate police officer who conducts an investigation must submit the findings to the officer in charge of the police station.
examples: A Sub-Inspector completes a field investigation and submits a report to the Station House Officer.
An investigating officer forwards witness statements and evidence to the officer in charge.

faqs.question: Who receives the report of a subordinate officer?
faqs.answer: The officer in charge of the police station receives the report.

title: Release of Accused When Evidence Deficient
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: If upon investigation it appears that there is insufficient evidence or reasonable ground of suspicion to justify forwarding the accused to a Magistrate, the officer in charge of the police station shall release the accused on executing a bond or bail bond to appear before a Magistrate if required.
simpleExplanation: If the investigation does not reveal enough evidence, the accused should be released instead of being unnecessarily sent for trial.
examples: A suspect is released after investigation reveals no evidence linking him to the offence.
An accused executes a bond promising to appear before the Magistrate if required later.

faqs.question: Can police release an accused during investigation?
faqs.answer: Yes, if there is insufficient evidence or reasonable suspicion.

faqs.question: Is the accused completely discharged?
faqs.answer: No. The accused may still be required to appear before a Magistrate if further proceedings arise.

title: Cases to be Sent to Magistrate When Evidence is Sufficient
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: If upon investigation there is sufficient evidence or reasonable ground to proceed, the officer in charge of the police station shall forward the accused to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence. In bailable offences, security may be taken for the accused's appearance before the Magistrate. Relevant articles, documents, complainants, and witnesses may also be forwarded or required to appear before the Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: When police find enough evidence against an accused, they must send the case to the Magistrate for further judicial proceedings.
punishment: No punishment prescribed; procedural provision governing commencement of prosecution.
examples: Police complete an investigation and submit the accused along with evidence before the Magistrate.
In a bailable offence, the accused furnishes security and appears before the Magistrate on the scheduled date.

faqs.question: What happens when sufficient evidence is found?
faqs.answer: The accused is forwarded to the Magistrate and the case proceeds to the next stage of the criminal process.

faqs.question: Can a person avoid custody in a bailable offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The police may take security for appearance before the Magistrate instead of forwarding the accused in custody.

title: Complainant and Witnesses Not to be Required to Accompany Police Officer and Not to be Subject to Restraint
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: No complainant or witness on his way to any Court shall be required to accompany a police officer, or be subjected to unnecessary restraint, inconvenience, or required to furnish security other than his own bond. However, if a complainant or witness refuses to attend or execute a bond as directed under section 190, the officer in charge of the police station may forward him in custody to the Magistrate, who may detain him until he executes such bond or until the hearing is completed.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses and complainants should not be unnecessarily detained or inconvenienced by police. However, they may be compelled to comply with lawful requirements to appear before court.
examples: A witness cannot be forced to travel with a police officer to court without justification.
A complainant refusing to execute a required bond may be produced before a Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can police detain a witness unnecessarily?
faqs.answer: No. Witnesses and complainants are protected from unnecessary restraint and inconvenience.

faqs.question: What happens if a witness refuses to execute a bond?
faqs.answer: The witness may be produced before a Magistrate.

title: Diary of Proceedings in Investigation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Every police officer making an investigation shall maintain a day-to-day diary recording the progress of investigation, including the time information was received, investigation commenced and concluded, places visited, and circumstances discovered. Statements recorded under section 180 shall be inserted in the case diary. Criminal Courts may call for such diaries to aid inquiry or trial, but they are not evidence in themselves.
simpleExplanation: Police officers must maintain a detailed investigation diary documenting all important steps taken during an investigation.
examples: An investigating officer records witness examinations and site visits in the case diary.
A court reviews the diary to understand the investigation process.

faqs.question: Can the accused inspect the police diary?
faqs.answer: Generally no, except in limited circumstances provided by law.

faqs.question: Is the case diary evidence?
faqs.answer: No. It is used to assist the court but is not substantive evidence.

title: Report of Police Officer on Completion of Investigation
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: Every investigation shall be completed without unnecessary delay. Upon completion, the officer in charge of the police station shall submit a report to the Magistrate containing details of the parties, offence, accused, witnesses, custody status, medical reports where applicable, and other prescribed particulars. Further investigation may be conducted even after submission of the report with permission of the Court during trial.
simpleExplanation: After completing an investigation, police must submit a final report (charge sheet or closure report) to the Magistrate.
examples: Police file a charge sheet after gathering sufficient evidence.
A supplementary report is filed after discovering additional evidence.

faqs.question: What is a police report under this section?
faqs.answer: It is the final investigation report submitted to the Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can investigation continue after filing the report?
faqs.answer: Yes. Further investigation is permitted under the law.

title: Police to Enquire and Report on Suicide, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: When a person commits suicide, dies under suspicious circumstances, is killed by another person, animal, machinery, or accident, the police officer shall inform the nearest Executive Magistrate and investigate the apparent cause of death. A report describing injuries and circumstances of death shall be prepared and forwarded to the Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Police must investigate unnatural or suspicious deaths and prepare an inquest report.
examples: Police investigate a suspicious death found in a residence.
An inquest report is prepared after a road accident fatality.

faqs.question: What is an inquest?
faqs.answer: An inquiry into the apparent cause of death.

faqs.question: Who receives the report?
faqs.answer: The District Magistrate or Sub-Divisional Magistrate.

title: Power to Summon Persons
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: A police officer conducting an inquiry under section 194 may summon persons acquainted with the facts of the case. Such persons are bound to attend and answer questions truthfully, except those answers that may expose them to criminal liability or penalties.
simpleExplanation: Police may call witnesses during an inquest and require them to provide truthful information.
examples: Neighbors are summoned to provide information regarding a suspicious death.
Family members are questioned about the circumstances leading to the death.

faqs.question: Must summoned persons answer all questions?
faqs.answer: They must answer truthfully except where answers may incriminate them.

faqs.question: Can elderly persons be compelled to travel?
faqs.answer: Special protections apply to women, elderly persons, and disabled individuals.

title: Inquiry by Magistrate into Cause of Death
chapter: Chapter XIII - Information to the Police and Their Powers to Investigate
officialText: In specified cases including custodial deaths, custodial disappearances, custodial rape, and suspicious deaths of women within seven years of marriage, a Magistrate shall conduct an inquiry into the cause of death in addition to any police investigation.
simpleExplanation: Certain deaths require an independent inquiry by a Magistrate to ensure transparency and accountability.
examples: A Magistrate conducts an inquiry into a custodial death.
A woman dies within seven years of marriage under suspicious circumstances and a magisterial inquiry is ordered.

faqs.question: When is a magisterial inquiry mandatory?
faqs.answer: In cases such as custodial deaths, custodial rape, and certain suspicious deaths.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate order exhumation of a body?
faqs.answer: Yes, when necessary to determine the cause of death.

title: Ordinary Place of Inquiry and Trial
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Every offence shall ordinarily be inquired into and tried by a Court within whose local jurisdiction it was committed.
simpleExplanation: A criminal case is normally heard by the court located where the offence occurred.
examples: A theft committed in Delhi is generally tried by a court in Delhi.

faqs.question: Which court normally hears a criminal case?
faqs.answer: The court having jurisdiction over the place where the offence occurred.

title: Place of Inquiry or Trial
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where it is uncertain where an offence was committed, where it was committed in multiple locations, where it is a continuing offence, or where it consists of acts done in different locations, it may be tried by a court having jurisdiction over any of those locations.
simpleExplanation: Some offences can be tried in more than one court depending on where parts of the offence occurred.
examples: An online fraud affecting multiple states may be tried in any competent jurisdiction connected with the offence.

faqs.question: Can multiple courts have jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on where different parts of the offence occurred.

title: Offence Triable Where Act Done or Consequence Ensued
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an act is an offence because of something done and a consequence that followed, the offence may be inquired into or tried by a court within whose jurisdiction either the act was done or the consequence occurred.
simpleExplanation: A case may be tried either where the act happened or where its harmful result occurred.
examples: A defamatory message sent from one city but received in another may be tried in either place.

faqs.question: Can a case be tried where the consequence occurred?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jurisdiction may exist at the place where the consequence ensued.

title: Place of Trial Where Act is Connected With Another Offence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an act is an offence because of its relation to another act which is also an offence, the first-mentioned offence may be inquired into or tried by a court within whose jurisdiction either act was done.
simpleExplanation: If two connected acts together create criminal liability, the case may be tried where either act occurred.
examples: A conspiracy formed in one city and executed in another may be tried in either jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Can related offences be tried in the same jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes. A court having jurisdiction over either connected act may try the offence.

title: Place of Trial in Case of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Offences such as dacoity, dacoity with murder, belonging to a gang of dacoits, escaping from custody, kidnapping, abduction, theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, criminal breach of trust, and possession of stolen property may be inquired into or tried by courts having jurisdiction over specified places connected with the offence, including where the offence occurred, where the accused is found, where the victim was detained, or where stolen property was possessed, received, retained, or required to be returned.
simpleExplanation: Certain offences can be tried in more than one place depending on where the crime occurred, where the accused was found, or where related property was located.
examples: A kidnapping case may be tried where the victim was abducted or where the victim was detained.
A theft case may be tried where the stolen property was recovered.

faqs.question: Can a kidnapping case be tried where the victim was kept?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court where the victim was concealed or detained has jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Can a theft case be tried where stolen goods were found?
faqs.answer: Yes. Jurisdiction extends to places where stolen property was possessed or recovered.

title: Offences Committed by Means of Electronic Communications, Letters, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Offences involving cheating through electronic communications, letters, or telecommunication messages may be tried where the communication was sent or received. Offences involving cheating and delivery of property may be tried where the property was delivered or received. Certain matrimonial offences may also be tried where the offender last resided with the spouse or where the aggrieved spouse resides.
simpleExplanation: Cyber cheating and communication-based offences can be tried where messages were sent, received, or where property changed hands.
examples: An online fraud can be tried where the victim received the fraudulent message.
A cheating case may be tried where the victim transferred money.

faqs.question: Can cyber fraud be tried where the victim received the message?
faqs.answer: Yes. The court where the communication was received has jurisdiction.

title: Offence Committed on Journey or Voyage
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When an offence is committed during a journey or voyage, it may be inquired into or tried by a court through or into whose local jurisdiction the person or thing involved in the offence passed during that journey or voyage.
simpleExplanation: Crimes committed while travelling may be tried in any area through which the journey passed.
examples: A theft committed on a train may be tried in any district through which the train travelled.
An offence committed during a sea voyage may be tried at a port connected to the journey.

faqs.question: Can an offence on a train be tried in multiple places?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any court along the route may have jurisdiction.

title: Place of Trial for Offences Triable Together
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where multiple offences committed by one or more persons may be charged and tried together under the provisions of this Sanhita, the offences may be inquired into or tried by any court competent to inquire into or try any of those offences.
simpleExplanation: When several offences are legally tried together, a single competent court may hear all of them.
examples: A conspiracy involving multiple offences may be tried in one court.
Several accused charged jointly may face trial in a single jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Can related offences be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law permits joint trials in specified circumstances.

title: Power to Order Cases to be Tried in Different Sessions Divisions
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The State Government may direct that any case or class of cases committed for trial in one district be tried in another sessions division, provided such direction is not inconsistent with any order of the High Court or Supreme Court.
simpleExplanation: The State Government can shift certain cases to another sessions division when permitted by law.
examples: A sensitive case may be transferred to another sessions division for administrative reasons.

faqs.question: Can the State Government transfer a case?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to legal restrictions and court directions.

title: High Court to Decide, in Case of Doubt, District Where Inquiry or Trial Shall Take Place
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When two or more courts have taken cognizance of the same offence and a dispute arises regarding which court should inquire into or try the offence, the matter shall be decided by the appropriate High Court, after which all other proceedings shall be discontinued.
simpleExplanation: The High Court decides which court should hear a case when multiple courts claim jurisdiction.
examples: Two courts in different districts begin proceedings relating to the same offence.

faqs.question: Who resolves jurisdiction disputes?
faqs.answer: The High Court resolves such disputes.

title: Power to Issue Summons or Warrant for Offence Committed Beyond Local Jurisdiction
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A Magistrate may inquire into an offence committed outside his jurisdiction if the offence is triable in India and may compel the accused to appear before him and send the accused to the competent Magistrate having jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate can assist in securing the appearance of a person accused of an offence committed outside the Magistrate's local jurisdiction.
examples: A person located in one state is produced before a Magistrate for an offence committed in another state.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate act even if the offence occurred elsewhere?
faqs.answer: Yes, where the offence is triable in India.

title: Offence Committed Outside India
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: An offence committed outside India by an Indian citizen or by any person on a ship or aircraft registered in India may be dealt with as if it had been committed in India. Such prosecution requires previous sanction of the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: India can prosecute certain offences committed abroad by Indian citizens or on Indian ships and aircraft.
examples: An Indian citizen commits a crime abroad and is later found in India.
An offence occurs on an aircraft registered in India.

faqs.question: Is Central Government approval required?
faqs.answer: Yes. Previous sanction of the Central Government is necessary.

title: Receipt of Evidence Relating to Offences Committed Outside India
chapter: Chapter XIV - Jurisdiction of the Criminal Courts in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In trials relating to offences committed outside India, the Central Government may direct that depositions and exhibits produced before foreign judicial authorities or Indian diplomatic or consular representatives be received as evidence by the court conducting the inquiry or trial.
simpleExplanation: Evidence collected abroad may be used in Indian courts when permitted by the Central Government.
examples: Witness statements recorded abroad are admitted in an Indian trial.

faqs.question: Can foreign evidence be used in Indian courts?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to directions of the Central Government.

title: Cognizance of Offences by Magistrate
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Subject to this Chapter, a Magistrate of the first class, and a Magistrate of the second class specially empowered, may take cognizance of an offence upon receiving a complaint, upon a police report, upon information received from any person other than a police officer, or upon his own knowledge that an offence has been committed.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how a Magistrate can formally begin criminal proceedings by taking cognizance of an offence.
examples: A Magistrate takes cognizance based on a police charge sheet.
A private complaint is filed directly before a Magistrate.

faqs.question: What does taking cognizance mean?
faqs.answer: It means the Magistrate formally recognizes and begins judicial proceedings regarding an alleged offence.

title: Transfer on Application of Accused
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: When a Magistrate takes cognizance of an offence under clause (c) of sub-section (1) of section 210, the accused shall, before any evidence is taken, be informed that he is entitled to have the case inquired into or tried by another Magistrate. If the accused objects, the case shall be transferred to another Magistrate specified by the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: When a Magistrate starts a case based on personal knowledge or information received from someone other than the police, the accused has the right to request that another Magistrate hear the case.
examples: A Magistrate takes cognizance based on personal knowledge of an offence and informs the accused of the right to seek transfer.
An accused requests transfer and the case is assigned to another Magistrate.

faqs.question: Does the accused have a right to another Magistrate?
faqs.answer: Yes, in cases covered under section 210(1)(c).

title: Making Over of Cases to Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: The Chief Judicial Magistrate or an empowered Magistrate may, after taking cognizance of an offence, make over the case for inquiry or trial to another competent Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Senior Magistrates may assign cases to other competent Magistrates for inquiry or trial.
examples: A Chief Judicial Magistrate assigns a case to another Magistrate for trial.

faqs.question: Who can transfer a case to another Magistrate?
faqs.answer: The Chief Judicial Magistrate or an empowered Magistrate.

title: Cognizance of Offences by Court of Session
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Except as otherwise expressly provided, no Court of Session shall take cognizance of any offence as a Court of original jurisdiction unless the case has been committed to it by a Magistrate under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: A Sessions Court normally cannot directly start a criminal case. The case must first be committed by a Magistrate.
examples: A murder case is first committed by a Magistrate before being tried by a Sessions Court.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Court directly take cognizance?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless specifically permitted by law.

title: Additional Sessions Judges to Try Cases Made Over to Them
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: An Additional Sessions Judge shall try such cases as the Sessions Judge or the High Court may assign to him.
simpleExplanation: Additional Sessions Judges hear cases allocated to them by the Sessions Judge or High Court.
examples: A Sessions Judge assigns a robbery trial to an Additional Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: Who assigns cases to an Additional Sessions Judge?
faqs.answer: The Sessions Judge or the High Court.

title: Prosecution for Contempt of Lawful Authority of Public Servants and Certain Offences Relating to Documents and Public Justice
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Certain offences relating to contempt of lawful authority of public servants, offences against public justice, and offences relating to documents produced in court can be prosecuted only upon a written complaint by the concerned public servant or court.
simpleExplanation: For certain offences affecting courts and public authorities, criminal proceedings can begin only after a formal complaint by the appropriate authority.
examples: A false document submitted in court may require a complaint from the court before prosecution.

faqs.question: Can anyone file a complaint for these offences?
faqs.answer: No. Only authorized public servants or courts may do so.

title: Procedure for Witnesses in Case of Threatening, etc.
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: A witness or any other person may file a complaint in relation to an offence under section 232 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses who are threatened or unlawfully influenced may directly initiate legal proceedings.
examples: A witness reports intimidation during a criminal trial.

faqs.question: Can a witness file a complaint directly?
faqs.answer: Yes, under this provision.

title: Prosecution for Offences Against State and Criminal Conspiracy
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Certain offences against the State and criminal conspiracies require previous sanction of the Central Government, State Government, or District Magistrate before a court may take cognizance.
simpleExplanation: For serious offences affecting the State, prosecution cannot begin without prior government approval.
examples: A prosecution for sedition-related offences requires prior sanction.

faqs.question: Why is sanction required?
faqs.answer: To ensure careful scrutiny before prosecution of sensitive offences.

title: Prosecution of Judges and Public Servants
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No court shall take cognizance of offences alleged to have been committed by Judges, Magistrates, certain public servants, or members of the armed forces while acting in discharge of official duties without prior sanction from the competent Government authority.
simpleExplanation: Government approval is generally required before prosecuting public officials for acts connected with official duties.
examples: A public servant accused of an act performed during official duty requires sanction before prosecution.

faqs.question: Is sanction always required?
faqs.answer: No. Certain specified offences are exempt from the sanction requirement.

title: Prosecution for Offences Against Marriage
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: Offences relating to marriage may generally be prosecuted only upon a complaint by the aggrieved person or persons authorized by law to act on their behalf.
simpleExplanation: Marriage-related offences usually require a complaint from the affected spouse or authorized relative.
examples: A spouse files a complaint regarding a marriage-related offence.

faqs.question: Can relatives file a complaint?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specified circumstances provided by law.

title: Prosecution of Offences Under Section 85 of Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No court shall take cognizance of an offence punishable under section 85 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 except upon a police report or complaint by the aggrieved person or specified relatives.
simpleExplanation: Proceedings under this offence can begin only through a police report or complaint by authorized persons.
examples: An aggrieved woman files a complaint under the relevant provision.

faqs.question: Who may file the complaint?
faqs.answer: The aggrieved person or specified relatives.

title: Cognizance of Offence
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No court shall take cognizance of an offence punishable under section 67 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 where the parties are in a marital relationship except upon a complaint made by the wife against the husband and upon prima facie satisfaction of the facts.
simpleExplanation: For this specific offence within a marital relationship, proceedings can begin only on the wife's complaint.
examples: A wife files a complaint alleging commission of the offence by her husband.

faqs.question: Can police independently initiate proceedings?
faqs.answer: The court requires a complaint by the wife and prima facie satisfaction.

title: Prosecution for Defamation
chapter: Chapter XV - Conditions Requisite for Initiation of Proceedings
officialText: No court shall take cognizance of an offence of defamation except upon a complaint by the person aggrieved. Special provisions apply where the alleged victim is the President, Vice-President, Governor, Minister, or other public servant acting in official capacity.
simpleExplanation: Defamation cases generally require a complaint by the affected person, with special procedures for high constitutional authorities and public servants.
examples: A private citizen files a defamation complaint against another individual.
A Public Prosecutor files a complaint on behalf of a Governor with prior sanction.

faqs.question: Can anyone file a defamation case?
faqs.answer: Generally only the aggrieved person or a legally authorized person may do so.

title: Examination of Complainant
chapter: Chapter XVI - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: A Magistrate taking cognizance upon a complaint shall examine the complainant and witnesses on oath and record their statements, subject to specified exceptions. In complaints against public servants, the Magistrate must provide an opportunity to explain the circumstances and obtain a report from a superior officer.
simpleExplanation: Before proceeding with a private complaint, the Magistrate usually records the statements of the complainant and witnesses under oath.
examples: A complainant appears before a Magistrate and gives a sworn statement.

faqs.question: Must every complainant be examined?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, subject to exceptions provided in the law.

title: Procedure by Magistrate Not Competent to Take Cognizance of Case
chapter: Chapter XVI - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: If a complaint is made to a Magistrate who is not competent to take cognizance, he shall return the complaint for presentation before the proper court or direct the complainant to the proper court.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate lacking jurisdiction must send the complaint to the appropriate court.
examples: A complaint filed in the wrong court is returned for filing before the correct Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens if a complaint is filed in the wrong court?
faqs.answer: The complainant is directed to the proper court.

title: Postponement of Issue of Process
chapter: Chapter XVI - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: A Magistrate may postpone issuing process against an accused and either inquire into the case himself or direct an investigation to determine whether sufficient grounds exist for proceeding. Such postponement is mandatory when the accused resides outside the Magistrate's jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: Before summoning an accused, the Magistrate may conduct a preliminary inquiry or order an investigation to determine whether the complaint has merit.
examples: A Magistrate orders a police inquiry before issuing summons to an accused living in another state.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate investigate before issuing summons?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may conduct an inquiry or order an investigation.

title: Dismissal of Complaint
chapter: Chapter XVI - Complaints to Magistrates
officialText: If, after considering the statements of the complainant and witnesses and the result of any inquiry or investigation under section 225, the Magistrate finds no sufficient ground for proceeding, he shall dismiss the complaint and briefly record the reasons.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate can dismiss a complaint if there is not enough basis to continue the case.
examples: A complaint lacking supporting evidence is dismissed after inquiry.

faqs.question: Must reasons be recorded?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Magistrate must record reasons for dismissal.

title: Issue of Process
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: If sufficient grounds exist, the Magistrate shall issue summons in summons-cases and may issue summons or warrants in warrant-cases. Process may also be issued electronically.
simpleExplanation: Once the Magistrate is satisfied that a case should proceed, summons or warrants are issued.
examples: A summons is issued to an accused in a minor criminal case.

faqs.question: Can summons be issued electronically?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Magistrate May Dispense with Personal Attendance of Accused
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: A Magistrate may permit an accused to appear through an advocate instead of appearing personally, but may later require personal attendance.
simpleExplanation: For convenience, the accused may be allowed to appear through a lawyer.
examples: An accused living in another city is permitted to appear through counsel.

faqs.question: Can personal appearance later be required?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Special Summons in Cases of Petty Offence
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: In petty offences, the Magistrate may allow the accused to plead guilty and pay the specified fine without appearing in person.
simpleExplanation: Minor cases can be resolved without requiring court attendance.
punishment: Fine up to ₹5,000 in eligible petty offences.
examples: An accused pays the fine by post and pleads guilty.

faqs.question: What is a petty offence?
faqs.answer: An offence punishable only with fine up to ₹5,000.

title: Supply to Accused of Copy of Police Report and Other Documents
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: The Magistrate shall furnish to the accused and victim copies of the police report, FIR, witness statements, confessions, and relevant documents, free of cost.
simpleExplanation: The accused has a right to receive important case documents.
examples: The accused receives the charge sheet and witness statements.

faqs.question: Can documents be supplied electronically?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Supply of Copies in Cases Triable by Court of Session
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: In cases triable exclusively by the Court of Session, copies of statements, confessions, and relied-upon documents shall be furnished to the accused.
simpleExplanation: The accused must receive the evidence and records before the case is committed to the Sessions Court.
examples: Copies of witness statements are supplied before committal.

faqs.question: Can voluminous documents be inspected instead of copied?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Commitment of Case to Court of Session
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: Where an offence is triable exclusively by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall commit the case, send records and evidence, and notify the Public Prosecutor.
simpleExplanation: Serious offences must be transferred to the Sessions Court for trial.
examples: A murder case is committed to the Sessions Court.

faqs.question: Within what period should committal generally be completed?
faqs.answer: Within ninety days, extendable for recorded reasons.

title: Procedure When There is a Complaint Case and Police Investigation in Respect of Same Offence
chapter: Chapter XVII - Commencement of Proceedings Before Magistrates
officialText: Where a complaint case and a police investigation concern the same offence, the Magistrate shall stay the complaint proceedings and seek a police report.
simpleExplanation: The law prevents parallel proceedings from conflicting with each other.
examples: A private complaint is stayed pending completion of police investigation.

faqs.question: Can both matters be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes, if cognizance is taken on the police report.

title: Contents of Charge
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Every charge shall state the offence, relevant legal provisions, and any particulars necessary to inform the accused of the allegation.
simpleExplanation: A criminal charge must clearly explain what offence the accused is alleged to have committed.
examples: A murder charge specifies the relevant BNS section.

faqs.question: Must the charge mention the law violated?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Particulars as to Time, Place and Person
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: The charge shall contain sufficient particulars regarding the time, place, person, or property involved in the offence.
simpleExplanation: The accused must know when, where, and against whom the alleged offence was committed.
examples: A theft charge specifies the date, place, and stolen property.

faqs.question: Why are particulars necessary?
faqs.answer: To give the accused fair notice of the allegation.

title: When Manner of Committing Offence Must be Stated
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Where the basic particulars are insufficient, the charge must also state the manner in which the offence was committed.
simpleExplanation: Additional details may be required to help the accused understand the allegation.
examples: A cheating charge explains how the deception occurred.

faqs.question: Is the manner always required?
faqs.answer: Only when necessary to provide adequate notice.

title: Words in Charge Taken in Sense of Law Under Which Offence is Punishable
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Words used in a charge shall be understood in the sense assigned to them by the law creating the offence.
simpleExplanation: Legal terms in a charge carry their statutory meanings.
examples: The term 'theft' is interpreted according to its legal definition.

faqs.question: How are words in a charge interpreted?
faqs.answer: According to the law defining the offence.

title: Effect of Errors
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Errors or omissions in a charge are not material unless they mislead the accused and cause a failure of justice.
simpleExplanation: Minor mistakes in a charge do not invalidate proceedings unless they prejudice the accused.
examples: A minor error in the victim's name may not invalidate the trial.

faqs.question: When does an error become important?
faqs.answer: When it misleads the accused and causes injustice.

title: Court May Alter Charge
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: A Court may alter or add to any charge at any time before judgment, subject to safeguards ensuring fairness to the accused and prosecution.
simpleExplanation: Courts can correct or modify charges if necessary before delivering judgment.
examples: A charge is amended when additional facts emerge during trial.

faqs.question: Can charges be changed during trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, before judgment.

title: Recall of Witnesses When Charge Altered
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: When a charge is altered or added, both prosecution and accused may recall or re-examine witnesses relating to the alteration.
simpleExplanation: Parties are given a chance to address the modified charge through additional evidence.
examples: A witness is recalled after a new charge is added.

faqs.question: Can witnesses be recalled?
faqs.answer: Yes, when the charge changes.

title: Separate Charges for Distinct Offences
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Every distinct offence shall ordinarily be charged and tried separately unless otherwise permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: Different offences generally require separate charges and separate trials.
examples: A theft and an unrelated assault are charged separately.

faqs.question: Can multiple offences be tried together?
faqs.answer: Only in circumstances allowed by law.

title: Offences of Same Kind Within Year May Be Charged Together
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Up to five offences of the same kind committed within twelve months may be charged and tried together.
simpleExplanation: Similar offences committed within a year may be combined in one trial.
examples: Five thefts committed within a year are tried together.

faqs.question: How many offences can be combined?
faqs.answer: Up to five offences of the same kind.

title: Trial for More Than One Offence
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Multiple offences arising from the same transaction may be charged and tried together.
simpleExplanation: Connected offences can be handled in a single trial for efficiency and consistency.
examples: House-breaking and rape committed during the same incident are tried together.

faqs.question: What is a same transaction?
faqs.answer: Acts so connected that they form part of one occurrence.

title: Where It Is Doubtful What Offence Has Been Committed
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Where it is uncertain which offence is made out by the facts, the accused may be charged with multiple alternative offences and convicted of the offence proved.
simpleExplanation: Alternative charges may be framed when the exact offence is unclear.
examples: An act may amount to theft, cheating, or criminal breach of trust.

faqs.question: Can a person be convicted of an offence not specifically charged?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it falls within the permitted alternatives.

title: When Offence Proved Included in Offence Charged
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: An accused may be convicted of a minor offence included within the offence charged, even if not separately charged.
simpleExplanation: A court may convict for a lesser included offence when the major offence is not fully proved.
examples: A charge of grievous hurt may result in conviction for a lesser hurt offence.

faqs.question: Can a lesser offence result in conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: What Persons May Be Charged Jointly
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: Persons connected with the same offence, transaction, conspiracy, stolen property, or related offences may be charged and tried together.
simpleExplanation: The law allows joint trials for accused persons whose cases are connected.
examples: A thief and the receiver of stolen property are tried together.

faqs.question: Can co-accused be tried together?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specified situations.

title: Withdrawal of Remaining Charges on Conviction on One of Several Charges
chapter: Chapter XVIII - The Charge
officialText: After conviction on one or more charges, remaining charges may be withdrawn with court consent, having the effect of acquittal unless the conviction is later set aside.
simpleExplanation: Courts may stop pursuing remaining charges once a conviction is secured.
examples: After conviction on the principal charge, lesser charges are withdrawn.

faqs.question: What happens to withdrawn charges?
faqs.answer: They are treated as acquittals unless the conviction is overturned.

title: Trial to be Conducted by Public Prosecutor
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: Every trial before a Court of Session shall be conducted by a Public Prosecutor.
simpleExplanation: The prosecution in Sessions Court cases must be led by a Public Prosecutor.
examples: A murder trial in Sessions Court is conducted by a Public Prosecutor.

faqs.question: Who conducts a Sessions trial?
faqs.answer: A Public Prosecutor.

title: Opening Case for Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: The prosecutor shall open the case by describing the charge and outlining the evidence proposed to prove the guilt of the accused.
simpleExplanation: The prosecution begins the trial by explaining the case against the accused.
examples: The prosecutor summarizes the evidence at the start of trial.

faqs.question: What happens at the opening of a Sessions trial?
faqs.answer: The prosecution presents its case outline.

title: Discharge
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: The accused may apply for discharge within sixty days of commitment. If the Judge finds insufficient grounds for proceeding, the accused shall be discharged.
simpleExplanation: Before trial begins, the accused can seek discharge if the evidence is inadequate.
examples: The court discharges the accused after finding no prima facie case.

faqs.question: When can discharge be sought?
faqs.answer: Within sixty days of commitment of the case.

title: Framing of Charge
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If the Judge finds grounds for presuming that the accused has committed an offence, he may frame a charge. If the offence is not exclusively triable by the Court of Session, the case may be transferred to a competent Magistrate. If the offence is exclusively triable by the Court of Session, the Judge shall frame a written charge within sixty days from the first hearing on charge and explain it to the accused.
simpleExplanation: If there is sufficient material suggesting that the accused committed the offence, the Sessions Judge formally frames the charge and informs the accused of the allegations.
examples: A murder case proceeds to trial after the Sessions Judge frames charges.
A case found not exclusively triable by the Sessions Court is transferred to a Magistrate.

faqs.question: When must a charge be framed?
faqs.answer: Within sixty days from the first hearing on charge.

title: Conviction on Plea of Guilty
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If the accused pleads guilty, the Judge shall record the plea and may, in his discretion, convict the accused on that plea.
simpleExplanation: An accused who voluntarily admits guilt may be convicted without a full trial.
examples: The accused admits guilt during the framing of charge stage.

faqs.question: Is the Judge bound to convict?
faqs.answer: No. Conviction is at the Judge's discretion.

title: Date for Prosecution Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If the accused does not plead guilty or claims trial, the Judge shall fix a date for examination of witnesses and may issue process to compel attendance of witnesses or production of documents.
simpleExplanation: When the accused contests the case, the court schedules prosecution evidence.
examples: The court summons witnesses for examination.

faqs.question: Can the court summon witnesses?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Evidence for Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: The Judge shall record evidence produced by the prosecution. Evidence and depositions of public servants may be recorded through audio-video electronic means. Cross-examination may be deferred or witnesses recalled where necessary.
simpleExplanation: The prosecution presents evidence and examines witnesses to prove its case.
examples: A witness gives testimony through video conferencing.
A witness is recalled for further cross-examination.

faqs.question: Can evidence be recorded electronically?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Acquittal
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: If after considering prosecution evidence, examining the accused, and hearing both sides, the Judge finds no evidence connecting the accused to the offence, an order of acquittal shall be recorded.
simpleExplanation: The accused must be acquitted if the prosecution fails to produce evidence proving involvement in the offence.
examples: No witness identifies the accused and no evidence links him to the crime.

faqs.question: Can a Judge acquit before defence evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes, if there is no evidence against the accused.

title: Entering Upon Defence
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: Where the accused is not acquitted, he shall be called upon to enter his defence and produce evidence. The court shall issue process for defence witnesses unless the request is vexatious, intended to delay proceedings, or defeat justice.
simpleExplanation: The accused gets an opportunity to present evidence and witnesses in defence.
examples: The accused produces alibi witnesses.

faqs.question: Can defence witnesses be summoned by court?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Arguments
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: After defence evidence concludes, the prosecutor sums up the case and the accused or advocate replies. Legal points raised by the defence may be answered by the prosecution with the court's permission.
simpleExplanation: Both sides present final arguments before judgment.
examples: The defence argues that prosecution evidence is unreliable.

faqs.question: Can prosecution reply to legal arguments?
faqs.answer: Yes, with the Judge's permission.

title: Judgment of Acquittal or Conviction
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: The Judge shall pronounce judgment within thirty days of completion of arguments, extendable to forty-five days for recorded reasons. If convicted, the accused shall be heard on sentence before punishment is imposed.
simpleExplanation: The court delivers its decision and hears the accused on punishment before sentencing.
examples: The accused is convicted and heard separately on sentencing.

faqs.question: When should judgment be delivered?
faqs.answer: Normally within thirty days after arguments conclude.

title: Previous Conviction
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: Where a previous conviction is alleged and denied by the accused, the court may take evidence regarding the previous conviction after conviction under section 252 or 258 and record findings on it.
simpleExplanation: The court may verify earlier convictions after finding the accused guilty of the present offence.
examples: Evidence of a prior conviction is produced after conviction in the present case.

faqs.question: When is prior conviction examined?
faqs.answer: After conviction in the current case.

title: Procedure in Cases Instituted Under Section 222(2)
chapter: Chapter XIX - Trial Before a Court of Session
officialText: A Sessions Court taking cognizance under section 222(2) shall follow the procedure for warrant-cases instituted otherwise than on a police report. The trial may be held in camera. Where accusations are found to be without reasonable cause, compensation up to five thousand rupees may be awarded against the complainant.
simpleExplanation: Special procedures apply to certain complaint-based Sessions cases, including compensation for false accusations.
punishment: Compensation up to ₹5,000 for accusations made without reasonable cause.
examples: The court awards compensation after finding the accusation baseless.

faqs.question: Can compensation be awarded for false accusations?
faqs.answer: Yes, up to ₹5,000.

title: Compliance with Section 230
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When, in any warrant-case instituted on a police report, the accused appears or is brought before a Magistrate at the commencement of the trial, the Magistrate shall satisfy himself that he has complied with the provisions of section 230.
simpleExplanation: Before starting the trial, the Magistrate must ensure that the accused has received all documents and records required under the law.
examples: The Magistrate verifies that copies of the police report and witness statements have been supplied to the accused.

faqs.question: Why is compliance with section 230 important?
faqs.answer: It ensures the accused has access to all necessary documents for a fair trial.

title: When Accused Shall Be Discharged
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: The accused may apply for discharge within sixty days from the supply of documents under section 230. If the Magistrate finds the charge groundless after considering the police report, documents, examination of the accused, and hearing both sides, he shall discharge the accused and record reasons.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate must discharge the accused if the allegations are unsupported by sufficient material.
examples: The police report fails to establish any connection between the accused and the offence.

faqs.question: Can an accused seek discharge?
faqs.answer: Yes, within sixty days of receiving the required documents.

title: Framing of Charge
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the Magistrate finds grounds for presuming that the accused has committed an offence triable by him and adequately punishable by him, he shall frame a written charge within sixty days from the first hearing on charge and explain it to the accused.
simpleExplanation: When sufficient grounds exist, the Magistrate formally frames the charge against the accused.
examples: The Magistrate frames a charge after finding a prima facie case.

faqs.question: When must the charge be framed?
faqs.answer: Within sixty days from the first hearing on charge.

title: Conviction on Plea of Guilty
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea and may, in his discretion, convict the accused on that plea.
simpleExplanation: A guilty plea may result in conviction without a full trial.
examples: The accused admits the offence and the Magistrate records the plea.

faqs.question: Is conviction automatic after a guilty plea?
