faqs.answer: No, the Magistrate has discretion to convict.

title: Evidence for Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused does not plead guilty, the Magistrate shall fix a date for examination of witnesses. Witness statements shall be supplied in advance. Evidence may be recorded through audio-video electronic means, and witnesses may be recalled for further cross-examination.
simpleExplanation: The prosecution presents evidence and examines witnesses before the court.
examples: A witness testifies through video conferencing.
A witness is recalled for additional cross-examination.

faqs.question: Must witness statements be supplied beforehand?
faqs.answer: Yes, they must be supplied in advance to the accused.

title: Evidence for Defence
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: The accused shall be called upon to enter his defence and produce evidence. The Magistrate may issue process to summon witnesses or documents unless the request is intended to delay proceedings or defeat justice.
simpleExplanation: The accused is given an opportunity to present evidence and witnesses in support of the defence.
examples: The accused calls an alibi witness.
The defence produces documents supporting its case.

faqs.question: Can defence witnesses be summoned?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless the request is frivolous or intended to delay proceedings.

title: Evidence for Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
category: Cases Instituted Otherwise Than on Police Report
officialText: In warrant-cases instituted otherwise than on a police report, the Magistrate shall hear the prosecution and record evidence produced in support of the complaint. Summons may be issued for witnesses and documents.
simpleExplanation: In private complaint cases, the complainant must first present evidence before the Magistrate.
examples: A complainant produces witnesses to support a private complaint.

faqs.question: Who presents evidence first in a complaint case?
faqs.answer: The prosecution or complainant presents evidence first.

title: When Accused Shall Be Discharged
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If, after considering the prosecution evidence, the Magistrate finds that no case has been made out which would warrant conviction if unrebutted, the accused shall be discharged. The Magistrate may also discharge the accused earlier if the charge is groundless.
simpleExplanation: The accused must be discharged if the complaint does not establish a prima facie case.
examples: The complainant fails to produce evidence supporting the allegations.

faqs.question: Can discharge occur before all evidence is recorded?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the charge is clearly groundless.

title: Procedure Where Accused is Not Discharged
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If there is ground for presuming that the accused committed an offence triable by the Magistrate, a charge shall be framed. Witnesses may be recalled for cross-examination, additional prosecution evidence may be recorded, and prosecution evidence may be closed if witnesses cannot be secured despite reasonable efforts.
simpleExplanation: If discharge is not granted, the Magistrate frames charges and proceeds with trial.
examples: The accused pleads not guilty after charges are framed.

faqs.question: Can witnesses be recalled after charges are framed?
faqs.answer: Yes, for cross-examination and re-examination.

title: Evidence for Defence
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: After prosecution evidence is completed under section 269, the accused shall enter upon his defence and produce evidence. The provisions of section 266 relating to defence evidence apply.
simpleExplanation: The accused receives an opportunity to present witnesses, documents, and other evidence in defence.
examples: The accused produces documentary evidence supporting innocence.

faqs.question: When does the defence present evidence?
faqs.answer: After the prosecution evidence has concluded.

title: Acquittal or Conviction
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If, in any case under this Chapter in which a charge has been framed, the Magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he shall record an order of acquittal. If the accused is found guilty, the Magistrate shall hear the accused on the question of sentence and pass sentence according to law. Where a previous conviction is alleged and denied, evidence regarding such conviction may be taken after conviction and a finding recorded thereon.
simpleExplanation: After the trial is completed, the Magistrate either acquits or convicts the accused. If convicted, the accused is given an opportunity to be heard on sentencing.
punishment: Depends on the offence for which the accused is convicted.
examples: The Magistrate acquits an accused because the prosecution failed to prove the case.
The accused is convicted and later sentenced after being heard on punishment.

faqs.question: Can the accused be heard before sentencing?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate must hear the accused on the question of sentence.

faqs.question: When is previous conviction considered?
faqs.answer: After conviction in the present case.

title: Absence of Complainant
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When proceedings have been instituted upon complaint, and on any day fixed for hearing the complainant is absent, and the offence may lawfully be compounded or is not a cognizable offence, the Magistrate may, after giving thirty days' time to the complainant to be present, discharge the accused before the charge has been framed.
simpleExplanation: If the complainant repeatedly fails to appear in certain complaint cases, the Magistrate may discharge the accused.
examples: A complainant does not appear despite notice, and the Magistrate discharges the accused.

faqs.question: Can an accused be discharged due to the complainant's absence?
faqs.answer: Yes, in eligible complaint cases before the charge is framed.

title: Compensation for Accusation Without Reasonable Cause
chapter: Chapter XX - Trial of Warrant-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: Where a Magistrate discharges or acquits an accused and finds that there was no reasonable ground for making the accusation, the complainant or informant may be directed to show cause why compensation should not be paid to the accused. After considering the explanation, the Magistrate may order compensation and, in default of payment, direct simple imprisonment. The order does not exempt the complainant from any civil or criminal liability arising from the accusation.
simpleExplanation: A person who makes a baseless accusation may be ordered to compensate the accused.
punishment: Compensation up to the amount of fine the Magistrate is empowered to impose; simple imprisonment up to 30 days in default of payment.
examples: A false complaint results in acquittal and compensation being awarded to the accused.
A complainant is ordered to pay compensation for a groundless accusation.

faqs.question: Can compensation be awarded for a false accusation?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Magistrate finds there was no reasonable basis for the accusation.

faqs.question: Can imprisonment be ordered for non-payment?
faqs.answer: Yes, simple imprisonment up to thirty days may be imposed.

title: Substance of Accusation to be Stated
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: When in a summons-case the accused appears or is brought before the Magistrate, the particulars of the offence shall be stated to him and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty or has any defence to make. A formal charge is not necessary. If the accusation appears groundless, the Magistrate may release the accused after recording reasons, and such release shall have the effect of discharge.
simpleExplanation: In summons cases, the accusation is explained to the accused without framing a formal charge.
examples: The Magistrate explains the allegations in a minor offence case without framing a written charge.
An accused is discharged because the accusation is found to be groundless.

faqs.question: Is a formal charge required in a summons case?
faqs.answer: No. The particulars of the accusation are sufficient.

faqs.question: Can the accused be discharged at this stage?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the accusation is groundless.

title: Conviction on Plea of Guilty
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea as nearly as possible in the words used by the accused and may, in his discretion, convict him thereon.
simpleExplanation: An accused who admits guilt in a summons case may be convicted based on that admission.
examples: An accused admits committing a minor offence and is convicted on the guilty plea.

faqs.question: Must the Magistrate record the exact words of the plea?
faqs.answer: The plea should be recorded as nearly as possible in the accused's own words.

faqs.question: Is conviction automatic after a guilty plea?
faqs.answer: No. The Magistrate may convict in his discretion.

title: Conviction on Plea of Guilty in Absence of Accused in Petty Cases
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: Where a summons has been issued under section 229 in a petty offence, the accused may plead guilty by post, electronic means, or through an advocate and remit the specified fine without appearing before the Magistrate. The Magistrate may convict the accused and impose the fine accordingly.
simpleExplanation: For minor offences, the accused can admit guilt and pay the fine without attending court personally.
punishment: Fine as specified in the summons.
examples: An accused pays a traffic-related fine through electronic mode and pleads guilty.
A person authorizes an advocate to submit a guilty plea in a petty offence case.

faqs.question: Must the accused appear personally?
faqs.answer: No. Personal appearance may be dispensed with in eligible petty cases.

title: Procedure When Accused Does Not Plead Guilty
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the accused does not plead guilty, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the prosecution, record evidence, hear the accused and take all evidence necessary for determination of the case.
simpleExplanation: If the accused contests the allegation, the court conducts a regular trial.
examples: The accused denies the allegation and witnesses are examined.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused pleads not guilty?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate proceeds with recording evidence and conducting the trial.

title: Acquittal or Conviction
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: After taking evidence and hearing the parties, the Magistrate shall either acquit or convict the accused and pass sentence according to law.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate decides whether the accused is guilty and passes the appropriate order.
examples: The accused is acquitted due to lack of evidence.
The accused is convicted after the offence is proved.

faqs.question: Can the Magistrate acquit the accused?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the prosecution fails to prove the case.

title: Non-Appearance or Death of Complainant
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: If the complainant is absent on the date fixed for hearing, the Magistrate may acquit the accused unless there is reason to adjourn the case. Similar principles apply where the complainant has died.
simpleExplanation: If the complainant does not appear, the court may dismiss the case and acquit the accused.
examples: The complainant repeatedly fails to attend court and the accused is acquitted.

faqs.question: Can the case continue if the complainant dies?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate may permit continuation where appropriate.

title: Withdrawal of Complaint
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: Before final order is passed, a complainant may seek permission to withdraw the complaint. If permitted, the accused shall be acquitted.
simpleExplanation: The complainant can withdraw the complaint with the court's permission.
examples: Parties settle the dispute and the complaint is withdrawn.

faqs.question: What happens after withdrawal?
faqs.answer: The accused is acquitted.

title: Power to Stop Proceedings in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: In summons cases instituted otherwise than upon complaint, the Magistrate may stop proceedings at any stage. Release of the accused after recording principal evidence amounts to acquittal; otherwise it amounts to discharge.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate may terminate certain summons cases before completion.
examples: Proceedings are stopped due to lack of evidence.

faqs.question: Does stopping proceedings always result in acquittal?
faqs.answer: No. It may amount to discharge depending on the stage of the case.

title: Power of Court to Convert Summons-Cases into Warrant-Cases
chapter: Chapter XXI - Trial of Summons-Cases by Magistrates
officialText: Where a summons case involves an offence punishable with imprisonment exceeding six months and the Magistrate considers it desirable, the case may be converted into a warrant case and tried accordingly.
simpleExplanation: More serious summons cases may be converted into warrant cases for a fuller trial procedure.
examples: A complex criminal case initially filed as a summons case is converted into a warrant case.

faqs.question: Why convert a summons case?
faqs.answer: To ensure a more detailed trial procedure where required.

title: Power to Try Summarily
chapter: Chapter XXII - Summary Trials
officialText: Chief Judicial Magistrates, Metropolitan Magistrates and specially empowered Magistrates may try specified offences summarily, including offences punishable with imprisonment up to three years, subject to statutory limitations.
simpleExplanation: Certain less serious offences can be disposed of quickly through summary trials.
punishment: As provided for the underlying offence.
examples: A minor theft case is tried summarily.

faqs.question: What is a summary trial?
faqs.answer: A simplified and expedited criminal trial procedure.

title: Summary Trial by Magistrate of Second Class
chapter: Chapter XXII - Summary Trials
officialText: The High Court may empower a Magistrate of the Second Class to try specified offences summarily.
simpleExplanation: Certain Second Class Magistrates may conduct summary trials when authorized by the High Court.
punishment: As provided for the underlying offence.
examples: A Second Class Magistrate conducts a summary trial after receiving High Court authorization.

faqs.question: Can every Second Class Magistrate conduct summary trials?
faqs.answer: No. Specific authorization from the High Court is required.

title: Procedure for Summary Trials
chapter: Chapter XXII - Summary Trials
officialText: Summary trials shall generally follow the procedure for summons cases. However, no sentence of imprisonment exceeding three months shall be passed in a summary trial.
simpleExplanation: Summary trials use a simplified procedure and have limits on imprisonment.
punishment: Maximum imprisonment of three months in summary trials.
examples: A Magistrate convicts an accused in a summary trial and imposes one month imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can a court impose one year imprisonment in a summary trial?
faqs.answer: No. Imprisonment cannot exceed three months.

title: Record in Summary Trials
chapter: Chapter XXII - Summary Trials
officialText: In every case tried summarily, the Magistrate shall enter, in such form as the State Government may direct, the following particulars, namely: (a) the serial number of the case; (b) the date of the commission of the offence; (c) the date of the report or complaint; (d) the name of the complainant, if any; (e) the name, parentage and residence of the accused; (f) the offence complained of and the offence proved, and where applicable the value of the property involved; (g) the plea of the accused and his examination, if any; (h) the finding; (i) the sentence or other final order; and (j) the date on which proceedings terminated.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate must maintain a prescribed record containing essential details of every summary trial.
examples: A Magistrate records the accused's plea and sentence in a summary theft case.
The court register includes the date of offence and final order.

faqs.question: What details are recorded in a summary trial?
faqs.answer: The law requires recording particulars such as the offence, accused details, plea, finding, and sentence.

title: Judgment in Cases Tried Summarily
chapter: Chapter XXII - Summary Trials
officialText: In every case tried summarily in which the accused does not plead guilty, the Magistrate shall record the substance of the evidence and a judgment containing a brief statement of the reasons for the finding.
simpleExplanation: Where the accused contests the case, the Magistrate must briefly record the evidence and reasons for the decision.
examples: The Magistrate summarizes witness testimony before convicting the accused.
A brief judgment explains why the accused was acquitted.

faqs.question: Must detailed evidence be recorded?
faqs.answer: No. Only the substance of the evidence and brief reasons for the decision are required.

title: Language of Record and Judgment
chapter: Chapter XXII - Summary Trials
officialText: (1) Every such record and judgment shall be written in the language of the Court. (2) The High Court may authorise any Magistrate empowered to try offences summarily to prepare the record or judgment or both through an officer appointed by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and the record or judgment so prepared shall be signed by such Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Records and judgments in summary trials must be prepared in the court's language and may be drafted by an authorised officer under the Magistrate's supervision.
examples: A judgment is written in the official language used by the Court.
An authorised court officer prepares the record which is signed by the Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can someone other than the Magistrate prepare the record?
faqs.answer: Yes, if authorised by the High Court and appointed by the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

title: Application of Chapter
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: (1) This Chapter applies where an accused is charged with an offence punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and not punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment exceeding seven years. It does not apply to offences affecting the socio-economic condition of the country or offences committed against a woman or child. (2) The Central Government shall notify offences affecting the socio-economic condition of the country.
simpleExplanation: Plea bargaining is available only for certain less serious offences and is excluded for offences against women, children, and specified socio-economic offences.
examples: An accused charged with a five-year offence may seek plea bargaining.
Plea bargaining is unavailable for an offence committed against a child.

faqs.question: Can plea bargaining be used for murder cases?
faqs.answer: No. It does not apply to offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.

title: Application for Plea Bargaining
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: (1) An accused may file an application for plea bargaining within thirty days from the date of framing of charge. (2) The application shall contain a brief description of the case and be accompanied by an affidavit stating that the application is voluntary and that the accused has not previously been convicted for the same offence. (3) The Court shall issue notice to the Public Prosecutor or complainant and the accused. (4) The Court shall examine the accused in camera to verify voluntariness. If satisfied, it shall allow up to sixty days for working out a mutually satisfactory disposition; otherwise, regular proceedings shall continue.
simpleExplanation: The accused must voluntarily apply for plea bargaining and satisfy the Court that the request is genuine.
examples: An accused files a plea bargaining application within thirty days of framing of charge.
The Court rejects an application found to be involuntary.

faqs.question: Must the application be voluntary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court verifies voluntariness through an in-camera examination.

title: Guidelines for Mutually Satisfactory Disposition
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: For working out a mutually satisfactory disposition, the Court shall convene meetings involving the Public Prosecutor, investigating officer, accused and victim in police-report cases, or the accused and victim in complaint cases. The Court shall ensure that the process remains voluntary. The accused and victim may participate with their advocates if they so desire.
simpleExplanation: The Court facilitates voluntary discussions between the parties to reach a mutually acceptable settlement.
examples: The victim and accused agree on compensation as part of a plea bargain.
The accused participates in the meeting with legal counsel.

faqs.question: Can advocates attend the plea bargaining meeting?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the accused or victim wishes.

title: Report of Mutually Satisfactory Disposition to be Submitted Before Court
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Where a satisfactory disposition is reached, the Court shall prepare a report signed by the presiding officer and all participants. If no disposition is reached, the Court shall record that fact and continue proceedings from the stage at which the plea bargaining application was filed.
simpleExplanation: The Court formally records whether plea bargaining succeeded or failed.
examples: A settlement agreement is signed by all parties and filed before the Court.
The Court resumes the regular trial after failed negotiations.

faqs.question: What happens if no agreement is reached?
faqs.answer: The case proceeds under the normal criminal procedure.

title: Disposal of Case
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Where a satisfactory disposition has been reached, the Court shall award compensation to the victim and hear the parties regarding punishment, probation, or admonition. If a minimum punishment is prescribed, the Court may impose one-half of such punishment, or one-fourth where the accused is a first-time offender. In other cases, the Court may impose one-fourth of the prescribed punishment, or one-sixth where the accused is a first-time offender.
simpleExplanation: Successful plea bargaining may result in reduced punishment and compensation to the victim.
punishment: Reduced punishment as prescribed under this section.
examples: A first-time offender receives one-sixth of the prescribed punishment.
The victim is awarded compensation as part of the settlement.

faqs.question: Can punishment be reduced under plea bargaining?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may impose reduced punishment according to the statute.

title: Judgment of Court
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The Court shall deliver its judgment in terms of section 293 in open Court and the judgment shall be signed by the presiding officer.
simpleExplanation: The Court pronounces the final plea bargaining judgment in open court.
examples: The Court pronounces a reduced sentence agreed through plea bargaining.

faqs.question: Where is the judgment delivered?
faqs.answer: In open Court.

title: Finality of Judgment
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The judgment delivered under this Chapter shall be final and no appeal shall lie against such judgment except a Special Leave Petition under Article 136 or a writ petition under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India.
simpleExplanation: A plea bargaining judgment is generally final and cannot be appealed through ordinary appellate procedures.
examples: A convicted person cannot file a regular appeal against a plea bargaining judgment.
A challenge may still be made through constitutional remedies.

faqs.question: Can a plea bargaining judgment be appealed?
faqs.answer: Ordinarily no, except through constitutional remedies such as SLPs and writ petitions.

title: Power of Court in Plea Bargaining
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: A Court shall have, for the purposes of discharging its functions under this Chapter, all the powers vested in respect of bail, trial of offences and other matters relating to the disposal of a case in such Court under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: While dealing with plea bargaining, the Court retains all its normal powers relating to bail, trial, and disposal of criminal cases.
examples: The Court grants bail while plea bargaining proceedings are pending.
The Court exercises its normal trial powers during plea bargaining.

faqs.question: Does plea bargaining limit the powers of the Court?
faqs.answer: No. The Court retains all powers available under the BNSS.

title: Period of Detention Undergone by Accused to be Set Off Against Sentence of Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The provisions of section 468 shall apply for setting off the period of detention undergone by the accused against the sentence of imprisonment imposed under this Chapter, in the same manner as they apply in respect of imprisonment under other provisions of this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: Any time already spent in custody by the accused will be deducted from the imprisonment imposed through plea bargaining.
examples: An accused who spent two months in custody receives credit for that period against the final sentence.

faqs.question: Will pre-trial detention be counted toward the sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The detention period is set off against the imprisonment imposed.

title: Savings
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other provisions of this Sanhita and nothing in such other provisions shall be construed to constrain the meaning of any provision of this Chapter. Explanation.—For the purposes of this Chapter, the expression 'Public Prosecutor' includes an Assistant Public Prosecutor appointed under section 19.
simpleExplanation: The plea bargaining provisions override any inconsistent provisions elsewhere in the BNSS and broadly define the term Public Prosecutor.
examples: A plea bargaining provision prevails where another procedural provision appears inconsistent.

faqs.question: Do plea bargaining provisions override conflicting provisions?
faqs.answer: Yes. This Chapter has overriding effect.

title: Statements of Accused Not to be Used
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force, the statements or facts stated by an accused in an application for plea bargaining filed under section 290 shall not be used for any other purpose except for the purpose of this Chapter.
simpleExplanation: Statements made by an accused while seeking plea bargaining cannot be used against him in other proceedings.
examples: Admissions made during plea bargaining discussions cannot later be used as evidence in a regular trial.

faqs.question: Can plea bargaining statements be used in another case?
faqs.answer: No. They may only be used for plea bargaining proceedings.

title: Non-Application of Chapter
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Plea Bargaining
officialText: Nothing in this Chapter shall apply to any juvenile or child as defined in section 2 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
simpleExplanation: Plea bargaining is not available for juveniles or children covered by the Juvenile Justice Act.
examples: A child in conflict with law cannot seek plea bargaining under this Chapter.

faqs.question: Can a juvenile use plea bargaining?
faqs.answer: No. Juveniles and children are excluded from the plea bargaining provisions.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter XXIV - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: In this Chapter, 'detained' includes detention under any law providing for preventive detention; and 'prison' includes any place declared by the State Government to be a subsidiary jail and any reformatory, Borstal institution or similar institution.
simpleExplanation: This section defines important terms used in the Chapter relating to attendance of prisoners before courts.
examples: A Borstal institution is treated as a prison for the purposes of this Chapter.

faqs.question: Does 'prison' include reformatory institutions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reformatory and Borstal institutions are included.

title: Power to Require Attendance of Prisoners
chapter: Chapter XXIV - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: A Criminal Court may order the production of a person confined or detained in prison where his attendance is required for answering a charge, participating in proceedings, or giving evidence as a witness. Orders of a Second Class Magistrate require countersignature of the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Courts can require prisoners to be brought before them when necessary for criminal proceedings or evidence.
examples: A prisoner is brought before court to face a fresh criminal charge.
A detained person is produced as a witness in another case.

faqs.question: Can a prisoner be produced as a witness?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may require attendance for giving evidence.

title: Power of State Government or Central Government to Exclude Certain Persons from Operation of Section 302
chapter: Chapter XXIV - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: The State Government or Central Government may direct that certain prisoners or classes of prisoners shall not be removed from prison. While making such order, regard shall be had to the nature of the offence, public order considerations, and public interest.
simpleExplanation: Governments may prohibit the production of certain prisoners before courts where security or public interest concerns exist.
examples: A high-risk prisoner is not allowed to be removed from prison due to security concerns.

faqs.question: Why can a prisoner be excluded from production before court?
faqs.answer: For reasons relating to public order, security, or public interest.

title: Officer in Charge of Prison to Abstain from Carrying Out Order in Certain Contingencies
chapter: Chapter XXIV - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: The prison officer shall not execute a production order where the prisoner is unfit due to sickness, under remand, likely to be released before completion of transfer, or covered by a government order under section 303. Reasons must be communicated to the Court.
simpleExplanation: Prison authorities may refuse to produce a prisoner in specific legally recognized circumstances.
examples: A seriously ill prisoner is not transported to court.
A prisoner covered by a government exclusion order is not produced.

faqs.question: Must the prison officer explain non-compliance?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reasons must be sent to the Court.

title: Prisoner to be Brought to Court in Custody
chapter: Chapter XXIV - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: Subject to section 304, the officer in charge of the prison shall produce the prisoner before the Court at the specified time and keep him in custody until examined or until the Court authorises his return to prison.
simpleExplanation: When a valid production order is received, the prison authorities must bring the prisoner to court and keep him in lawful custody.
examples: A prisoner is escorted to court for testimony and returned to prison after examination.

faqs.question: Who is responsible for the prisoner's custody during court attendance?
faqs.answer: The prison authorities remain responsible until the Court authorises return.

title: Power to Issue Commission for Examination of Witness in Prison
chapter: Chapter XXIV - Attendance of Persons Confined or Detained in Prisons
officialText: The provisions of this Chapter shall be without prejudice to the power of the Court to issue, under section 319, a commission for the examination, as a witness, of any person confined or detained in a prison; and the provisions of Part B of Chapter XXV shall apply in relation to the examination on commission of any such person in the prison as they apply in relation to the examination on commission of any other person.
simpleExplanation: Courts may appoint a commission to record the testimony of a prisoner without requiring the prisoner to be physically produced before the Court.
examples: A prisoner serving a sentence is examined as a witness through a commission.
The Court records evidence from a detained person without transporting him to Court.

faqs.question: Can a prisoner's evidence be recorded without bringing him to Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may issue a commission for examination of the prisoner.

title: Language of Courts
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The State Government may determine what shall be, for purposes of this Sanhita, the language of each Court within the State other than the High Court.
simpleExplanation: The State Government decides the official language to be used in courts within the State, except the High Court.
examples: A State Government declares Kannada as the language of certain courts.
District courts use the language notified by the State Government.

faqs.question: Who determines the language of a court?
faqs.answer: The State Government determines the language of courts other than the High Court.

title: Evidence to be Taken in Presence of Accused
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Except as otherwise expressly provided, all evidence taken during a trial or proceeding shall be taken in the presence of the accused or, when personal attendance is dispensed with, in the presence of his advocate, including through audio-video electronic means. Where evidence of a woman below eighteen years alleging rape or another sexual offence is recorded, appropriate measures may be taken to prevent confrontation with the accused while preserving the right of cross-examination.
simpleExplanation: Evidence is normally recorded in the presence of the accused or the accused's lawyer, ensuring a fair trial.
examples: A witness testifies through video conferencing in the presence of the accused's advocate.
A minor rape survivor gives evidence without facing the accused directly.

faqs.question: Must evidence always be recorded in the presence of the accused?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless personal attendance is dispensed with, in which case the advocate may represent the accused.

title: Record in Summons-Cases and Inquiries
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In summons-cases, inquiries under sections 164 to 167, and proceedings under section 491 otherwise than during trial, the Magistrate shall record a memorandum of the substance of evidence in the language of the Court. If unable to do so personally, he shall record reasons and cause it to be written from his dictation in open Court. Such memorandum shall be signed by the Magistrate and form part of the record.
simpleExplanation: For simpler proceedings, only a summary of witness evidence is recorded rather than the full testimony.
examples: A Magistrate records the essence of a witness statement in a summons case.
The memorandum becomes part of the court record.

faqs.question: Is full evidence recorded in summons cases?
faqs.answer: No. A memorandum of the substance of evidence is generally sufficient.

title: Record in Warrant-Cases
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In all warrant-cases tried before a Magistrate, the evidence of each witness shall be taken down in writing by the Magistrate, by dictation in open Court, or under his supervision by an authorised officer. Evidence may also be recorded through audio-video electronic means. The evidence shall ordinarily be recorded in narrative form and signed by the Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Witness testimony in warrant cases must be formally recorded and become part of the court record.
examples: A witness's testimony is recorded through video conferencing.
The Magistrate signs the written evidence after recording.

faqs.question: Can evidence be recorded electronically?
faqs.answer: Yes. Audio-video electronic recording is permitted.

title: Record in Trial Before Court of Session
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: In all trials before a Court of Session, the evidence of each witness shall be taken down in writing by the presiding Judge, by dictation in open Court, or under his direction by an authorised court officer. The evidence shall ordinarily be recorded in narrative form and signed by the Judge.
simpleExplanation: The Sessions Judge must ensure that witness evidence is formally recorded during Sessions trials.
examples: A Sessions Judge records witness testimony during a murder trial.
Evidence recorded by a court officer is signed by the Judge.

faqs.question: Who signs evidence recorded in a Sessions trial?
faqs.answer: The presiding Sessions Judge.

title: Language of Record of Evidence
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Evidence shall ordinarily be recorded in the language of the Court. Where a witness gives evidence in another language, it may be recorded in that language if practicable; otherwise a true translation shall be prepared and signed by the Magistrate or Judge. The translation shall form part of the record.
simpleExplanation: Witness evidence given in another language must be translated accurately for the court record.
examples: Evidence given in Tamil is translated into the court language.
The Judge signs the translated record.

faqs.question: What happens if a witness speaks a different language?
faqs.answer: The evidence is translated and included in the official record.

title: Procedure in Regard to Evidence When Completed
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: After evidence is recorded under section 310 or 311, it shall be read over to the witness in the presence of the accused or advocate and corrected if necessary. If the witness disputes any portion, the Court may record the objection and its remarks. Where the record is in another language, it shall be interpreted to the witness.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses are given an opportunity to verify the accuracy of their recorded testimony.
examples: A witness requests correction of an incorrectly recorded statement.
The Court records an objection raised by the witness.

faqs.question: Can a witness challenge the recorded testimony?
faqs.answer: Yes. Objections may be recorded by the Court.

title: Interpretation of Evidence to Accused or His Advocate
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Whenever evidence is given in a language not understood by the accused, it shall be interpreted to him in open Court. Where the accused is represented by an advocate who does not understand the language, it shall be interpreted to the advocate. The Court may interpret only so much of formal documents as necessary.
simpleExplanation: The accused has a right to understand the evidence being presented in court.
examples: Evidence given in Marathi is interpreted to an accused who understands only Hindi.
An advocate receives interpretation of testimony given in another language.

faqs.question: Why is interpretation required?
faqs.answer: To ensure that the accused can understand and effectively defend against the evidence.

title: Remarks Respecting Demeanour of Witness
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When a presiding Judge or Magistrate records the evidence of a witness, he shall also record such remarks, if any, as he thinks material respecting the demeanour of the witness while under examination.
simpleExplanation: The Judge or Magistrate may note important observations about the behaviour or conduct of a witness while giving evidence.
examples: A Judge records that a witness appeared evasive during questioning.
A Magistrate notes the witness's confidence and consistency.

faqs.question: Why are remarks about demeanour recorded?
faqs.answer: They may help the Court assess the credibility and reliability of the witness.

title: Record of Examination of Accused
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Whenever the accused is examined by a Magistrate or Court of Session, the entire examination including every question and answer shall be recorded in full by the presiding Judge or Magistrate, or under his direction by an authorised officer. The record shall be shown or read to the accused, interpreted where necessary, signed by the accused and the Judge or Magistrate, and certified as a true account. Where the accused is examined through electronic communication, his signature shall be obtained within seventy-two hours. This section does not apply to summary trials.
simpleExplanation: The complete statement of the accused must be accurately recorded, verified, and signed.
examples: An accused answers questions during trial and the entire examination is recorded.
An accused examined through video conferencing signs the record within seventy-two hours.

faqs.question: Must the accused sign the recorded statement?
faqs.answer: Yes, except where electronic examination requires signature within seventy-two hours.

title: Interpreter to be Bound to Interpret Truthfully
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When the services of an interpreter are required by a Criminal Court for interpretation of evidence or statements, the interpreter shall be bound to state the true interpretation of such evidence or statement.
simpleExplanation: Court interpreters must provide accurate and truthful translations.
punishment: False interpretation may attract legal consequences under applicable law.
examples: An interpreter translates witness testimony from Tamil to Hindi.
A court interpreter assists an accused who does not understand the court language.

faqs.question: Can an interpreter alter the meaning of testimony?
faqs.answer: No. The interpreter must provide a truthful interpretation.

title: Record in High Court
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Every High Court may prescribe by general rule the manner in which evidence of witnesses and examination of accused shall be recorded in proceedings before it, and such evidence shall be taken down in accordance with those rules.
simpleExplanation: High Courts may establish their own rules regarding recording of evidence and examination of accused persons.
examples: A High Court adopts electronic recording procedures for witness testimony.

faqs.question: Who determines how evidence is recorded in the High Court?
faqs.answer: The High Court through its rules.

title: When Attendance of Witness May be Dispensed With and Commission Issued
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where examination of a witness is necessary for the ends of justice but attendance cannot be procured without unreasonable delay, expense or inconvenience, the Court may issue a commission for examination of the witness. A commission shall be issued for examination of the President, Vice-President, Governor, or Administrator of a Union Territory when their evidence is required.
simpleExplanation: Courts may record evidence through a commission when bringing a witness to court is impractical.
examples: A seriously ill witness is examined through a commission.
Evidence of a Governor is recorded through a commission.

faqs.question: Why is a commission issued?
faqs.answer: To record evidence where personal attendance would be unreasonable or impractical.

title: Commission to Whom to be Issued
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A commission for examination of a witness shall be directed to the Chief Judicial Magistrate where the witness is located within India, or to such authority as may be specified by the Central Government where the witness is outside the jurisdiction or outside India.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies the authority to whom a commission should be addressed.
examples: A commission is sent to the Chief Judicial Magistrate of another district.

faqs.question: Who receives a commission for examining a witness?
faqs.answer: Usually the Chief Judicial Magistrate or another authority notified by the Central Government.

title: Execution of Commissions
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Upon receipt of a commission, the Chief Judicial Magistrate or appointed Magistrate shall summon the witness or proceed to the place where the witness is located and record evidence in the same manner as in warrant cases.
simpleExplanation: The authority receiving the commission is responsible for recording the witness's evidence.
examples: A Magistrate visits a hospital to record the statement of a bedridden witness.

faqs.question: How is evidence recorded under a commission?
faqs.answer: In the same manner as evidence in warrant cases.

title: Parties May Examine Witnesses
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Parties may submit written interrogatories for examination of witnesses under commission and may appear personally or through advocates to examine, cross-examine, and re-examine the witness.
simpleExplanation: Both parties retain the right to question witnesses examined under a commission.
examples: The defence cross-examines a witness whose evidence is being recorded under commission.

faqs.question: Can advocates participate in commission proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. They may examine and cross-examine witnesses.

title: Return of Commission
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: After execution, the commission together with the deposition shall be returned to the issuing Court. The deposition may be inspected by the parties, read in evidence, and form part of the record.
simpleExplanation: Evidence recorded through commission becomes part of the official court record.
examples: The deposition of a witness recorded in another State is returned to the trial court.

faqs.question: Can evidence recorded under commission be used in trial?
faqs.answer: Yes. It forms part of the record and may be read in evidence.

title: Adjournment of Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a commission has been issued under section 319, the inquiry, trial, or proceeding may be adjourned for a reasonable period sufficient for execution and return of the commission.
simpleExplanation: The Court may temporarily postpone proceedings until the commissioned evidence is received.
examples: A trial is adjourned until evidence recorded abroad is returned.

faqs.question: Why can a proceeding be adjourned?
faqs.answer: To allow time for execution and return of a commission.

title: Execution of Foreign Commissions
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The provisions regarding execution and return of commissions apply to commissions issued by specified courts, judges, and magistrates in areas outside the operation of this Sanhita and in foreign jurisdictions notified by the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: This section governs execution of commissions received from certain foreign and external jurisdictions.
examples: A foreign court requests examination of a witness located in India.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to foreign courts?
faqs.answer: Yes, where notified by the Central Government.

title: Deposition of Medical Witness
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The deposition of a civil surgeon or other medical witness, taken and attested by a Magistrate in the presence of the accused or taken on commission, may be used in evidence even if the medical witness is not called. The Court may summon such witness if required.
simpleExplanation: Medical evidence can often be admitted without requiring the doctor to appear personally.
examples: A post-mortem doctor's deposition is read into evidence without requiring personal attendance.

faqs.question: Can a medical witness still be summoned?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may summon the witness if necessary.

title: Identification Report of Magistrate
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A report of identification made by an Executive Magistrate regarding a person or property may be used as evidence even if the Magistrate is not called as a witness, subject to provisions of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam. The Court may summon the Magistrate if required.
simpleExplanation: Identification reports prepared by Magistrates can be admitted as evidence without requiring their personal testimony.
examples: A test identification parade report is produced during trial.

faqs.question: Can the Magistrate still be called to testify?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may summon and examine the Magistrate.

title: Evidence of Officers of Mint, Security Press and Forensic Institutions
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Reports of gazetted officers of Mints, Security Printing Presses, Forensic Science Laboratories, Government Examiners of Questioned Documents and similar notified authorities may be used as evidence without requiring personal testimony, though the Court may summon such officers if necessary.
simpleExplanation: Certain official forensic and technical reports can be admitted directly into evidence.
examples: A forensic document examination report is admitted without calling the expert.

faqs.question: Can the Court summon the officer who prepared the report?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Court considers it necessary.

title: Reports of Government Scientific Experts
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Reports of Government scientific experts such as Chemical Examiners, Chief Controller of Explosives, Director of Finger Print Bureau, Forensic Science Laboratory officials, Serologists, and other notified experts may be used as evidence. The Court may summon such experts or permit a responsible officer to appear on their behalf.
simpleExplanation: Scientific expert reports are admissible as evidence and experts may be called when necessary.
examples: A fingerprint report from the Finger Print Bureau is produced in evidence.
A forensic scientist's report is admitted during trial.

faqs.question: Must a scientific expert always appear in court?
faqs.answer: No. Their report may be admitted directly unless the Court requires testimony.

title: Admission or Denial of Documents
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where documents are filed by the prosecution or accused, a list shall be prepared and the parties shall be called upon to admit or deny the genuineness of each document within thirty days of supply. If genuineness is not disputed, the document may be read in evidence without formal proof, unless the Court directs otherwise.
simpleExplanation: Parties must quickly identify which documents are disputed so that unnecessary witnesses and proof can be avoided.
examples: The accused admits the genuineness of bank records, eliminating the need for formal proof.
A disputed forensic report requires the expert to be called.

faqs.question: What happens if a document is not disputed?
faqs.answer: It may be admitted into evidence without requiring proof of signature.

title: Affidavit in Proof of Conduct of Public Servants
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: When any application is made to a Court during an inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita and allegations are made concerning a public servant, the applicant may give evidence of the alleged facts by affidavit, and the Court may order that such evidence be given in that manner.
simpleExplanation: Facts relating to the conduct of a public servant may be proved through an affidavit instead of oral testimony.
examples: A complainant files an affidavit alleging misconduct by a public servant.
The Court permits evidence relating to a government officer's conduct to be submitted through affidavit.

faqs.question: Can allegations against a public servant be proved through an affidavit?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may permit such facts to be proved by affidavit.

title: Evidence of Formal Character on Affidavit
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The evidence of a person whose evidence is of a formal character may be given by affidavit and may be read in evidence in any inquiry, trial or proceeding. The Court may summon and examine such person if requested by the prosecution or the accused, or if it thinks fit.
simpleExplanation: Routine or formal evidence may be submitted through affidavits, though the witness may still be called for examination.
examples: A clerk submits an affidavit confirming official records.
The accused requests the Court to summon the deponent for questioning.

faqs.question: Can a person who submitted an affidavit be called to Court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may summon and examine the person.

title: Authorities Before Whom Affidavits May Be Sworn
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Affidavits may be sworn or affirmed before a Judge, Judicial Magistrate, Executive Magistrate, Commissioner of Oaths, or Notary. Affidavits shall clearly distinguish facts based on personal knowledge from those based on belief and state the grounds of such belief. The Court may strike out scandalous or irrelevant matter.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies who can administer affidavits and the standards that affidavits must meet.
examples: An affidavit is sworn before a Notary.
The Court removes irrelevant allegations from an affidavit.

faqs.question: Who can administer an affidavit under BNSS?
faqs.answer: A Judge, Magistrate, Commissioner of Oaths, or Notary.

title: Previous Conviction or Acquittal How Proved
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A previous conviction or acquittal may be proved by certified extracts of court records, jail certificates, warrants of commitment, and evidence establishing the identity of the accused as the person previously convicted or acquitted.
simpleExplanation: The law specifies how prior convictions or acquittals can be formally proved before a court.
examples: A certified judgment copy is produced to prove a previous conviction.
A jail certificate is used to establish that a sentence was served.

faqs.question: How can a previous conviction be proved?
faqs.answer: Through certified court records, jail certificates, or warrants along with proof of identity.

title: Record of Evidence in Absence of Accused
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where an accused has absconded and there is no immediate prospect of arrest, the Court may examine prosecution witnesses in his absence and record their depositions. Such depositions may later be used against the accused if the witness is dead, incapable of giving evidence, cannot be found, or cannot reasonably be produced. Similar provisions apply in certain grave offences committed by unknown persons.
simpleExplanation: Courts may preserve evidence when an accused is absconding so that important testimony is not lost.
examples: A witness's testimony is recorded while the accused remains absconding.
The deposition is later used after the accused is arrested.

faqs.question: Can evidence be recorded before the accused is arrested?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the accused has absconded and arrest is not immediately likely.

title: Evidence of Public Servants, Experts and Police Officers in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXV - Evidence in Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a document or report prepared by a public servant, scientific expert or medical officer is to be used in evidence and such person is unavailable due to transfer, retirement, death, inability, or delay concerns, the Court shall secure the presence of the successor officer holding the post. Such deposition may also be given through audio-video electronic means.
simpleExplanation: Successor officers may testify regarding official reports when the original author is unavailable.
examples: A successor forensic officer testifies regarding a laboratory report prepared by a retired expert.
Evidence is given through video conferencing.

faqs.question: Can a successor officer testify about an official report?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may require the successor officer to give evidence.

title: Person Once Convicted or Acquitted Not to Be Tried for Same Offence
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A person who has been tried by a competent Court and convicted or acquitted shall not be tried again for the same offence while that conviction or acquittal remains in force, subject to specified exceptions. A discharge is not an acquittal for the purposes of this section.
simpleExplanation: A person generally cannot be tried twice for the same offence, reflecting the principle of double jeopardy.
examples: A person acquitted of theft cannot be tried again for the same theft.
A person convicted of causing hurt may later be tried for culpable homicide if the victim later dies.

faqs.question: Can someone be tried twice for the same offence?
faqs.answer: Generally no, except in limited circumstances specified by law.

title: Appearance by Public Prosecutors
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor may appear and plead without written authority in any Court where the case is under inquiry, trial, or appeal. Private advocates instructed by complainants act under the directions of the Public Prosecutor and may submit written arguments with permission of the Court.
simpleExplanation: Public Prosecutors have authority to conduct prosecutions without requiring separate authorization.
examples: A Public Prosecutor conducts a criminal trial without filing a separate authority letter.
A private advocate assists under the supervision of the Public Prosecutor.

faqs.question: Can a Public Prosecutor appear without written authorization?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law expressly permits it.

title: Permission to Conduct Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A Magistrate may permit prosecution to be conducted by any person other than a police officer below the rank of Inspector. A police officer who participated in the investigation shall not be permitted to conduct the prosecution. The prosecution may be conducted personally or through an advocate.
simpleExplanation: The Court may allow private persons to conduct prosecutions, subject to safeguards ensuring fairness.
examples: A complainant is permitted to conduct the prosecution through an advocate.
An investigating officer is barred from conducting the prosecution.

faqs.question: Can the investigating officer conduct the prosecution?
faqs.answer: No. An officer involved in the investigation cannot prosecute the same case.

title: Right of Person Against Whom Proceedings Are Instituted to Be Defended
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Any person accused of an offence before a Criminal Court, or against whom proceedings are instituted under this Sanhita, may of right be defended by an advocate of his choice.
simpleExplanation: Every accused person has the fundamental right to be represented by a lawyer of their choice.
examples: An accused appoints a private advocate to represent him during trial.
A person facing criminal proceedings exercises the right to legal representation.

faqs.question: Can an accused choose their own lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accused has a legal right to be defended by an advocate of their choice.

title: Legal Aid to Accused at State Expense in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where, in a trial or appeal before a Court, the accused is not represented by an advocate and appears unable to engage one due to insufficient means, the Court shall assign an advocate for his defence at the expense of the State. The High Court may frame rules regarding selection of advocates, facilities to be provided, and fees payable by the Government.
simpleExplanation: An accused who cannot afford a lawyer is entitled to free legal representation at State expense.
examples: A financially disadvantaged accused is provided a legal aid lawyer.
The Court appoints an advocate at government expense.

faqs.question: Can an accused get a free lawyer?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the accused lacks sufficient means, the Court must provide legal aid.

title: Procedure When Corporation or Registered Society is an Accused
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A corporation, including a registered society, may appoint a representative for purposes of an inquiry or trial. Any act required to be done in the presence of the accused may be performed in the presence of the representative. The Court shall presume proper appointment upon filing of a written statement by the managing director or authorised person unless proved otherwise.
simpleExplanation: Companies and registered societies may participate in criminal proceedings through authorised representatives.
examples: A company appoints its legal manager to appear before the Court.
A registered society is represented through an authorised officer.

faqs.question: Can a company appear through a representative?
faqs.answer: Yes. A corporation may appoint a representative for the inquiry or trial.

title: Tender of Pardon to Accomplice
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Chief Judicial Magistrate or Magistrate of the First Class may tender a pardon to a person involved in certain serious offences on condition that the person makes a full and true disclosure of all facts relating to the offence and all persons concerned. The Magistrate shall record reasons and the acceptance or rejection of the pardon.
simpleExplanation: A participant in a crime may receive a pardon in exchange for fully cooperating with the investigation and trial.
punishment: Pardon from prosecution subject to compliance with conditions.
examples: An accomplice agrees to testify against the main accused in exchange for a pardon.
A co-conspirator provides complete details of the offence.

faqs.question: Who can receive a pardon?
faqs.answer: A person directly or indirectly involved in certain serious offences who agrees to make a full disclosure.

title: Power to Direct Tender of Pardon
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: After commitment of a case and before judgment, the Court to which the case is committed may tender a pardon to a person believed to be directly or indirectly concerned in the offence, on the same conditions applicable under section 343.
simpleExplanation: Even after a case reaches the trial court, the court may grant pardon to obtain important evidence.
punishment: Pardon subject to compliance with conditions.
examples: A Sessions Court grants pardon to a conspirator who agrees to testify.

faqs.question: Can a pardon be granted during trial?
faqs.answer: Yes. The trial court may grant pardon before judgment.

title: Trial of Person Not Complying with Conditions of Pardon
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a person who accepted a pardon fails to comply with its conditions by concealing material facts or giving false evidence, such person may be tried for the original offence and for giving false evidence. The prosecution must prove non-compliance if the accused claims compliance.
simpleExplanation: An accomplice who breaks the conditions of a pardon can lose its protection and be prosecuted.
punishment: Trial for the original offence and for giving false evidence.
examples: An approver conceals facts during testimony and is later prosecuted.
A witness who accepted pardon gives false evidence and loses immunity.

faqs.question: What happens if an approver lies in court?
faqs.answer: The pardon may be withdrawn and the person can be prosecuted.

title: Power to Postpone or Adjourn Proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Proceedings shall ordinarily continue from day to day until witnesses in attendance are examined. Adjournments may be granted only for recorded reasons. Generally, not more than two adjournments shall be granted due to circumstances beyond the control of a party. Engagement of an advocate in another court is not a valid ground for adjournment.
simpleExplanation: Courts should avoid unnecessary delays and ensure speedy trials.
examples: The Court refuses adjournment because counsel is busy in another court.
A trial proceeds on consecutive days until witnesses are examined.

faqs.question: How many adjournments can generally be granted?
faqs.answer: Normally not more than two adjournments for reasons beyond a party's control.

title: Local Inspection
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A Judge or Magistrate may visit and inspect any place where an offence is alleged to have been committed or any other relevant place. A memorandum of relevant observations shall be recorded and form part of the case record.
simpleExplanation: The Court may personally inspect a location to better understand the evidence.
examples: A Magistrate visits the scene of a road accident.
A Judge inspects the location where an alleged assault occurred.

faqs.question: Can a Judge inspect the crime scene?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may conduct a local inspection when necessary.

title: Power to Summon Material Witness or Examine Person Present
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Court may summon any person as a witness, examine any person present though not summoned, or recall and re-examine any witness if such evidence appears essential for a just decision of the case.
simpleExplanation: Courts have broad powers to call or recall witnesses whenever necessary to discover the truth.
examples: The Court recalls a witness for clarification.
A bystander present in court is examined as a witness.

faqs.question: Can a Court recall a witness?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may recall and re-examine witnesses whenever necessary.

title: Power of Magistrate to Order Person to Give Specimen Signatures or Handwriting, etc.
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: A Magistrate of the First Class may order any person, including an accused, to provide specimen signatures, fingerprints, handwriting, or voice samples where necessary for an investigation or proceeding. Such order may be made even without arrest if reasons are recorded.
simpleExplanation: Courts may require individuals to provide identifying samples for comparison and investigation.
punishment: Non-compliance may attract legal consequences under applicable law.
examples: An accused provides handwriting samples for forensic comparison.
A voice sample is collected for investigation.

faqs.question: Can a Magistrate order a voice sample?
faqs.answer: Yes. Voice samples, fingerprints, handwriting, and signatures may be ordered.

title: Expenses of Complainants and Witnesses
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Subject to rules made by the State Government, a Criminal Court may order payment by the Government of reasonable expenses incurred by complainants or witnesses attending an inquiry, trial, or proceeding under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses and complainants may be reimbursed for reasonable expenses incurred in attending court.
examples: A witness receives travel expenses for attending court.
A complainant is reimbursed for necessary attendance costs.

faqs.question: Can witnesses claim expenses for attending court?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may direct payment of reasonable expenses.

title: Power to Examine Accused
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Court may question the accused at any stage of the inquiry or trial and shall question him generally on the case after prosecution evidence is completed and before the defence is called. No oath shall be administered. The accused is not liable to punishment for refusing to answer or for giving false answers. The Court may take assistance from the prosecutor and defence counsel in preparing questions and may permit a written statement by the accused.
simpleExplanation: The Court must give the accused an opportunity to explain circumstances appearing in the evidence against him.
punishment: No punishment for refusing to answer or giving false answers under this section.
examples: After prosecution witnesses finish testifying, the Judge asks the accused to explain certain incriminating evidence.
The accused submits a written statement instead of answering orally.

faqs.question: Is the accused examined under oath?
faqs.answer: No. No oath is administered during examination under this section.

title: Oral Arguments and Memorandum of Arguments
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: After closing evidence, parties may make concise oral arguments and may submit a memorandum of arguments before concluding oral submissions. A copy shall be furnished to the opposite party. Adjournments for filing written arguments shall not ordinarily be granted.
simpleExplanation: Parties may present oral and written arguments summarising their case before judgment.
examples: The defence submits a written note summarising legal precedents.
The prosecutor presents concise oral arguments.

faqs.question: Can written arguments be filed?
faqs.answer: Yes. A memorandum of arguments may be submitted to the Court.

title: Accused Person to be Competent Witness
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: An accused person is a competent witness for the defence and may give evidence on oath in disproof of charges. However, he can be examined only upon his own written request, and his failure to testify shall not create any adverse presumption against him.
simpleExplanation: The accused may testify in his own defence but cannot be compelled to do so.
examples: An accused voluntarily enters the witness box to explain his actions.
The Court does not draw adverse conclusions because the accused chooses not to testify.

faqs.question: Can an accused be forced to testify?
faqs.answer: No. Testifying is voluntary and requires the accused's written request.

title: No Influence to be Used to Induce Disclosure
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Except as provided in sections 343 and 344 relating to pardon, no influence, promise, threat, or inducement shall be used upon an accused person to make him disclose or withhold any matter within his knowledge.
simpleExplanation: An accused cannot be pressured or induced to make disclosures except in legally permitted situations.
examples: Police cannot threaten an accused to force disclosure of information.
Promises of benefit cannot be used to obtain statements outside lawful procedures.

faqs.question: Can an accused be threatened into giving information?
faqs.answer: No. Threats or inducements are prohibited.

title: Provision for Inquiries and Trial Being Held in Absence of Accused in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where the Court is satisfied that personal attendance of the accused is not necessary in the interests of justice, or where the accused persistently disturbs proceedings, it may dispense with attendance if the accused is represented by an advocate. Attendance through audio-video electronic means is treated as personal attendance.
simpleExplanation: The Court may continue proceedings without the accused being physically present in certain situations.
examples: An accused participates through video conferencing.
A disruptive accused is exempted from attending while represented by counsel.

faqs.question: Can a trial proceed without the accused being physically present?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specified circumstances and usually when represented by an advocate.

title: Inquiry, Trial or Judgment in Absentia of Proclaimed Offender
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a proclaimed offender absconds to evade trial and there is no immediate prospect of arrest, the Court may proceed with the trial and pronounce judgment in his absence after following prescribed safeguards, including publication of notices and issuance of warrants.
simpleExplanation: A proclaimed offender who deliberately avoids trial may be tried in absentia.
punishment: The offender remains liable for punishment upon conviction despite absence.
examples: A proclaimed offender living abroad is tried after repeated failure to appear.
The Court records evidence and pronounces judgment in the offender's absence.

faqs.question: Can a proclaimed offender be tried without being present?
faqs.answer: Yes, after complying with the safeguards laid down in this section.

title: Procedure Where Accused Does Not Understand Proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where an accused, though not of unsound mind, cannot be made to understand the proceedings, the Court may continue the inquiry or trial. If conviction results before a Court other than the High Court, the proceedings shall be submitted to the High Court for appropriate orders.
simpleExplanation: Special safeguards apply when an accused cannot understand court proceedings.
examples: An accused with severe communication difficulties is tried, subject to High Court review if convicted.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused cannot understand the proceedings?
faqs.answer: The case may proceed, but a conviction requires review by the High Court.

title: Power to Proceed Against Other Persons Appearing to be Guilty of Offence
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where evidence during inquiry or trial indicates that a person not already an accused has committed an offence for which he could be tried together with the accused, the Court may summon, arrest, or detain such person and proceed against him. Proceedings against that person shall commence afresh.
simpleExplanation: The Court may add a new accused if evidence reveals their involvement in the offence.
examples: A witness implicates another participant who was not originally charged.
The Court summons a newly identified conspirator.

faqs.question: Can a Court add a new accused during trial?
faqs.answer: Yes. If evidence shows involvement in the offence, the Court may proceed against that person.

title: Compounding of Offences
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Certain offences specified in the Tables under this section may be compounded by designated persons, either with or without permission of the Court. Compounding results in acquittal of the accused. No offence may be compounded except as provided in this section.
simpleExplanation: For specified offences, the victim and accused may settle the matter through legal compromise, resulting in acquittal.
punishment: Composition results in acquittal of the accused.
examples: A victim of simple hurt agrees to compound the offence.
A defamation case is settled with permission of the Court.

faqs.question: What is compounding of an offence?
faqs.answer: It is a lawful settlement between parties that results in acquittal of the accused.

title: Withdrawal from Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: The Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor may, with the consent of the Court and before judgment is pronounced, withdraw from prosecution of any person. If withdrawal occurs before charge, the accused is discharged; if after charge, the accused is acquitted. The victim must be given an opportunity to be heard before withdrawal is allowed.
simpleExplanation: A prosecutor may withdraw a criminal case with Court approval, subject to statutory safeguards.
punishment: Results in discharge or acquittal depending on the stage of proceedings.
examples: The prosecutor withdraws the case before charges are framed and the accused is discharged.
The Court hears the victim before permitting withdrawal.

faqs.question: Can a prosecutor withdraw a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only with the consent of the Court and subject to legal conditions.

title: Procedure in Cases Which Magistrate Cannot Dispose Of
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If during an inquiry or trial a Magistrate finds that he lacks jurisdiction, that the case should be tried by another Magistrate, or that it should be tried by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, he shall stay the proceedings and submit the case with a report to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or other competent Magistrate. The receiving Magistrate may try the case, refer it to a subordinate Magistrate, or commit the accused for trial.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate who is not the proper authority to decide a case must transfer it to the appropriate court.
examples: A Magistrate discovers that the offence occurred outside his jurisdiction and transfers the case.
A case requiring trial before the Chief Judicial Magistrate is forwarded accordingly.

faqs.question: What happens if a Magistrate lacks jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate must stay proceedings and submit the case to the appropriate authority.

title: Procedure When Magistrate Finds Case Should Be Committed
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: If at any stage before judgment a Magistrate finds that the case ought to be tried by the Court of Session, he shall commit the case to that Court, and the provisions relating to commitment proceedings shall apply.
simpleExplanation: A Magistrate must send serious cases to the Sessions Court when required by law.
examples: A murder case initially before a Magistrate is committed to the Court of Session.
