faqs.question: When can a Magistrate commit a case to the Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: Whenever it appears that the case should be tried by the Court of Session.

title: Trial of Persons Previously Convicted of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Where a person previously convicted of certain serious offences relating to coinage, property or similar offences is again accused of such offences punishable with imprisonment of three years or more, the Magistrate may send the case to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or commit it to the Court of Session unless he is competent to impose an adequate sentence himself.
simpleExplanation: Cases involving repeat offenders for serious offences may be transferred to higher courts.
examples: A repeat property offender is committed to the Court of Session for trial.

faqs.question: Why are repeat offenders sometimes tried by higher courts?
faqs.answer: Because a more severe sentence may be required than the Magistrate can impose.

title: Procedure When Magistrate Cannot Pass Sentence Sufficiently Severe
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: (1) Whenever a Magistrate is of opinion, after hearing the evidence for the prosecution and the accused, that the accused is guilty, and that he ought to receive a punishment different in kind from, or more severe than, that which such Magistrate is empowered to inflict, or, being a Magistrate of the second class, is of opinion that the accused ought to be required to execute a bond or bail bond under section 125, he may record the opinion and submit his proceedings, and forward the accused, to the Chief Judicial Magistrate to whom he is subordinate. (2) When more accused persons than one are being tried together, and the Magistrate considers it necessary to proceed under sub-section (1) in regard to any of such accused, he shall forward all the accused who are, in his opinion, guilty to the Chief Judicial Magistrate. (3) The Chief Judicial Magistrate to whom the proceedings are submitted may, if he thinks fit, examine the parties and recall and examine any witness who has already given evidence in the case and may call for and take any further evidence and shall pass such judgment, sentence or order in the case as he thinks fit and is according to law.
simpleExplanation: If a Magistrate believes that an accused deserves a punishment that is beyond the Magistrate's legal powers to impose, the Magistrate must send the case to the Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM). The CJM can review the evidence, hear the parties again if necessary, take additional evidence, and then pass an appropriate judgment and sentence.
punishment: The section itself does not prescribe a punishment. It provides a procedure for referring cases to the Chief Judicial Magistrate when a more severe or different punishment may be required.
examples: A Second Class Magistrate finds an accused guilty of a serious offence but believes a longer prison sentence is necessary than he can legally impose. The case is forwarded to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
Several accused are tried together. One requires a sentence beyond the Magistrate's powers. All accused found guilty are forwarded to the Chief Judicial Magistrate for appropriate orders.
The Chief Judicial Magistrate recalls a witness and records additional evidence before deciding the final sentence.

faqs.question: Why is a case sent to the Chief Judicial Magistrate?
faqs.answer: Because the trial Magistrate believes the accused deserves a punishment beyond the Magistrate's legal sentencing powers.

faqs.question: Can the Chief Judicial Magistrate take additional evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Chief Judicial Magistrate may recall witnesses, examine parties, and take further evidence before passing judgment.

faqs.question: Does the accused have to be sent along with the case?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate forwards both the proceedings and the accused to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens when multiple accused are tried together?
faqs.answer: All accused whom the Magistrate considers guilty must generally be forwarded to the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

title: Conviction or Commitment on Evidence Partly Recorded by One Magistrate and Partly by Another
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Whenever any Judge or Magistrate, after having heard and recorded the whole or any part of the evidence in any inquiry or trial, ceases to exercise jurisdiction and is succeeded by another Judge or Magistrate having jurisdiction, the successor may act on the evidence recorded by the predecessor or partly recorded by the predecessor and partly by himself. The successor may re-summon and further examine any witness if necessary in the interests of justice. This section does not apply to summary trials or proceedings stayed under section 361 or submitted under section 364.
simpleExplanation: A new Judge or Magistrate can continue a case using evidence already recorded by the previous Judge or Magistrate, avoiding the need to start the trial again.
examples: A Magistrate is transferred and the successor continues the trial using existing evidence.
A successor Judge recalls a witness for clarification before delivering judgment.

faqs.question: Must a trial restart when a Judge is transferred?
faqs.answer: No. The successor Judge may continue on the basis of evidence already recorded.

title: Court to be Open
chapter: Chapter XXVI - General Provisions as to Inquiries and Trials
officialText: Criminal Courts shall ordinarily function as open courts accessible to the public. The presiding Judge or Magistrate may restrict access in appropriate cases. Trials relating to rape, specified sexual offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, and offences under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 shall be conducted in camera. Publication of proceedings in such cases is prohibited except with permission of the Court, subject to protection of the identity and address of the parties.
simpleExplanation: Criminal trials are generally open to the public, but sensitive sexual offence cases must be heard privately.
punishment: Unauthorized publication may attract legal consequences under applicable laws.
examples: A theft trial is conducted in an open courtroom.
A rape trial is conducted in camera with restricted public access.

faqs.question: Are all criminal trials open to the public?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, but sexual offence cases are usually conducted in camera.

title: Procedure in Case of Accused Being Person of Unsound Mind During Inquiry
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: When a Magistrate has reason to believe that a person against whom an inquiry is being held is of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence, the Magistrate shall arrange medical examination by the civil surgeon or another authorised medical officer. If necessary, the accused shall be referred to a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist. The Magistrate shall determine whether the accused is incapable of entering defence. If no prima facie case exists, the accused may be discharged. Otherwise, proceedings may be postponed for treatment. Where intellectual disability renders the accused incapable of entering defence, the inquiry shall be closed and the accused dealt with under section 369.
simpleExplanation: Special procedures apply when an accused person is mentally incapable of defending themselves during an inquiry.
examples: A Magistrate orders psychiatric evaluation of an accused who appears mentally incapable.
An inquiry is postponed while the accused receives treatment.

faqs.question: What happens if an accused cannot understand the inquiry proceedings?
faqs.answer: The Court must determine mental capacity and may postpone or close proceedings depending on the circumstances.

title: Procedure in Case of Person of Unsound Mind Tried Before Court
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: If during a trial the Magistrate or Court finds reason to believe that the accused is of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence, it shall determine that fact, obtain psychiatric or psychological assessment, and record findings. If no prima facie case exists, the accused may be discharged. If a prima facie case exists, the trial may be postponed for treatment. Where intellectual disability renders the accused incapable of entering defence, the trial shall not proceed and the accused shall be dealt with under section 369.
simpleExplanation: A trial cannot fairly continue if the accused is mentally incapable of defending themselves.
examples: A Sessions Court postpones a trial while the accused undergoes psychiatric treatment.
An accused with severe intellectual disability is not subjected to trial.

faqs.question: Can a trial continue if the accused is mentally incapable?
faqs.answer: No. The Court must first address the accused's mental condition as required by law.

title: Release of Person of Unsound Mind Pending Investigation or Trial
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: A person found incapable of entering defence due to unsoundness of mind or intellectual disability shall ordinarily be released on bail if appropriate treatment and supervision can be ensured. If bail is not appropriate, the Court may order placement in a suitable treatment facility in accordance with the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017. The Court may order discharge, supervised release, or transfer to a residential care facility depending on medical opinion and public safety considerations.
simpleExplanation: The law focuses on treatment, care and supervision rather than ordinary detention for mentally incapable accused persons.
examples: An accused is released to family members who undertake responsibility for treatment.
A mentally ill accused is admitted to a mental health facility.

faqs.question: Can a mentally ill accused be released on bail?
faqs.answer: Yes, if proper treatment and supervision arrangements are available.

title: Resumption of Inquiry or Trial
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: Where an inquiry or trial has been postponed under section 367 or 368, the Magistrate or Court may resume proceedings once the accused has ceased to be of unsound mind and is capable of making a defence. A certificate from the authorised officer regarding the accused's fitness may be received in evidence.
simpleExplanation: Proceedings may continue once the accused regains the mental capacity required to defend themselves.
examples: A criminal trial resumes after medical certification that the accused is fit to stand trial.
The Court accepts a medical certificate confirming recovery.

faqs.question: When can a postponed trial restart?
faqs.answer: When the accused is certified as capable of making a defence.

title: Resumption of Inquiry or Trial
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: (1) If, when the accused appears or is again brought before the Magistrate or Court, as the case may be, the Magistrate or Court considers him capable of making his defence, the inquiry or trial shall proceed. (2) If the Magistrate or Court considers the accused to be still incapable of making his defence, the Magistrate or Court shall act according to the provisions of section 367 or section 368, as the case may be, and if the accused is found to be of unsound mind and consequently incapable of making his defence, shall deal with such accused in accordance with the provisions of section 369.
simpleExplanation: When an accused who was previously found incapable of defending himself appears before the Court again, the Court must determine whether the person is now fit to participate in the proceedings.
examples: A mentally ill accused recovers after treatment and the trial resumes.
The Court finds the accused still incapable and continues treatment-related procedures.

faqs.question: Can a postponed trial resume after recovery?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the accused becomes capable of making a defence, the inquiry or trial proceeds.

title: When Accused Appears to Have Been of Sound Mind
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: When the accused appears to be of sound mind at the time of inquiry or trial, and the Magistrate is satisfied from the evidence that there is reason to believe that the accused committed an act which, if he had been of sound mind, would have been an offence, and that at the time of commission of the act he was, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing the nature of the act or that it was wrong or contrary to law, the Magistrate shall proceed with the case and, if necessary, commit him for trial before the Court of Session.
simpleExplanation: Even if an accused is mentally fit during trial, the Court must examine whether the accused was of unsound mind when the offence was committed.
examples: An accused who has recovered from mental illness claims insanity at the time of the offence.
The Court examines evidence relating to the accused's mental condition when the act occurred.

faqs.question: Does recovery from mental illness remove the insanity defence?
faqs.answer: No. The Court considers the mental condition at the time of the alleged offence.

title: Judgment of Acquittal on Ground of Unsoundness of Mind
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: Whenever any person is acquitted on the ground that, at the time at which he is alleged to have committed an offence, he was by reason of unsoundness of mind incapable of knowing the nature of the act or that it was wrong or contrary to law, the finding shall state specifically whether he committed the act or not.
simpleExplanation: When an accused is acquitted because of insanity, the Court must clearly state whether the accused actually committed the act.
examples: The Court records that the accused committed the act but was legally insane at the time.

faqs.question: Why must the Court state whether the act was committed?
faqs.answer: Because further custody or supervision decisions may depend on that finding.

title: Person Acquitted on Ground of Unsoundness of Mind to be Detained in Safe Custody
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: (1) Where the Court finds that the accused committed the act but is entitled to acquittal on the ground of unsoundness of mind, the Court may order the person to be detained in safe custody or delivered to a relative or friend. (2) Detention in a public mental health establishment shall comply with the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017. (3) Delivery to a relative or friend requires an application and security ensuring proper care, prevention of harm, and production for inspection when required. (4) The Court shall report the action taken to the State Government.
simpleExplanation: A person acquitted because of insanity may still be placed under supervision or treatment for safety reasons.
examples: An accused is placed in a mental health facility after acquittal on insanity grounds.
A family member takes responsibility for the person's care after providing security.

faqs.question: Can an acquitted person still be detained?
faqs.answer: Yes. Safe custody or supervised care may be ordered when necessary.

title: Power of State Government to Empower Officer in Charge to Discharge Functions
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: The State Government may empower the officer in charge of a jail where a person is confined under section 369 or section 374 to discharge all or any functions of the Inspector-General of Prisons under sections 376 and 377.
simpleExplanation: The State Government may delegate certain responsibilities concerning mentally ill detainees to jail authorities.
examples: A jail superintendent is authorised to perform duties otherwise assigned to the Inspector-General of Prisons.

faqs.question: Can the State Government delegate these functions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Delegation may be made to the officer in charge of the jail.

title: Procedure Where Prisoner of Unsound Mind is Reported Capable of Making His Defence
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: If a person detained under section 369 is certified by the Inspector-General of Prisons or the Mental Health Review Board as capable of making his defence, he shall be produced before the Magistrate or Court. The Court shall then proceed under section 371. Such certificate shall be receivable as evidence.
simpleExplanation: When medical authorities certify that a detained person has recovered sufficiently to defend himself, the Court may resume proceedings.
examples: A psychiatric review certifies the accused fit for trial and the case resumes.

faqs.question: Who decides whether the accused is fit to defend himself?
faqs.answer: The Inspector-General of Prisons or Mental Health Review Board may certify fitness.

title: Procedure Where Person of Unsound Mind Detained is Declared Fit to be Released
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: (1) Where a person detained under section 369 or section 374 is certified as fit to be released without danger to himself or others, the State Government may order release, continued detention, or transfer to a public mental health establishment. (2) The State Government may appoint a Commission consisting of a Judicial Officer and two Medical Officers to inquire into the person's mental condition and report whether release is appropriate.
simpleExplanation: The State Government determines whether a person who has recovered from mental illness may be safely released.
examples: A recovered detainee is released after a Commission recommends release.

faqs.question: Who decides whether a detained person should be released?
faqs.answer: The State Government, based on medical and judicial assessments.

title: Delivery of Person of Unsound Mind to Care of Relative or Friend
chapter: Chapter XXVII - Provisions as to Accused Persons of Unsound Mind
officialText: The State Government may deliver a person detained under section 369 or section 374 to a relative or friend upon application and furnishing of security. The relative or friend must ensure proper care, prevent harm, produce the person for inspection, and where required produce the accused before the Court. If the inspecting officer certifies that the accused is capable of making a defence, the Court shall proceed under section 371.
simpleExplanation: Family members or friends may take responsibility for a mentally ill accused subject to safeguards imposed by the State Government.
examples: A family member undertakes responsibility for the care of an accused suffering from mental illness.

faqs.question: Can a mentally ill accused be released to family members?
faqs.answer: Yes. Subject to security, supervision, and compliance with legal conditions.

title: Procedure in Cases Mentioned in Section 215
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When a Court considers it expedient in the interests of justice to inquire into offences referred to in section 215(1)(b), committed in relation to proceedings before it or documents produced before it, the Court may conduct a preliminary inquiry, record a finding, make a written complaint, send it to a competent Magistrate, require security for appearance of the accused, or commit the accused to custody where appropriate.
simpleExplanation: Courts may initiate proceedings for offences such as perjury, fabrication of evidence, or other offences affecting the administration of justice.
examples: A Court initiates proceedings against a witness suspected of giving false evidence.
A forged document produced during trial leads to a complaint under this section.

faqs.question: Can a Court initiate prosecution for perjury?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may file a complaint when it considers such action necessary in the interests of justice.

title: Appeal
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) Any person whose application for a complaint under section 379 has been refused, or against whom such a complaint has been made, may appeal to the superior Court. The superior Court may direct withdrawal of the complaint or direct that a complaint be made. (2) An order under this section, and subject thereto an order under section 379, shall be final and shall not be subject to revision.
simpleExplanation: A person aggrieved by a Court's decision to make or refuse a complaint under section 379 may appeal to the higher Court.
examples: A person appeals against a complaint filed for alleged perjury.
A superior Court directs that a complaint should be filed after reviewing the matter.

faqs.question: Can an order under section 379 be challenged?
faqs.answer: Yes. An appeal lies under section 380, but the appellate order is final.

title: Power to Order Costs
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: Any Court dealing with an application made to it for filing a complaint under section 379 or an appeal under section 380 shall have power to make such order as to costs as may be just.
simpleExplanation: The Court may award costs while deciding applications or appeals relating to offences affecting the administration of justice.
examples: The Court orders a party to pay costs for filing a frivolous application.
Costs are awarded to compensate a party who successfully defends an appeal.

faqs.question: Can the Court award costs in proceedings under sections 379 and 380?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may pass any order regarding costs that it considers just.

title: Procedure of Magistrate Taking Cognizance
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) A Magistrate to whom a complaint is made under section 379 or section 380 shall, notwithstanding anything contained in Chapter XVI, proceed, as far as may be, as if the case were instituted on a police report. (2) If an appeal is pending against the decision from which the matter arose, the Magistrate may adjourn the hearing until the appeal is decided.
simpleExplanation: Complaints filed under sections 379 and 380 are generally handled in the same manner as cases instituted on a police report.
examples: A Magistrate treats a perjury complaint similarly to a police-report case.
Proceedings are adjourned until the related appeal is decided.

faqs.question: Can the Magistrate postpone proceedings if an appeal is pending?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may adjourn the matter until the appeal is resolved.

title: Summary Procedure for Trial for Giving False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) Where a Court of Session or Magistrate of the First Class forms an opinion that a witness has knowingly or wilfully given or fabricated false evidence in a proceeding, and it is necessary in the interests of justice, the Court may take cognizance and try the offender summarily after giving an opportunity of being heard. The Court may sentence the offender to imprisonment up to three months, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both. (2) Summary trial procedure shall be followed as far as practicable. (3) The Court may alternatively make a complaint under section 379. (4) Proceedings shall be stayed if an appeal or revision against the underlying judgment is pending.
simpleExplanation: Courts can summarily punish persons who deliberately give false evidence during proceedings.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A witness intentionally lies under oath and is summarily prosecuted.
A forged statement is submitted as evidence and the Court initiates action.

faqs.question: Can a Court punish a witness for lying during trial?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may conduct a summary trial and impose punishment.

title: Procedure in Certain Cases of Contempt
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) When offences under sections 210, 213, 214, 215 or 267 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 are committed in the view or presence of a Court, the Court may detain the offender, take cognizance before rising on the same day, and after giving an opportunity of showing cause, sentence the offender to fine not exceeding one thousand rupees, and in default of payment, simple imprisonment up to one month. (2) The Court shall record the facts, statement, finding and sentence. (3) In cases under section 267 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the record shall specify the nature of the interruption or insult.
simpleExplanation: Courts may immediately deal with certain offences committed directly in their presence.
punishment: Fine up to ₹1,000 and in default simple imprisonment up to 1 month.
examples: A person intentionally insults the Court during proceedings.
A person disrupts court proceedings and is punished summarily.

faqs.question: Can a Court punish misconduct committed in its presence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may take immediate action under this section.

title: Procedure Where Court Considers Case Should Not Be Dealt With Under Section 384
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) Where the Court considers that imprisonment other than in default of fine or a higher fine should be imposed, or for any other reason the case should not be dealt with under section 384, it may record the facts and forward the case to a Magistrate having jurisdiction. The Court may require security for appearance or forward the accused in custody. (2) The Magistrate shall thereafter proceed as if the case were instituted on a police report.
simpleExplanation: Serious contempt-related offences may be referred to a Magistrate for regular trial.
punishment: Punishment depends on the offence proved before the Magistrate.
examples: A serious courtroom disruption is referred for a regular criminal trial.
The offender is sent in custody to the Magistrate.

faqs.question: What happens if the Court considers summary punishment insufficient?
faqs.answer: The matter may be referred to a Magistrate for a full trial.

title: When Registrar or Sub-Registrar to be Deemed a Civil Court
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When the State Government so directs, any Registrar or Sub-Registrar appointed under the Registration Act, 1908 shall be deemed to be a Civil Court within the meaning of sections 384 and 385.
simpleExplanation: Registrars and Sub-Registrars may be treated as Civil Courts for specific purposes relating to offences affecting administration of justice.
examples: A Sub-Registrar is treated as a Civil Court when dealing with certain contempt-related matters.

faqs.question: Can a Registrar be treated as a Civil Court?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the State Government issues a direction to that effect.

title: Discharge of Offender on Submission of Apology
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: When a Court has punished an offender under section 384 or forwarded the offender under section 385, the Court may discharge the offender or remit the punishment if the offender submits to the Court's order or makes an apology satisfactory to the Court.
simpleExplanation: A genuine apology or compliance with the Court's directions may result in discharge or remission of punishment.
punishment: Punishment may be remitted or discharged.
examples: An offender apologises sincerely for insulting the Court and is discharged.
The Court remits punishment after compliance with its directions.

faqs.question: Can an apology reduce punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may discharge the offender or remit punishment.

title: Imprisonment or Committal of Person Refusing to Answer or Produce Document
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: If a witness or person required to produce a document or thing refuses to answer lawful questions or produce the required document or thing without reasonable excuse, the Court may sentence him to simple imprisonment or commit him to custody for a term not exceeding seven days. If refusal continues, proceedings under section 384 or section 385 may be initiated.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses who refuse to cooperate with lawful court directions may be detained or punished.
punishment: Simple imprisonment or custody up to 7 days, with possible further action.
examples: A witness refuses to answer relevant questions without justification.
A person refuses to produce a document ordered by the Court.

faqs.question: Can a witness be jailed for refusing to answer questions?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may impose custody or imprisonment under this section.

title: Summary Procedure for Punishment for Non-Attendance by a Witness in Obedience to Summons
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) Where a witness who is legally bound to attend Court fails to appear without just excuse, departs before being lawfully discharged, or otherwise neglects to obey a summons, the Court may summarily try the witness and impose a fine not exceeding five hundred rupees. (2) The procedure for summary trials shall be followed as far as practicable.
simpleExplanation: Witnesses who ignore court summons without valid reasons may be fined.
examples: A witness ignores a court summons and fails to appear.
A witness leaves the courtroom before being excused.

faqs.question: What happens if a witness ignores a summons?
faqs.answer: The Court may impose a fine after following summary procedure.

title: Appeals from Convictions Under Sections 383, 384, 388 and 389
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: (1) Any person convicted under sections 383, 384, 388 or 389 by any Court other than a High Court may appeal to the Court to which decrees or orders of that Court are ordinarily appealable. (2) The appellate Court may alter or reverse the finding or sentence. (3) Appeals from convictions by a Court of Small Causes lie to the Court of Session. (4) Appeals from convictions by a Registrar or Sub-Registrar deemed to be a Civil Court lie to the Court of Session having jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: Persons convicted under these summary procedures have a right to appeal to a higher court.
examples: A witness fined for non-attendance files an appeal before the appropriate appellate court.
A person punished for false evidence challenges the conviction.

faqs.question: Can a person convicted under section 383 appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes. An appeal is specifically provided under this section.

title: Certain Judges and Magistrates Not to Try Certain Offences When Committed Before Themselves
chapter: Chapter XXVIII - Provisions as to Offences Affecting the Administration of Justice
officialText: Except as provided in sections 383, 384, 388 and 389, no Judge of a Criminal Court (other than a Judge of a High Court) or Magistrate shall try any person for any offence referred to in section 215, when such offence is committed before himself or in contempt of his authority, or is brought under his notice as such Judge or Magistrate in the course of a judicial proceeding.
simpleExplanation: A Judge or Magistrate generally cannot try a person for offences affecting the administration of justice that were committed before them, except in specific situations provided by law.
examples: A Magistrate cannot ordinarily try a person for perjury committed before him.
Special procedures under sections 383 and 384 may still apply.

faqs.question: Why can't the same Judge try such offences?
faqs.answer: To maintain impartiality and avoid conflict of interest.

title: Judgment
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Every judgment in a criminal trial shall be pronounced in open Court immediately after the termination of the trial or within forty-five days thereafter after due notice to the parties. The Court may deliver the whole judgment, read it out, or read only the operative part and explain its substance. Copies of judgments shall be made available free of cost and, as far as practicable, uploaded on the Court portal within seven days.
simpleExplanation: This section governs how and when criminal judgments must be pronounced and made available to the parties.
examples: A Sessions Court pronounces judgment 30 days after completion of trial.
The operative portion is read in open Court and the full judgment is uploaded online.

faqs.question: Within how many days should a judgment be pronounced?
faqs.answer: Normally immediately after trial or within 45 days.

faqs.question: Will parties receive a copy of the judgment?
faqs.answer: Yes. A copy must be made available free of cost.

title: Language and Contents of Judgment
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Every judgment shall be written in the language of the Court and contain the points for determination, the decision thereon, and the reasons for the decision. It shall specify the offence, the law under which the conviction is recorded, and the punishment imposed. In cases involving death sentence, special reasons for imposing such sentence must be recorded.
simpleExplanation: The section prescribes the mandatory contents of a criminal judgment.
examples: A judgment records the issues, findings, reasons and sentence.
A death sentence judgment includes special reasons justifying the punishment.

faqs.question: Must a Court explain its reasons for conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes. Reasons for every decision must be recorded.

faqs.question: Are special reasons required for death sentences?
faqs.answer: Yes. The judgment must record special reasons.

title: Order for Notifying Address of Previously Convicted Offender
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: When a person previously convicted of an offence punishable with imprisonment of three years or more is again convicted of a similar serious offence, the Court may direct that the person's residence and changes thereof be notified for a period not exceeding five years after release. The State Government may frame rules for implementation and penalties for breach.
simpleExplanation: Courts may require repeat offenders to keep authorities informed about their residence after release.
punishment: Violation of rules framed under this section may attract punishment prescribed by State Government rules.
examples: A repeat offender is required to notify authorities whenever he changes residence.
An appellate court confirms an address-notification order.

faqs.question: How long can the notification requirement continue?
faqs.answer: Up to five years after completion of the sentence.

title: Order to Pay Compensation
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: When imposing a sentence of fine, the Court may direct that all or part of the fine be used to defray prosecution expenses, compensate victims for loss or injury, compensate dependents of deceased victims, or reimburse bona fide purchasers of stolen property. Even where no fine forms part of the sentence, the Court may order the accused to pay compensation to the victim.
simpleExplanation: Courts can direct compensation to victims and affected persons as part of the sentencing process.
punishment: Compensation as determined by the Court.
examples: A fraud victim receives compensation ordered by the Court.
Dependents of a deceased victim receive compensation from the fine imposed.

faqs.question: Can compensation be awarded even when no fine is imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may independently order compensation.

title: Victim Compensation Scheme
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Every State Government, in coordination with the Central Government, shall prepare a Victim Compensation Scheme. District Legal Services Authorities and State Legal Services Authorities shall determine compensation. Victims may receive compensation even where the offender is not identified or where no trial takes place. Interim relief, medical aid and rehabilitation assistance may also be provided.
simpleExplanation: Victims of crime can receive financial assistance and rehabilitation support through a statutory compensation scheme.
examples: A victim receives compensation even though the offender was never identified.
Immediate medical assistance is provided under the scheme.

faqs.question: Can compensation be granted if the offender is not found?
faqs.answer: Yes. Victims may still receive compensation under the scheme.

title: Treatment of Victims
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: All public and private hospitals shall immediately provide free first-aid or medical treatment to victims of specified offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 and the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012, and shall immediately inform the police of the incident.
simpleExplanation: Hospitals must provide free emergency treatment to victims of certain serious offences without delay.
punishment: Liability may arise under applicable laws for failure to comply.
examples: A sexual assault victim receives immediate free treatment at a private hospital.
The hospital informs the police after providing emergency care.

faqs.question: Can a hospital refuse treatment until police formalities are completed?
faqs.answer: No. Immediate treatment must be provided first.

title: Witness Protection Scheme
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Every State Government shall prepare and notify a Witness Protection Scheme for the State with a view to ensure protection of witnesses.
simpleExplanation: States must establish witness protection mechanisms to safeguard witnesses from threats and intimidation.
examples: A witness in an organised crime case receives protection under the State scheme.
Identity protection measures are provided to a vulnerable witness.

faqs.question: Who is responsible for creating a witness protection scheme?
faqs.answer: The State Government.

title: Compensation to Persons Groundlessly Arrested
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Where a person causes another person to be arrested without sufficient grounds, the Magistrate may award compensation up to one thousand rupees for loss of time and expenses. Such compensation may be recovered as a fine, and in default of payment, simple imprisonment up to thirty days may be imposed.
simpleExplanation: A person who causes another person's arrest without valid grounds may be ordered to compensate the wrongly arrested person.
punishment: Compensation up to ₹1,000 and, on default, simple imprisonment up to 30 days.
examples: A false complainant causes an innocent person to be arrested and is ordered to pay compensation.
Compensation is recovered as though it were a fine.

faqs.question: Can a wrongly arrested person receive compensation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may award compensation under this section.

title: Order to Pay Costs in Non-Cognizable Cases
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: When a complaint relating to a non-cognizable offence results in conviction, the Court may order the accused to pay the complainant's prosecution costs, including process fees, witness expenses and reasonable advocate's fees. In default of payment, simple imprisonment up to thirty days may be imposed. Such orders may also be passed by appellate or revisional courts.
simpleExplanation: A convicted person in a non-cognizable case may be required to reimburse the complainant's litigation expenses.
punishment: Payment of costs; in default, simple imprisonment up to 30 days.
examples: A convicted accused is ordered to reimburse the complainant's legal expenses.
The appellate court confirms an order directing payment of costs.

faqs.question: What costs can be recovered?
faqs.answer: Process fees, witness expenses and reasonable advocate's fees.

title: Order to Release on Probation of Good Conduct or After Admonition
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: When a person not under twenty-one years of age is convicted of an offence punishable with fine only or imprisonment up to seven years, or when a person under twenty-one years of age or a woman is convicted of an offence not punishable with death or imprisonment for life, and no previous conviction is proved, the Court may release the offender on probation of good conduct instead of sentencing him immediately. In suitable minor cases, the Court may also release the offender after due admonition. The Court may impose conditions and require execution of a bond or bail bond for a period not exceeding three years.
simpleExplanation: Instead of sending eligible first-time offenders to prison, the Court may release them on probation or after a warning, depending on the nature of the offence and the offender's circumstances.
punishment: Release on probation or after admonition instead of immediate punishment.
examples: A first-time offender convicted of a minor theft is released on probation for two years.
A young offender is released after receiving a formal warning from the Court.

faqs.question: Who can benefit from probation under this section?
faqs.answer: Eligible first-time offenders, young persons, and women convicted of certain offences.

faqs.question: Can probation be cancelled?
faqs.answer: Yes. If conditions are violated, the offender may be arrested and sentenced.

title: Special Reasons to Be Recorded in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Where a Court could have dealt with an accused under section 401, the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958, or laws relating to youthful offenders but chooses not to do so, it shall record special reasons in its judgment for not granting such benefit.
simpleExplanation: If a Court refuses probation or rehabilitation benefits that could legally have been granted, it must explain why.
examples: The Court records reasons for denying probation to a repeat offender.
A judgment explains why rehabilitation was considered inappropriate.

faqs.question: Must the Court justify refusing probation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Special reasons must be recorded.

title: Court Not to Alter Judgment
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: Save as otherwise provided by this Sanhita or any other law, no Court, after signing its judgment or final order disposing of a case, shall alter or review it except to correct a clerical or arithmetical error.
simpleExplanation: Once a judgment is signed, the Court generally cannot change it except to correct minor mistakes.
examples: A typing error in the judgment is corrected.
The Court cannot reconsider the merits of the case after signing the judgment.

faqs.question: Can a Court review its own criminal judgment?
faqs.answer: Generally no, except for clerical or arithmetic corrections.

title: Copy of Judgment to Be Given to Accused and Other Persons
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: An accused sentenced to imprisonment shall receive a free copy of the judgment immediately after pronouncement. Certified copies or translations shall be provided without delay. In death sentence cases, a certified copy shall be supplied free of cost whether requested or not. Persons affected by a judgment may also obtain copies upon application and payment of prescribed charges.
simpleExplanation: The law ensures that accused persons and affected individuals can obtain copies of judgments and orders.
examples: A convicted prisoner receives a free copy of the judgment.
An accused requests a translation of the judgment into his own language.

faqs.question: Does a prisoner get a free copy of the judgment?
faqs.answer: Yes. A copy must be provided free of cost.

title: Judgment When to Be Translated
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: The original judgment shall be filed with the record of proceedings and, where it is recorded in a language different from that of the Court, a translation into the language of the Court shall be added if required by either party.
simpleExplanation: Judgments written in another language may be translated into the language of the Court when requested.
examples: A judgment recorded in English is translated into the Court language upon request.

faqs.question: When is translation of a judgment required?
faqs.answer: When the original is in a different language and a party requests translation.

title: Court of Session to Send Copy of Finding and Sentence to District Magistrate
chapter: Chapter XXIX - The Judgment
officialText: In cases tried by the Court of Session or a Chief Judicial Magistrate, a copy of the finding and sentence, if any, shall be forwarded to the District Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the trial was held.
simpleExplanation: Important convictions and sentences must be communicated to the District Magistrate.
examples: A Sessions Court sends a copy of its conviction order to the District Magistrate.

faqs.question: Why is the District Magistrate informed?
faqs.answer: For administrative and legal record purposes.

title: Sentence of Death to Be Submitted by Court of Session for Confirmation
chapter: Chapter XXX - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: Whenever a Court of Session passes a sentence of death, the proceedings shall immediately be submitted to the High Court. The sentence cannot be executed unless confirmed by the High Court. The convicted person shall be committed to jail custody pending confirmation.
simpleExplanation: A death sentence passed by a Sessions Court is not final until the High Court confirms it.
examples: A murder convict sentenced to death awaits High Court confirmation.

faqs.question: Can a death sentence be executed immediately?
faqs.answer: No. It must first be confirmed by the High Court.

title: Power to Direct Further Inquiry or Additional Evidence
chapter: Chapter XXX - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: While considering a death sentence reference, the High Court may conduct further inquiry itself or direct the Court of Session to take additional evidence on any matter relating to the guilt or innocence of the convicted person.
simpleExplanation: The High Court can seek more evidence before deciding whether to confirm a death sentence.
examples: The High Court orders additional forensic evidence before confirming a death sentence.

faqs.question: Can the High Court seek additional evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. It may conduct or direct further inquiry.

title: Power of High Court to Confirm Sentence or Annul Conviction
chapter: Chapter XXX - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: In a death sentence reference, the High Court may confirm the sentence, impose another lawful sentence, annul the conviction, convict the accused of another offence, order a new trial, or acquit the accused. No confirmation shall be made until the appeal period has expired or any appeal filed has been disposed of.
simpleExplanation: The High Court has wide powers while reviewing a death sentence.
punishment: As determined by the High Court.
examples: The High Court commutes a death sentence to life imprisonment.
The High Court orders a retrial.

faqs.question: Can the High Court overturn a death sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. It may alter the sentence or even acquit the accused.

title: Confirmation or New Sentence to Be Signed by Two Judges
chapter: Chapter XXX - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: In every death sentence reference, the order confirming the sentence or passing a new sentence shall be made, passed and signed by at least two Judges of the High Court.
simpleExplanation: At least two High Court Judges must participate in decisions relating to confirmation of a death sentence.
examples: A Division Bench confirms a death sentence.

faqs.question: How many Judges must sign a death sentence confirmation?
faqs.answer: At least two High Court Judges.

title: Procedure in Case of Difference of Opinion
chapter: Chapter XXX - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: Where Judges hearing a death sentence reference are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be decided in accordance with the procedure prescribed in section 433.
simpleExplanation: If High Court Judges disagree equally, a special procedure is followed to resolve the matter.
examples: One Judge votes for acquittal and another for conviction, requiring reference under section 433.

faqs.question: What happens if Judges disagree?
faqs.answer: The matter is decided according to section 433.

title: Procedure in Cases Submitted to High Court for Confirmation
chapter: Chapter XXX - Submission of Death Sentences for Confirmation
officialText: After the High Court passes an order confirming, modifying or otherwise deciding a death sentence reference, its proper officer shall promptly send a certified copy of the order, physically or electronically, to the Court of Session.
simpleExplanation: The Sessions Court must be informed of the High Court's decision in a death sentence reference.
examples: The High Court electronically transmits its confirmation order to the Sessions Court.

faqs.question: How is the Sessions Court informed of the High Court's decision?
faqs.answer: Through a certified copy sent by the High Court.

title: No Appeal to Lie Unless Otherwise Provided
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: No appeal shall lie from any judgment or order of a Criminal Court except as provided by this Sanhita or any other law. A victim has the right to appeal against acquittal, conviction for a lesser offence, or inadequate compensation.
simpleExplanation: Appeals are allowed only when specifically provided by law. Victims are also granted certain appeal rights.
examples: A victim appeals against an acquittal.
A victim challenges compensation awarded by the trial court.

faqs.question: Can every criminal order be appealed?
faqs.answer: No. Appeals are available only where the law permits.

title: Appeal from Orders Requiring Security or Refusing Surety
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: A person ordered to furnish security for keeping peace or good behaviour under section 136, or a person aggrieved by refusal or rejection of a surety under section 140, may appeal to the Court of Session.
simpleExplanation: Persons affected by security or surety orders may challenge those orders before the Sessions Court.
examples: A person ordered to execute a bond for good behaviour files an appeal.

faqs.question: Which court hears such appeals?
faqs.answer: The Court of Session.

title: Appeals from Convictions
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: A person convicted by a High Court exercising extraordinary original criminal jurisdiction may appeal to the Supreme Court. Persons convicted by a Sessions Judge, Additional Sessions Judge, or in cases where a sentence exceeding seven years has been imposed may appeal to the High Court. Convictions by Magistrates and orders under sections 364 or 401 may be appealed before the Court of Session. Appeals relating to certain sexual offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 shall be disposed of within six months.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies which appellate court can hear appeals from different criminal convictions.
examples: A person sentenced to ten years imprisonment by a Sessions Court appeals to the High Court.
A person convicted by a Magistrate appeals to the Court of Session.

faqs.question: Where does a person convicted by a Magistrate appeal?
faqs.answer: To the Court of Session.

faqs.question: Where does a person convicted by a Sessions Court appeal?
faqs.answer: To the High Court.

title: No Appeal in Certain Cases When Accused Pleads Guilty
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Notwithstanding anything in section 415, where an accused person has pleaded guilty and has been convicted on such plea, there shall be no appeal if the conviction is by a High Court, or if the conviction is by a Court of Session or Magistrate of the first or second class, except as to the extent or legality of the sentence.
simpleExplanation: A person who voluntarily pleads guilty generally cannot appeal against the conviction itself, but may challenge the sentence imposed.
examples: An accused pleads guilty before a Magistrate and can appeal only against the sentence.
A conviction based on a guilty plea before the High Court cannot be appealed.

faqs.question: Can a person appeal after pleading guilty?
faqs.answer: Only regarding the legality or extent of the sentence, not the conviction itself.

title: No Appeal in Petty Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: No appeal shall lie in certain petty cases where only minor sentences of imprisonment or fine are imposed, subject to the limits specified for High Courts, Sessions Courts, Magistrates and summary trials. However, an appeal may lie if another form of punishment is combined with the sentence.
simpleExplanation: Very minor convictions carrying small fines or short imprisonment generally cannot be appealed.
examples: A person fined ₹100 by a Magistrate cannot ordinarily file an appeal.
A petty conviction with an additional punishment may still be appealable.

faqs.question: Why are petty cases not appealable?
faqs.answer: To avoid unnecessary appellate proceedings in trivial matters.

title: Appeal by State Government Against Sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: The State Government may direct the Public Prosecutor to appeal against a sentence on the ground of inadequacy. The Central Government may do so in cases investigated by agencies under Central Acts. The appellate court may enhance the sentence only after giving the accused an opportunity to be heard.
simpleExplanation: If the government believes a sentence is too lenient, it can appeal seeking a harsher punishment.
punishment: Possible enhancement of sentence upon appeal.
examples: The State appeals because a serious offender received an unusually light sentence.
The High Court increases the sentence after hearing the accused.

faqs.question: Can a sentence be increased on appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, after giving the accused a reasonable opportunity to be heard.

title: Appeal in Case of Acquittal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: The District Magistrate, State Government, Central Government or complainant in specified cases may appeal against an acquittal. Appeals to the High Court require leave of the High Court. A complainant may appeal only after obtaining special leave.
simpleExplanation: Certain authorities and complainants may challenge an acquittal before a higher court.
examples: The State Government appeals against an acquittal in a murder case.
A complainant obtains special leave to challenge an acquittal.

faqs.question: Can a private complainant appeal against acquittal?
faqs.answer: Yes, with special leave from the High Court.

title: Appeal Against Conviction by High Court in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Where the High Court reverses an acquittal and convicts the accused, sentencing him to death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for ten years or more, the accused may appeal to the Supreme Court.
simpleExplanation: An accused gets a right to appeal to the Supreme Court when the High Court overturns an acquittal and imposes a severe sentence.
examples: The High Court converts an acquittal into a life sentence, allowing appeal to the Supreme Court.

faqs.question: When can a person directly appeal to the Supreme Court under this section?
faqs.answer: When the High Court reverses an acquittal and imposes a severe sentence.

title: Special Right of Appeal in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Where several persons are convicted in one trial and an appealable judgment or order has been passed in respect of any of them, all or any of the convicted persons shall have a right of appeal.
simpleExplanation: When multiple people are convicted together, they may all benefit from the right of appeal if the judgment is appealable.
examples: Three co-accused convicted together file a joint appeal.

faqs.question: Can co-accused appeal together?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the judgment is appealable.

title: Appeal to Court of Session How Heard
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Appeals to the Court of Session shall be heard by the Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge. Appeals from convictions by a Magistrate of the second class may be heard by the Chief Judicial Magistrate. Cases may be assigned by the Sessions Judge or directed by the High Court.
simpleExplanation: This section specifies which judicial officers may hear appeals before the Sessions Court.
examples: A Sessions Judge assigns an appeal to an Additional Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: Who hears appeals in the Sessions Court?
faqs.answer: The Sessions Judge, Additional Sessions Judge, or in some cases the Chief Judicial Magistrate.

title: Petition of Appeal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Every appeal shall be made by a written petition presented by the appellant or his advocate and shall ordinarily be accompanied by a copy of the judgment or order appealed against.
simpleExplanation: Appeals must be filed in writing and should include the judgment being challenged.
examples: An advocate files a written appeal petition with a copy of the conviction order.

faqs.question: Must a copy of the judgment accompany an appeal?
faqs.answer: Ordinarily yes, unless the appellate court directs otherwise.

title: Procedure When Appellant in Jail
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: If the appellant is in jail, he may submit the petition of appeal and accompanying documents to the officer in charge of the jail, who shall forward them to the appropriate appellate court.
simpleExplanation: Prisoners can file appeals through jail authorities without personally approaching the court.
examples: A convicted prisoner submits his appeal papers to the jail superintendent.

faqs.question: How can a prisoner file an appeal?
faqs.answer: By giving the appeal petition to the officer in charge of the jail.

title: Summary Dismissal of Appeal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: After examining the appeal petition and judgment, the appellate court may summarily dismiss the appeal if there is no sufficient ground for interference. Before dismissal, the appellant must ordinarily be given an opportunity to be heard. The court may call for the record of the case and must record reasons when dismissing an appeal summarily.
simpleExplanation: An appellate court may reject a meritless appeal at the preliminary stage after following procedural safeguards.
examples: The appellate court dismisses a frivolous appeal after reviewing the petition.
The court records reasons before summarily dismissing an appeal.

faqs.question: Can an appeal be dismissed without a full hearing?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the court finds no sufficient grounds, subject to procedural safeguards.

title: Procedure for Hearing Appeals Not Dismissed Summarily
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: (1) If the Appellate Court does not dismiss the appeal summarily, it shall cause notice of the time and place at which such appeal will be heard to be given to the appellant or his advocate, the officer appointed by the State Government, the complainant in complaint cases, and the accused in appeals under sections 418 or 419, and shall furnish them with a copy of the grounds of appeal. (2) The Appellate Court shall send for the record of the case, if not already available, and hear the parties. If the appeal relates only to the extent or legality of the sentence, the Court may dispose of it without calling for the record. (3) Where the only ground of appeal is the alleged severity of the sentence, the appellant shall not urge any other ground except with the leave of the Court.
simpleExplanation: If an appeal is admitted, the Court must notify the relevant parties, obtain the case record if necessary, and hear the appeal before deciding it.
examples: A convicted person files an appeal and receives notice of the hearing date.
The Court decides a sentence-only appeal without calling for the complete trial record.

faqs.question: Will the complainant be informed about an appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, in complaint cases the complainant must receive notice.

title: Powers of Appellate Court
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: After examining the record and hearing the parties, the Appellate Court may dismiss the appeal; reverse an acquittal and order further inquiry, retrial, commitment for trial, or conviction; reverse a conviction and acquit or discharge the accused; order retrial; alter findings; alter the nature or extent of the sentence; enhance or reduce sentence where permitted by law; reverse or alter other orders; and pass consequential or incidental orders. No sentence shall be enhanced without giving the accused an opportunity to show cause.
simpleExplanation: The appellate court has broad powers to correct errors made by lower courts.
punishment: May result in enhancement, reduction, alteration, acquittal, or retrial depending on the appeal.
examples: The High Court overturns an acquittal and convicts the accused.
An appellate court orders a retrial because of procedural irregularities.

faqs.question: Can an appellate court increase a sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only after giving the accused an opportunity to be heard.

title: Judgments of Subordinate Appellate Court
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: The provisions of Chapter XXIX relating to judgments of criminal courts of original jurisdiction shall apply, so far as practicable, to judgments in appeal delivered by a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate. Unless otherwise directed, the accused need not be present when the appellate judgment is delivered.
simpleExplanation: Appeal judgments must generally follow the same standards as trial court judgments.
examples: A Sessions Court records reasons and findings while deciding an appeal.

faqs.question: Must the accused attend the appellate judgment?
faqs.answer: Normally no, unless the appellate court directs otherwise.

title: Order of High Court on Appeal to be Certified to Lower Court
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: (1) When the High Court decides an appeal, it shall certify its judgment or order to the court whose decision was appealed. Where necessary, the order shall be routed through the Chief Judicial Magistrate or District Magistrate. (2) The lower court shall make such orders as are conformable to the High Court's judgment and amend its records if required.
simpleExplanation: The High Court's appellate decision must be formally communicated to and implemented by the lower court.
examples: The trial court releases an accused after receiving the High Court's acquittal order.

faqs.question: Who implements the High Court's appellate order?
faqs.answer: The lower court whose order was appealed.

title: Suspension of Sentence Pending Appeal; Release of Appellant on Bail
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Pending an appeal by a convicted person, the Appellate Court may suspend execution of the sentence and release the appellant on bail, bond or bail bond. In cases involving death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment of ten years or more, the Public Prosecutor must be given an opportunity to oppose release. The High Court may exercise similar powers in appeals before subordinate courts. Certain convicted persons may also be temporarily released on bail to enable them to file appeals.
simpleExplanation: A convicted person may obtain suspension of sentence and release on bail while the appeal is pending.
punishment: Sentence remains suspended during the period ordered by the Court.
examples: A convicted person is granted bail while waiting for the High Court to hear his appeal.

faqs.question: Can a convicted person get bail after conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the appellate court grants suspension of sentence and bail.

title: Arrest of Accused in Appeal from Acquittal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: When an appeal against acquittal is presented under section 419, the High Court may issue a warrant directing that the accused be arrested and brought before it or a subordinate court. The court may commit him to prison pending disposal of the appeal or admit him to bail.
simpleExplanation: The High Court may secure the attendance of an acquitted person while hearing an appeal against acquittal.
examples: An acquitted accused is directed to appear before the High Court during an appeal.

faqs.question: Can an acquitted person be arrested during an appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the High Court issues a warrant.

title: Appellate Court May Take Further Evidence or Direct It to Be Taken
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: If the Appellate Court considers additional evidence necessary, it shall record reasons and may take such evidence itself or direct it to be taken by a Magistrate, Court of Session, or other competent court. The accused or his advocate has the right to be present when such evidence is recorded.
simpleExplanation: Additional evidence may be collected during an appeal when necessary for justice.
examples: The High Court directs fresh forensic evidence to be recorded.

faqs.question: Can new evidence be produced during an appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the appellate court considers it necessary.

title: Procedure Where Judges of Court of Appeal Are Equally Divided
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: When a High Court Bench hearing an appeal is equally divided in opinion, the case together with the differing opinions shall be laid before another Judge. The judgment shall follow the opinion of that Judge. If required, the appeal may be reheard by a larger Bench.
simpleExplanation: A mechanism is provided to resolve disagreements among High Court Judges hearing an appeal.
examples: Two Judges disagree and the case is referred to a third Judge.

faqs.question: What happens if High Court Judges disagree on an appeal?
faqs.answer: The matter is referred to another Judge or larger Bench.

title: Finality of Judgments and Orders on Appeal
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Judgments and orders passed by an Appellate Court upon an appeal shall be final except in cases provided under sections 418, 419, sub-section (4) of section 425, or Chapter XXXII. However, the Appellate Court may still hear appeals against acquittal or for enhancement of sentence arising out of the same case.
simpleExplanation: Most appellate decisions are final unless the law specifically permits further proceedings.
examples: An appellate judgment becomes final after disposal of the appeal.

faqs.question: Are appellate decisions final?
faqs.answer: Generally yes, subject to specific statutory exceptions.

title: Abatement of Appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXI - Appeals
officialText: Appeals under sections 418 and 419 abate on the death of the accused. Other appeals generally abate on the death of the appellant, except appeals against sentences of fine. In appeals against convictions involving death or imprisonment, near relatives may apply within thirty days to continue the appeal.
simpleExplanation: Most appeals end when the appellant dies, although close family members may continue certain appeals.
examples: The spouse of a deceased appellant seeks permission to continue the appeal.

faqs.question: Can relatives continue an appeal after the appellant's death?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specified cases and within the prescribed time.

title: Reference to High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: Where a court considers that the validity of an Act, Ordinance, Regulation, or provision thereof is necessary for disposal of a case and believes it to be invalid or inoperative, it shall refer the question to the High Court. A Court of Session may also refer important questions of law to the High Court. Pending the decision, the accused may be committed to jail or released on bail.
simpleExplanation: Certain constitutional or legal validity questions must be referred to the High Court for decision.
examples: A Sessions Court refers a constitutional validity question to the High Court.

faqs.question: When can a case be referred to the High Court?
faqs.answer: When determination of an important legal validity issue is necessary.

title: Disposal of Case According to Decision of High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: When a question has been referred under section 436, the High Court shall pass such order as it thinks fit and send a copy to the referring court, which shall dispose of the case in conformity with the High Court's decision. The High Court may also direct payment of costs.
simpleExplanation: The referring court must follow the High Court's ruling on the legal question.
examples: The trial court resumes proceedings after receiving the High Court's answer.

faqs.question: Must the lower court follow the High Court's decision?
faqs.answer: Yes, the case must be disposed of accordingly.

title: Calling for Records to Exercise Powers of Revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: The High Court or Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before an inferior criminal court to satisfy itself regarding the correctness, legality, propriety, or regularity of any finding, sentence, order, or proceeding. Execution of sentence may be suspended and the accused may be released on bond or bail pending examination. Revisional powers cannot be exercised against interlocutory orders.
simpleExplanation: Higher courts can review records of lower courts to correct legal or procedural errors.
examples: A Sessions Judge calls for records to examine legality of a conviction.

faqs.question: Can revision be filed against an interim order?
faqs.answer: No, revisional powers do not apply to interlocutory orders.

title: Power to Order Inquiry
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: On examining a record under section 438 or otherwise, the High Court or Sessions Judge may direct the Chief Judicial Magistrate or a subordinate Magistrate to make further inquiry into a complaint dismissed under section 226 or section 227(4), or into the case of an accused who has been discharged. No such direction shall be made without giving the discharged person an opportunity to show cause.
simpleExplanation: Higher courts may order further investigation or inquiry into cases that were dismissed or where an accused was discharged.
examples: The High Court orders further inquiry into a complaint that was dismissed.

faqs.question: Can a discharged accused be subjected to further inquiry?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only after being given an opportunity to be heard.

title: Sessions Judge's Powers of Revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: A Sessions Judge may exercise revisional powers similar to those of the High Court in proceedings whose records have been called for. Where revision proceedings are initiated before the Sessions Judge, his decision shall be final, and no further revision by the same person shall be entertained before the High Court or any other court.
simpleExplanation: The Sessions Judge has significant revision powers, and once such revision is decided, the same party cannot seek another revision on the same matter.
examples: A convicted person files a revision before the Sessions Judge and cannot later file another revision before the High Court on the same issue.

faqs.question: Can a person file revision before both the Sessions Judge and High Court?
faqs.answer: No. Once decided by one, another revision is generally barred.

title: Additional Sessions Judge's Powers of Revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: An Additional Sessions Judge shall have and may exercise all the powers of a Sessions Judge under this Chapter in respect of any case which may be transferred to him by or under any general or special order of the Sessions Judge.
simpleExplanation: An Additional Sessions Judge can exercise the same revision powers as a Sessions Judge for cases assigned to him.
examples: A revision petition transferred by the Sessions Judge is heard by an Additional Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: Can an Additional Sessions Judge exercise revisional powers?
faqs.answer: Yes, for cases transferred to him by the Sessions Judge.

title: High Court's Powers of Revision
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: The High Court may, in its discretion, exercise any of the powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 427, 430, 431 and 432, or on a Court of Session by section 344, in any proceeding whose record has been called for or which otherwise comes to its knowledge. No order prejudicial to the accused or another person shall be made without giving an opportunity of being heard. The High Court cannot convert an acquittal into a conviction in revision.
simpleExplanation: The High Court has extensive powers to review criminal proceedings and correct errors, subject to safeguards.
examples: The High Court modifies an illegal sentence through revision.
The Court refuses to convert an acquittal into a conviction during revision.

faqs.question: Can the High Court convict an acquitted person in revision?
faqs.answer: No. It cannot convert an acquittal into a conviction in revision proceedings.

title: Power of High Court to Withdraw or Transfer Revision Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: Where persons convicted at the same trial file revision applications before both the High Court and the Sessions Judge, the High Court may determine which Court should finally dispose of them and may transfer revision applications accordingly. Once a transferred revision is disposed of by the Sessions Judge, no further revision shall lie before the High Court at the instance of the same person.
simpleExplanation: The High Court can consolidate and transfer revision petitions to avoid conflicting decisions.
examples: Revision petitions arising from the same conviction are consolidated before the High Court.

faqs.question: Can revision cases be transferred between courts?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court may transfer them for efficient disposal.

title: Option of Court to Hear Parties
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: Except where otherwise expressly provided, no party has a right to be heard personally or by an advocate before a Court exercising revisional powers. However, the Court may, if it thinks fit, hear any party personally or through an advocate.
simpleExplanation: Parties do not automatically have a right to oral arguments in revision proceedings.
examples: The High Court decides a revision petition without oral hearing.
The Court permits counsel to argue a complex legal issue.

faqs.question: Do parties have a right to be heard in revision?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless the Court decides otherwise.

title: High Court's Order to Be Certified to Lower Court
chapter: Chapter XXXII - Reference and Revision
officialText: When a case is revised by the High Court or a Sessions Judge, the decision or order shall be certified to the Court whose finding, sentence or order was revised. That Court shall make orders conforming to the revisional decision and amend the record where necessary.
simpleExplanation: Lower courts must implement decisions made by revisional courts.
examples: A trial court updates its records after a revisional order.

faqs.question: Who implements a revisional order?
faqs.answer: The Court whose order was revised.

title: Power of Supreme Court to Transfer Cases and Appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: Whenever it appears to the Supreme Court that an order is expedient for the ends of justice, it may transfer any case or appeal from one High Court to another High Court or from a Criminal Court subordinate to one High Court to another Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction subordinate to another High Court. Frivolous or vexatious applications may result in compensation orders.
simpleExplanation: The Supreme Court can transfer criminal cases between States or High Courts to ensure justice.
punishment: Compensation may be imposed for frivolous applications.
examples: A sensitive criminal case is transferred to another State for a fair trial.

faqs.question: Who can request a transfer before the Supreme Court?
faqs.answer: The Attorney-General of India or an interested party.

title: Power of High Court to Transfer Cases and Appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: The High Court may transfer criminal cases or appeals where a fair and impartial inquiry or trial cannot be held, where difficult legal questions arise, or where transfer is required for convenience or the ends of justice. It may transfer cases to another competent court, commit them to a Court of Session, or try them itself.
simpleExplanation: The High Court can transfer criminal proceedings to ensure fairness and efficient justice.
punishment: Compensation may be awarded for frivolous transfer applications.
examples: A case is transferred because local influence may affect the trial.

faqs.question: Can the High Court transfer a criminal case to itself?
faqs.answer: Yes, when the circumstances justify such transfer.

title: Power of Sessions Judge to Transfer Cases and Appeals
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: Whenever it appears to a Sessions Judge that transfer is expedient for the ends of justice, he may transfer any case from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court within his sessions division. The procedural safeguards applicable to High Court transfers substantially apply.
simpleExplanation: A Sessions Judge may transfer cases within the sessions division when necessary.
punishment: Compensation up to ₹10,000 may be imposed for frivolous applications.
examples: A Sessions Judge transfers a case to another Magistrate within the district.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Judge transfer a criminal case?
faqs.answer: Yes, within the sessions division.

title: Withdrawal of Cases and Appeals by Sessions Judges
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: A Sessions Judge may withdraw or recall any case or appeal made over to a Chief Judicial Magistrate or Additional Sessions Judge. He may thereafter hear the matter himself or transfer it to another competent Court.
simpleExplanation: The Sessions Judge can reassign cases and appeals among courts under his supervision.
