examples: A Sessions Judge recalls a case from an Additional Sessions Judge.

faqs.question: Can a Sessions Judge take back an assigned case?
faqs.answer: Yes, under this section.

title: Withdrawal of Cases by Judicial Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: A Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw any case from a Magistrate subordinate to him and try it himself or transfer it to another competent Magistrate. A Judicial Magistrate may also recall a case previously made over by him.
simpleExplanation: Chief Judicial Magistrates and Judicial Magistrates can reassign certain cases under their authority.
examples: A Chief Judicial Magistrate transfers a case to another Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can a Chief Judicial Magistrate withdraw a case from a subordinate Magistrate?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Making Over or Withdrawal of Cases by Executive Magistrates
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: A District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may make over any proceeding started before him to a subordinate Magistrate, withdraw a case from such Magistrate, dispose of it himself, or refer it to another Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: Executive Magistrates can assign and reassign proceedings among subordinate Magistrates.
examples: A District Magistrate transfers a proceeding to a subordinate Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can a District Magistrate reassign proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Reasons to Be Recorded
chapter: Chapter XXXIII - Transfer of Criminal Cases
officialText: A Sessions Judge or Magistrate making an order under section 448, 449, 450 or 451 shall record the reasons for making such order.
simpleExplanation: Reasons must be documented whenever cases are transferred or withdrawn under these provisions.
examples: A Sessions Judge records reasons for transferring a criminal case.

faqs.question: Must reasons be recorded for transfer orders?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Execution of Order Passed Under Section 409
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a Court of Session receives an order of confirmation or other order of the High Court in a death sentence reference, it shall carry such order into effect by issuing a warrant or taking necessary steps.
simpleExplanation: The Sessions Court must implement the High Court's decision regarding a death sentence.
punishment: Execution of death sentence as confirmed by the High Court.
examples: The Sessions Court issues a warrant after receiving confirmation of a death sentence.

faqs.question: Who executes a confirmed death sentence?
faqs.answer: The Court of Session.

title: Execution of Sentence of Death Passed by High Court
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a sentence of death is passed by the High Court in appeal or revision, the Court of Session shall, on receiving the order, cause the sentence to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant.
simpleExplanation: The Sessions Court implements death sentences imposed directly by the High Court.
examples: The High Court imposes a death sentence and the Sessions Court executes the order.

faqs.question: Who executes a death sentence imposed by the High Court?
faqs.answer: The Court of Session.

title: Postponement of Execution of Sentence of Death in Case of Appeal to Supreme Court
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where an appeal lies to the Supreme Court, or where a certificate or special leave petition is intended to be sought, the High Court shall postpone execution of the death sentence until the appeal, certificate application, or special leave proceedings are concluded.
simpleExplanation: Death sentences cannot be executed while legal remedies before the Supreme Court remain available.
punishment: Execution stayed pending appeal or related proceedings.
examples: Execution is postponed while the convict seeks special leave to appeal.

faqs.question: Can a death sentence be executed while a Supreme Court appeal is pending?
faqs.answer: No.

title: Commutation of Sentence of Death on Pregnant Woman
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court shall commute the sentence to imprisonment for life.
simpleExplanation: The law prohibits execution of a pregnant woman and mandates conversion of the sentence to life imprisonment.
punishment: Imprisonment for life instead of death.
examples: A pregnant death-row convict receives commutation to life imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can a pregnant woman be executed?
faqs.answer: No. Her death sentence must be commuted to life imprisonment.

title: Power to Appoint Place of Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Except where otherwise provided by law, the State Government may direct where a person liable to imprisonment or custody shall be confined. A person confined in a civil jail may be removed to a criminal jail, and later returned subject to statutory conditions.
simpleExplanation: The State Government determines the place where prisoners are confined.
examples: A prisoner is transferred from a civil jail to a criminal jail.

faqs.question: Who decides where a prisoner is confined?
faqs.answer: The State Government.

title: Execution of Sentence of Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a person is sentenced to imprisonment, the Court shall forward a warrant to the jail and send the accused there unless already confined. If the accused is absent, a warrant for arrest shall be issued and the sentence shall commence from the date of arrest.
simpleExplanation: This section lays down how imprisonment sentences are formally executed.
examples: The Court issues a warrant committing a convicted person to prison.

faqs.question: When does the sentence begin if the accused is absent?
faqs.answer: From the date of arrest.

title: Direction of Warrant for Execution
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Every warrant for execution of a sentence of imprisonment shall be directed to the officer in charge of the jail or other place where the prisoner is or is to be confined.
simpleExplanation: The imprisonment warrant must be addressed to the authority responsible for custody of the prisoner.
examples: A warrant is addressed to the Superintendent of the Central Prison.

faqs.question: To whom is a prison warrant directed?
faqs.answer: The officer in charge of the jail or place of confinement.

title: Warrant with Whom to Be Lodged
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When the prisoner is to be confined in a jail, the warrant shall be lodged with the jailor.
simpleExplanation: The imprisonment warrant must be formally deposited with the jail authority.
examples: The Court forwards the warrant to the jailor before the prisoner is admitted.

faqs.question: Who receives the imprisonment warrant?
faqs.answer: The jailor of the prison where the prisoner is confined.

title: Warrant for Levy of Fine
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When an offender has been sentenced to pay a fine and payment has not been made, the Court may recover the amount by issuing a warrant for attachment and sale of movable property or by issuing a warrant to the Collector to recover it as arrears of land revenue from movable or immovable property. Special restrictions apply where imprisonment in default has already been undergone.
simpleExplanation: Courts can recover unpaid fines by attaching property or through revenue recovery proceedings.
punishment: Recovery of unpaid fine through legal enforcement measures.
examples: The Court attaches a vehicle belonging to a defaulter.
The Collector recovers the unpaid fine as arrears of land revenue.

faqs.question: Can property be seized to recover a fine?
faqs.answer: Yes. Movable property may be attached and sold.

title: Effect of Warrant for Levy of Fine
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A warrant issued under section 461 for attachment and sale of property may be executed within the Court's jurisdiction and may also be executed outside that jurisdiction if endorsed by the District Magistrate where the property is located.
simpleExplanation: A fine recovery warrant can be enforced outside the issuing Court's area after proper endorsement.
examples: Property located in another district is attached after endorsement by the District Magistrate.

faqs.question: Can a recovery warrant be executed outside the Court's district?
faqs.answer: Yes, with endorsement from the appropriate District Magistrate.

title: Warrant for Levy of Fine Issued by Court in Territory Where Sanhita Does Not Extend
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A warrant issued by a Criminal Court in a territory to which this Sanhita does not extend for recovery of a fine may be executed within territories where the Sanhita applies as if it were issued under section 461.
simpleExplanation: Fine recovery warrants from certain external territories may be enforced under BNSS procedures.
examples: A recovery warrant from a specified territory is executed through the local Collector.

faqs.question: Can external fine recovery warrants be enforced under BNSS?
faqs.answer: Yes, as provided by this section.

title: Suspension of Execution of Sentence of Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where an offender is sentenced to fine only with imprisonment in default of payment, the Court may allow payment within thirty days or in installments, suspend execution of imprisonment, and release the offender on bond. If payment is not made as directed, imprisonment may be enforced immediately.
simpleExplanation: The Court may grant time or installments to pay a fine before enforcing default imprisonment.
punishment: Imprisonment may follow if the fine remains unpaid.
examples: The Court allows a fine to be paid in three installments.
A person is released on bond pending payment of the fine.

faqs.question: Can a Court allow payment of a fine in installments?
faqs.answer: Yes, within the limits specified by law.

title: Who May Issue Warrant
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Every warrant for execution of a sentence may be issued either by the Judge or Magistrate who passed the sentence or by his successor in office.
simpleExplanation: A successor Judge or Magistrate can issue warrants to enforce sentences passed earlier.
examples: A newly appointed Magistrate issues a warrant on a previously decided case.

faqs.question: Can a successor Judge issue an execution warrant?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Sentence on Escaped Convict When to Take Effect
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Where a sentence is passed on an escaped convict, a death sentence, life imprisonment, or fine takes effect immediately. A term sentence may take effect immediately or after the remaining portion of the previous sentence depending on its severity.
simpleExplanation: This section determines how new sentences operate when imposed on escaped prisoners.
examples: An escaped convict receives a new life sentence that takes effect immediately.

faqs.question: When does a new sentence begin for an escaped convict?
faqs.answer: It depends on the type and severity of the new sentence.

title: Sentence on Offender Already Sentenced for Another Offence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a person already undergoing imprisonment is sentenced on a subsequent conviction, the new sentence ordinarily begins after completion of the previous sentence unless the Court directs concurrent running. Where the person is already serving life imprisonment, subsequent sentences generally run concurrently.
simpleExplanation: The Court decides whether multiple sentences run one after another or at the same time.
punishment: Concurrent or consecutive imprisonment as directed.
examples: A Court orders two sentences to run concurrently.
A second sentence begins after the first ends.

faqs.question: Can two prison sentences run together?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Court directs concurrent sentences.

title: Period of Detention Undergone by Accused to Be Set Off Against Sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The period of detention undergone by an accused during investigation, inquiry, or trial of the same case before conviction shall be set off against the term of imprisonment imposed upon conviction.
simpleExplanation: Time already spent in custody before conviction is deducted from the final prison sentence.
punishment: Reduction of remaining imprisonment term through set-off.
examples: Six months spent in judicial custody is deducted from a three-year sentence.

faqs.question: Does pre-trial detention count towards the sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is generally adjusted against the sentence.

title: Saving
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Nothing in sections 466 or 467 excuses a person from any punishment arising from previous or subsequent convictions. Imprisonment in default of payment of fine shall generally take effect after substantive sentences are completed.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies how multiple punishments and default imprisonment are to be enforced.
examples: Default imprisonment for non-payment of fine begins after substantive imprisonment ends.

faqs.question: Does a new conviction cancel an earlier punishment?
faqs.answer: No.

title: Return of Warrant on Execution of Sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: When a sentence has been fully executed, the officer executing it shall return the warrant to the Court that issued it with an endorsement certifying the manner in which the sentence was executed.
simpleExplanation: After a sentence is completed, the executing authority must report compliance to the Court.
examples: A prison superintendent returns the executed warrant to the Court.

faqs.question: What happens after a sentence is executed?
faqs.answer: The warrant is returned to the issuing Court with a completion certificate.

title: Money Ordered to Be Paid Recoverable as a Fine
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Any money, other than a fine, payable by virtue of an order made under this Sanhita, and the method of recovery of which is not otherwise expressly provided for, shall be recoverable as if it were a fine. In relation to orders under section 400, the provisions of section 461 shall apply as if the reference to compensation under section 395 also included an order for payment of costs under section 400.
simpleExplanation: When the BNSS requires payment of money and no separate recovery procedure exists, the amount can be recovered in the same way as a criminal fine.
punishment: Recovery proceedings similar to recovery of a fine.
examples: Costs ordered by a Court may be recovered using fine recovery procedures.
Compensation directed by a Court can be enforced through attachment proceedings.

faqs.question: Can Court-ordered costs be recovered like a fine?
faqs.answer: Yes. This section allows recovery of certain monetary orders as if they were fines.

title: Mercy Petition in Death Sentence Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: A convict sentenced to death, or his legal heir or relative, may file a mercy petition before the President under Article 72 or the Governor under Article 161 of the Constitution within the prescribed period. The section prescribes timelines for filing petitions, obtaining comments from State Governments, recommendations by the Central Government, consideration by the President, communication of decisions, and provides that the orders of the President or Governor shall be final and not subject to appeal.
simpleExplanation: This section establishes the complete procedure and timelines for mercy petitions in death sentence cases.
examples: A death-row convict files a mercy petition before the Governor.
After rejection by the Governor, a petition is submitted to the President.

faqs.question: Who can file a mercy petition?
faqs.answer: The convict, a legal heir, or another relative.

faqs.question: Can a President's decision on a mercy petition be appealed?
faqs.answer: No. The decision is final under this section.

title: Power to Suspend or Remit Sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The appropriate Government may, at any time, suspend the execution of a sentence or remit the whole or part of the punishment, with or without conditions accepted by the convict. The Government may seek the opinion of the trial Judge, impose conditions, cancel suspension or remission if conditions are violated, and issue rules governing such petitions. The section also defines the expression 'appropriate Government'.
simpleExplanation: The Central or State Government may reduce, suspend, or forgive a sentence, subject to legal conditions.
punishment: Sentence may be suspended, reduced, or remitted.
examples: A prisoner receives remission of part of his sentence for good conduct.
The Government suspends a sentence subject to compliance with conditions.

faqs.question: Who can suspend a criminal sentence?
faqs.answer: The appropriate Government, depending on the nature of the offence.

faqs.question: Can remission be cancelled?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the conditions attached to it are violated.

title: Power to Commute Sentence
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The appropriate Government may, without the consent of the convicted person, commute a sentence of death to imprisonment for life; imprisonment for life to imprisonment for a term not less than seven years; imprisonment of seven years or more to imprisonment of not less than three years; imprisonment of less than seven years to fine; and rigorous imprisonment to simple imprisonment for any term for which the offender could have been sentenced.
simpleExplanation: The Government may replace a punishment with a lesser form of punishment according to the rules specified in this section.
punishment: Conversion of a sentence into a lesser punishment.
examples: A death sentence is commuted to life imprisonment.
Rigorous imprisonment is converted into simple imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can a death sentence be commuted?
faqs.answer: Yes. It may be commuted to imprisonment for life.

title: Restriction on Powers of Remission or Commutation in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: Notwithstanding anything contained in section 473, where a sentence of imprisonment for life is imposed for an offence for which death is one of the punishments provided by law, or where a death sentence has been commuted to imprisonment for life under section 474, the offender shall not be released from prison unless he has served at least fourteen years of imprisonment.
simpleExplanation: Certain life convicts cannot be released before completing a minimum of fourteen years in prison.
punishment: Mandatory minimum imprisonment of fourteen years before release.
examples: A person whose death sentence is commuted to life imprisonment must serve at least fourteen years.
A life convict in a capital offence case cannot be released before fourteen years.

faqs.question: Can a life convict be released before fourteen years in these cases?
faqs.answer: No. A minimum of fourteen years of imprisonment is mandatory.

title: Concurrent Power of Central Government in Case of Death Sentences
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The powers conferred by sections 473 and 474 upon the State Government may, in the case of sentences of death, also be exercised by the Central Government.
simpleExplanation: In death sentence cases, both the State Government and the Central Government can exercise powers relating to suspension, remission, or commutation of sentences.
examples: The Central Government commutes a death sentence to life imprisonment.
A death sentence remission request is considered by the Central Government.

faqs.question: Can the Central Government commute a death sentence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Central Government has concurrent powers in death sentence cases.

title: State Government to Act After Concurrence with Central Government in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIV - Execution, Suspension, Remission and Commutation of Sentences
officialText: The State Government shall not exercise powers of remission or commutation in cases investigated by a Central agency, involving property of the Central Government, or involving offences committed by Central Government servants in discharge of official duties, except with the concurrence of the Central Government. Similar concurrence is required where concurrent sentences involve offences relating to matters within the executive power of the Union.
simpleExplanation: For certain cases connected to the Central Government, the State Government must obtain approval from the Central Government before granting remission or commutation.
examples: A case investigated by the CBI requires Central Government concurrence before remission.
A Central Government employee convicted for acts done in official duty receives remission only after Central approval.

faqs.question: When is Central Government concurrence required?
faqs.answer: When the case involves Central agencies, Central Government property, or acts done by Central Government servants in official duty.

title: In What Cases Bail to Be Taken
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Any person other than one accused of a non-bailable offence who is arrested or detained without warrant, or appears before a Court, and is prepared to furnish bail, shall be released on bail. If the person is indigent and unable to furnish sureties, he may be released on executing a bond. A person unable to furnish bail within one week of arrest may be presumed indigent.
simpleExplanation: Persons accused of bailable offences have a right to be released on bail, and poor persons may be released on personal bond.
examples: A person arrested for a bailable offence is released after furnishing bail.
An indigent accused is released on a personal bond without sureties.

faqs.question: Is bail a right in bailable offences?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bail must generally be granted.

title: Maximum Period for Which Undertrial Prisoner Can Be Detained
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: An undertrial prisoner accused of an offence not punishable with death or life imprisonment shall be released on bail upon undergoing detention extending up to one-half of the maximum imprisonment specified for the offence. A first-time offender may be released on bond after detention extending up to one-third of the maximum sentence. No person shall be detained beyond the maximum term prescribed for the offence. The benefit does not apply where multiple cases or offences are pending against the accused.
simpleExplanation: Long-term undertrial detention is restricted, and eligible prisoners must be released after serving a substantial portion of the possible sentence.
punishment: Release on bail or bond after prescribed detention period.
examples: A first-time offender accused of an offence punishable with six years imprisonment is released after spending two years in custody.
An undertrial is released after completing half of the maximum possible sentence period.

faqs.question: What is the BNSS half-sentence rule?
faqs.answer: An undertrial may be released after spending half of the maximum prescribed sentence in detention.

title: When Bail May Be Taken in Case of Non-Bailable Offence
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: A person accused of a non-bailable offence may be released on bail unless there are reasonable grounds to believe that he is guilty of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, or falls within specified categories of repeat offenders. Special consideration may be given to children, women, sick, or infirm persons. Conditions may be imposed to ensure attendance, prevent repetition of offences, and prevent tampering with evidence. Courts may also cancel bail if necessary.
simpleExplanation: Bail in non-bailable offences is discretionary and depends on the seriousness of the offence and the circumstances of the accused.
examples: A woman accused of a serious offence is granted bail due to special circumstances.
A Court cancels bail after the accused attempts to influence witnesses.

faqs.question: Can bail be granted in non-bailable offences?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on the facts and legal requirements of the case.

faqs.question: Can conditions be imposed while granting bail?
faqs.answer: Yes. Courts may impose conditions to protect the investigation and trial.

title: Bail to Require Accused to Appear Before Next Appellate Court
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) Before conclusion of the trial and before disposal of the appeal, the Court trying the offence or the Appellate Court, as the case may be, shall require the accused to execute a bond or bail bond to appear before the higher Court as and when such Court issues notice in respect of any appeal or petition filed against the judgment of the respective Court, and such bond shall remain in force for six months. (2) If the accused fails to appear, the bond shall stand forfeited and the procedure under section 491 shall apply.
simpleExplanation: Before a trial or appeal concludes, the accused must execute a bond to ensure future appearance before a higher court if required.
punishment: Forfeiture of bond and recovery proceedings under section 491 for non-appearance.
examples: A convicted person signs a bond to appear before the High Court if an appeal is filed.
An accused fails to appear after receiving notice from the appellate court, causing bond forfeiture.

faqs.question: How long does the bond remain valid?
faqs.answer: The bond remains in force for six months.

faqs.question: What happens if the accused does not appear?
faqs.answer: The bond is forfeited and proceedings under section 491 may be initiated.

title: Direction for Grant of Bail to Person Apprehending Arrest
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) When any person has reason to believe that he may be arrested on accusation of having committed a non-bailable offence, he may apply to the High Court or the Court of Session for a direction that in the event of such arrest he shall be released on bail. (2) The Court may impose conditions including cooperation with investigation, non-interference with witnesses, restriction on leaving India, and other conditions considered necessary. (3) If such person is arrested thereafter, he shall be released on bail in accordance with the Court's direction. (4) This section does not apply to offences under section 65 and section 70(2) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
simpleExplanation: This section provides for anticipatory bail, allowing a person to seek protection from arrest before being arrested.
examples: A person fearing arrest in a non-bailable case applies for anticipatory bail.
The Court grants anticipatory bail subject to cooperation with the investigation.

faqs.question: What is anticipatory bail?
faqs.answer: It is a direction that a person be released on bail if arrested in a specified case.

faqs.question: Can the Court impose conditions?
faqs.answer: Yes. Conditions may include cooperation with investigation and restrictions on travel.

title: Special Powers of High Court or Court of Session Regarding Bail
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) A High Court or Court of Session may direct that any person accused of an offence and in custody be released on bail and may impose appropriate conditions. It may also set aside or modify conditions imposed by a Magistrate. Notice to the Public Prosecutor is generally required in serious offences, including offences punishable with life imprisonment and specified offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. (2) The presence of the informant or authorised representative is mandatory during hearings relating to certain serious offences. (3) The High Court or Court of Session may direct that a person released on bail be arrested and committed to custody.
simpleExplanation: The High Court and Sessions Court have wide powers to grant, modify, or cancel bail.
punishment: Cancellation of bail and recommitment to custody where necessary.
examples: The High Court grants bail after a Magistrate refused it.
The Court cancels bail after finding misuse of liberty by the accused.

faqs.question: Can the High Court cancel bail already granted?
faqs.answer: Yes. It may order the accused to be arrested and taken into custody.

faqs.question: Can bail conditions imposed by a Magistrate be changed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court or Sessions Court may modify or remove them.

title: Amount of Bond and Reduction Thereof
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) The amount of every bond executed under this Chapter shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case and shall not be excessive. (2) The High Court or the Court of Session may direct that the bail required by a police officer or Magistrate be reduced.
simpleExplanation: Bail amounts must be reasonable and proportionate to the circumstances of the case.
examples: A Sessions Court reduces an excessively high bail amount fixed by a Magistrate.
The Court fixes bail based on the accused's circumstances.

faqs.question: Can a bail amount be reduced?
faqs.answer: Yes. The High Court or Sessions Court may reduce it.

faqs.question: Can bail be fixed at any amount?
faqs.answer: No. The amount must not be excessive.

title: Bond of Accused and Sureties
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) Before release on bond or bail bond, the accused shall execute a bond and, where required, one or more sufficient sureties shall execute a bail bond undertaking that the accused will appear at the specified time and place and continue to attend as directed. (2) Any conditions imposed for release on bail shall be incorporated in the bond. (3) The bond may also require appearance before the High Court, Court of Session, or other Court when called upon. (4) The Court may accept affidavits regarding the sufficiency and fitness of sureties or may conduct an inquiry itself or through a subordinate Magistrate.
simpleExplanation: This section governs execution of bonds and verification of sureties before release on bail.
punishment: Forfeiture proceedings may follow if bond conditions are violated.
examples: A surety signs a bail bond guaranteeing the accused's appearance in Court.
The Court verifies a surety's financial capacity through an affidavit.

faqs.question: Who can act as a surety?
faqs.answer: A person found fit and sufficient by the Court.

faqs.question: Can the Court verify the surety's credentials?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may accept affidavits or conduct an inquiry.

title: Declaration by Sureties
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Every person standing surety to an accused person for his release on bail shall make a declaration before the Court as to the number of persons to whom he has stood surety, including the accused, and shall provide all relevant particulars.
simpleExplanation: A surety must disclose how many people he is already standing surety for and provide relevant details to the Court.
examples: A person acting as surety informs the Court that he is already a surety in two other cases.
The Court verifies the details provided by a proposed surety.

faqs.question: Why must a surety make a declaration?
faqs.answer: To help the Court assess the adequacy and reliability of the surety.

title: Discharge from Custody
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) As soon as the bond or bail bond has been executed, the person for whose appearance it has been executed shall be released. Where he is in jail, the Court admitting him to bail shall issue an order of release to the officer in charge of the jail, who shall release him upon receipt of the order. (2) Nothing in this section, section 478 or section 480 requires release of a person who is liable to be detained for some other matter.
simpleExplanation: Once bail formalities are completed, the accused must be released unless he is required in another case.
examples: An accused is released immediately after furnishing bail.
A prisoner remains in custody because he is wanted in another criminal case.

faqs.question: Is release automatic after executing a bail bond?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless the person is lawfully detained for another matter.

title: Power to Order Sufficient Bail When That First Taken Is Insufficient
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: If, through mistake, fraud or otherwise, insufficient sureties have been accepted, or if they subsequently become insufficient, the Court may issue a warrant of arrest directing that the person released on bail be brought before it and may order him to furnish sufficient sureties. On failure to do so, he may be committed to jail.
simpleExplanation: If the Court later finds the sureties inadequate, it can require new sureties or send the accused back to jail.
punishment: Commitment to jail upon failure to furnish sufficient sureties.
examples: A surety is later found insolvent and the Court demands a replacement surety.
The accused is remanded to custody after failing to provide fresh sureties.

faqs.question: Can bail be reconsidered if sureties become insufficient?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may require fresh and adequate sureties.

title: Discharge of Sureties
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: (1) Any surety for a person released on bail may apply to a Magistrate for discharge of the bond. (2) The Magistrate shall issue a warrant for the arrest of the accused. (3) Upon the accused appearing or surrendering, the bond shall be discharged and the accused shall be required to furnish fresh sureties, failing which he may be committed to jail.
simpleExplanation: A surety can withdraw from responsibility, but the accused must then arrange new sureties.
punishment: Possible commitment to jail if fresh sureties are not furnished.
examples: A surety withdraws due to personal reasons and the accused provides a replacement.
The accused is jailed after failing to furnish new sureties.

faqs.question: Can a surety withdraw from a bail bond?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law allows a surety to seek discharge from the bond.

title: Deposit Instead of Recognizance
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: When any person is required to execute a bond or bail bond, the Court or officer may, except in the case of a bond for good behaviour, permit him to deposit a sum of money or Government promissory notes of an amount fixed by the Court or officer in lieu of executing such bond.
simpleExplanation: Instead of executing a bond with sureties, the Court may permit a cash or government securities deposit.
examples: An accused deposits cash instead of arranging sureties.
Government securities are accepted as security for release.

faqs.question: Can cash be deposited instead of a bond?
faqs.answer: Yes, if permitted by the Court.

title: Procedure When Bond Has Been Forfeited
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Where a bond under this Sanhita has been forfeited, the Court shall record the grounds of proof and may call upon the person bound by the bond to pay the penalty or show cause why it should not be paid. If sufficient cause is not shown, the penalty may be recovered as a fine. If recovery is not possible, a surety may be imprisoned in civil jail for up to six months. The Court may remit part of the penalty. The estate of a deceased surety is discharged from liability. Certified copies of judgments may be used as evidence against sureties in specified cases.
simpleExplanation: This section sets out the procedure and consequences when a bail bond or similar bond is broken.
punishment: Recovery of penalty; civil imprisonment up to six months for sureties in certain cases.
examples: An accused absconds and the bond amount is recovered from the surety.
The Court remits part of the bond penalty after considering circumstances.

faqs.question: What happens if a bail bond is forfeited?
faqs.answer: The Court may recover the penalty and take action against the surety.

title: Cancellation of Bond and Bail Bond
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: Where a bond or bail bond for appearance in a case is forfeited, both the accused's bond and the bonds of his sureties shall stand cancelled. The accused shall not thereafter be released on his own bond unless sufficient cause for the breach is shown. Fresh bonds and sureties may be required.
simpleExplanation: If a person violates bail conditions, existing bonds are cancelled and stricter conditions may be imposed.
punishment: Cancellation of bond and requirement of fresh security.
examples: An accused fails to appear and his bail bond is cancelled.
Fresh sureties are demanded before granting bail again.

faqs.question: Can an accused get bail again after bond forfeiture?
faqs.answer: Yes, but usually only upon execution of fresh bonds and sureties.

title: Procedure in Case of Insolvency or Death of Surety or When Bond Is Forfeited
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: When a surety becomes insolvent or dies, or when a bond is forfeited under section 491, the Court may require the accused to furnish fresh security in accordance with the original order. If fresh security is not furnished, the Court may proceed as though there has been a default in complying with the original order.
simpleExplanation: Fresh sureties may be required when existing sureties become unavailable or inadequate.
punishment: Possible detention or other action for failure to furnish fresh security.
examples: A surety dies and the accused is required to provide a new surety.
The Court takes action after the accused fails to furnish fresh security.

faqs.question: What happens if a surety dies?
faqs.answer: The Court may require the accused to furnish fresh security.

title: Bond Required from Child
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: When the person required to execute a bond is a child, the Court or officer may accept, in place of the child's bond, a bond executed solely by one or more sureties.
simpleExplanation: A child may not be required to execute a personal bond if sureties provide security on the child's behalf.
examples: Parents execute a bond on behalf of a child.

faqs.question: Can a child's bond be replaced by a surety bond?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Appeal from Orders Under Section 491
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: All orders passed under section 491 are appealable. Orders made by a Magistrate may be appealed to the Sessions Judge, and orders made by a Court of Session may be appealed to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from orders of that Court.
simpleExplanation: A person affected by a bond forfeiture order has a right to appeal.
examples: A surety appeals against a bond penalty order.

faqs.question: Can a bond forfeiture order be appealed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Power to Direct Levy of Amount Due on Certain Recognizances
chapter: Chapter XXXV - Provisions as to Bail and Bonds
officialText: The High Court or Court of Session may direct any Magistrate to levy the amount due on a bond for appearance or attendance before such High Court or Court of Session.
simpleExplanation: Higher courts can direct Magistrates to recover amounts payable under bonds.
punishment: Recovery of amount due under the bond.
examples: A Magistrate recovers a forfeited bond amount on directions from the High Court.

faqs.question: Can the High Court order recovery of a bond amount?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Order for Custody and Disposal of Property Pending Trial in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When property is produced before a Criminal Court or Magistrate during an investigation, inquiry or trial, the Court may make appropriate orders for its custody. Property subject to speedy decay or where expedient may be sold or otherwise disposed of. The Court shall prepare a statement of the property within fourteen days, take photographs or videography, and within thirty days order its disposal, destruction, confiscation or delivery as provided by law.
simpleExplanation: The Court manages seized property during criminal proceedings and may document and dispose of it when appropriate.
examples: Perishable goods seized in a case are sold before they spoil.
A vehicle involved in an offence is photographed and released.

faqs.question: Can seized property be disposed of before trial ends?
faqs.answer: Yes, in certain circumstances such as natural decay.

title: Order for Disposal of Property at Conclusion of Trial
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When an investigation, inquiry or trial concludes, the Court may order destruction, confiscation, delivery or other disposal of property or documents connected with the offence. Delivery may be subject to execution of a bond. Certain orders are not to be implemented for two months or until disposal of appeal unless exceptions apply.
simpleExplanation: After the case ends, the Court decides what should happen to seized property.
examples: Recovered stolen property is returned to its rightful owner.
Illegal items are confiscated and destroyed.

faqs.question: Can property be returned to its owner after trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the Court determines that the person is entitled to possession.

title: Payment to Innocent Purchaser of Money Found on Accused
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Where a convicted person is found guilty of theft or receiving stolen property and an innocent purchaser had bought the stolen property without knowledge of its origin, the Court may direct that money found with the convicted person be used to reimburse the purchaser up to the amount paid, provided the stolen property is restored to its rightful owner.
simpleExplanation: An innocent buyer of stolen goods may receive compensation from money recovered from the offender.
examples: A buyer unknowingly purchases stolen jewellery and later receives reimbursement.

faqs.question: Can an innocent purchaser recover his money?
faqs.answer: Yes, subject to the conditions in this section.

title: Appeal Against Orders Under Section 498 or Section 499
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Any person aggrieved by an order under section 498 or section 499 may appeal to the appropriate appellate court. The appellate court may stay the order, modify, alter, annul it, or make any further order that is just. Similar powers may be exercised by courts of appeal, confirmation or revision while dealing with the main case.
simpleExplanation: Persons affected by property disposal orders have a right to challenge those orders before a higher court.
examples: A property owner appeals against a confiscation order.
An appellate court modifies the trial court's disposal order.

faqs.question: Can a property disposal order be appealed?
faqs.answer: Yes.

title: Destruction of Libellous and Other Matter
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: On a conviction under section 294, section 295, or sub-sections (3) and (4) of section 356 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Court may order the destruction of all copies of the offending material in respect of which the conviction was recorded and which are in the custody of the Court or in the possession or control of the convicted person. Similarly, upon conviction under sections 274, 275, 276 or 277 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Court may order the destruction of the food, drink, drug or medical preparation involved in the offence.
simpleExplanation: Courts can order the destruction of unlawful publications, obscene material, or adulterated food, drugs and similar items connected with a conviction.
punishment: Destruction of the offending material or goods.
examples: Obscene publications seized during investigation are destroyed after conviction.
Adulterated medicines involved in an offence are ordered to be destroyed.

faqs.question: Can a Court destroy illegal publications after conviction?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Court may order destruction of all copies connected with the offence.

title: Power to Restore Possession of Immovable Property
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When a person is convicted of an offence involving criminal force, show of force or criminal intimidation resulting in dispossession of another person from immovable property, the Court may order restoration of possession to the dispossessed person and may evict any person wrongfully occupying the property. Such order shall ordinarily be made within one month of conviction and shall not prejudice any civil rights that may be established in a civil court.
simpleExplanation: A criminal court may restore possession of land or buildings to a person who was unlawfully dispossessed through force or intimidation.
punishment: Restoration of possession to the rightful occupant.
examples: A landowner forcibly evicted by criminals is restored to possession.
An appellate court orders restoration of property after upholding conviction.

faqs.question: Can a criminal court restore possession of land?
faqs.answer: Yes, where dispossession occurred through force, intimidation, or similar criminal acts.

title: Procedure by Police Upon Seizure of Property
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: When seizure of property by a police officer is reported to a Magistrate and the property is not produced before a Criminal Court during inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may make orders regarding its disposal, custody, production, or delivery to the person entitled to possession. If the rightful owner is unknown, the Magistrate may issue a proclamation requiring claimants to establish their claims within six months.
simpleExplanation: The Magistrate decides how seized property should be handled when it is not produced before a criminal court.
examples: A seized motorcycle is returned to its lawful owner.
A proclamation is issued seeking claimants for unclaimed property.

faqs.question: What happens if seized property has no known owner?
faqs.answer: The Magistrate issues a public proclamation inviting claims.

title: Procedure Where No Claimant Appears Within Six Months
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: If no person establishes a claim to seized property within six months and the person from whose possession it was seized cannot show lawful acquisition, the Magistrate may order that the property be placed at the disposal of the State Government. The property may be sold and the proceeds dealt with according to rules prescribed by the State Government. An appeal lies against such order.
simpleExplanation: Unclaimed property may become State property if nobody proves ownership within the prescribed period.
punishment: Transfer of property to the State Government.
examples: Unclaimed seized valuables are sold by the State Government.
A person fails to prove legal ownership and loses claim to the property.

faqs.question: What happens to unclaimed seized property?
faqs.answer: It may be transferred to the State Government and sold.

title: Power to Sell Perishable Property
chapter: Chapter XXXVI - Disposal of Property
officialText: Where property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or its sale would benefit the owner, or its value is less than ten thousand rupees, the Magistrate may direct its sale at any time. The provisions relating to claims and disposal shall apply, as far as practicable, to the proceeds of the sale.
simpleExplanation: Perishable or low-value property may be sold quickly to prevent loss of value.
examples: Perishable food items are sold before they spoil.
Low-value seized goods are sold and proceeds retained pending claims.

faqs.question: Can perishable goods be sold before ownership is decided?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Magistrate may order their sale to prevent loss.

title: Irregularities Which Do Not Vitiate Proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: Where a Magistrate, though not legally empowered, erroneously but in good faith performs certain acts specified in this section, including issuing search warrants, ordering investigations, holding inquests, taking cognizance, tendering pardons, recalling cases, or selling property, the proceedings shall not be set aside merely on the ground of lack of empowerment.
simpleExplanation: Certain procedural mistakes made in good faith by a Magistrate do not automatically invalidate proceedings.
examples: A good-faith error in issuing a search warrant does not invalidate the case.
A pardon tendered by mistake may remain valid if done in good faith.

faqs.question: Do all procedural mistakes invalidate proceedings?
faqs.answer: No. Some irregularities committed in good faith are protected.

title: Irregularities Which Vitiate Proceedings
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: If a Magistrate who is not legally empowered performs certain specified acts, including attaching property, demanding security, ordering maintenance, trying offenders, deciding appeals, or revising orders, the proceedings shall be void.
simpleExplanation: Some actions taken without legal authority are so serious that the resulting proceedings are invalid.
examples: A Magistrate without jurisdiction conducts a trial, making the proceedings void.
An unauthorized maintenance order is invalid.

faqs.question: When are proceedings considered void?
faqs.answer: When specified acts are performed by a Magistrate lacking legal authority.

title: Proceedings in Wrong Place
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No finding, sentence or order shall be set aside merely because the inquiry, trial or proceeding took place in the wrong sessions division, district, subdivision or local area unless the error has in fact occasioned a failure of justice.
simpleExplanation: A case is not invalid simply because it was heard in the wrong area unless real injustice resulted.
examples: A conviction remains valid despite a minor territorial jurisdiction error.
A retrial may be ordered if the error caused prejudice.

faqs.question: Does a wrong venue automatically invalidate a case?
faqs.answer: No. A failure of justice must be shown.

title: Non-Compliance with Provisions of Section 183 or Section 316
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: Where a confession or statement recorded under section 183 or section 316 is tendered in evidence and the Court finds procedural non-compliance, it may take evidence regarding such non-compliance and admit the statement if satisfied that the accused was not prejudiced and that the statement was genuinely made.
simpleExplanation: Minor procedural defects in recording confessions do not necessarily make them inadmissible.
examples: A confession remains admissible despite a technical recording defect.
The Court examines whether the accused suffered prejudice.

faqs.question: Can a confession still be admitted despite procedural defects?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the accused was not prejudiced and the statement was genuine.

title: Effect of Omission to Frame, or Absence of, or Error in Charge
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No finding, sentence or order shall be invalid merely because no charge was framed or because of any error, omission, irregularity or misjoinder in the charge unless a failure of justice has actually occurred. Appellate or revisional courts may order a fresh charge or retrial where necessary.
simpleExplanation: Errors relating to charges only matter if they actually prejudice the accused or cause injustice.
examples: A conviction is upheld despite a technical error in the charge.
A retrial is ordered where failure to frame a charge caused prejudice.

faqs.question: Does an error in a charge automatically invalidate a conviction?
faqs.answer: No. A failure of justice must be demonstrated.

title: Finding or Sentence When Reversible by Reason of Error, Omission or Irregularity
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No finding, sentence or order shall be reversed merely because of any error, omission or irregularity in proceedings, complaints, summons, warrants, sanctions or judgments unless such defect has caused a failure of justice. Courts must consider whether objections could have been raised earlier.
simpleExplanation: Technical mistakes generally do not invalidate proceedings unless they cause actual injustice.
examples: An appeal fails because the procedural defect caused no prejudice.
A sanction defect is ignored where justice was not affected.

faqs.question: What is the test applied by the Court?
faqs.answer: Whether the error resulted in a failure of justice.

title: Defect or Error Not to Make Attachment Unlawful
chapter: Chapter XXXVII - Irregular Proceedings
officialText: No attachment made under this Sanhita shall be deemed unlawful, nor shall any person making it be deemed a trespasser, merely because of any defect or want of form in the summons, conviction, writ of attachment or related proceedings.
simpleExplanation: Minor defects in attachment proceedings do not make the attachment illegal.
examples: An attachment remains valid despite a clerical defect in the writ.
An officer executing an attachment is protected from liability.

faqs.question: Can a technical defect invalidate an attachment?
faqs.answer: No. Minor defects do not make an attachment unlawful.

title: Definitions
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: For the purposes of this Chapter, unless the context otherwise requires, 'period of limitation' means the period specified in section 514 for taking cognizance of an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the term 'period of limitation' used in the limitation provisions.
examples: The limitation period is determined according to section 514.

faqs.question: What is meant by period of limitation?
faqs.answer: It is the statutory period within which a Court may take cognizance of an offence.

title: Bar to Taking Cognizance After Lapse of Period of Limitation
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: Except as otherwise provided, no Court shall take cognizance of specified categories of offences after the expiry of the limitation period. The limitation period is six months for offences punishable with fine only, one year for offences punishable with imprisonment up to one year, and three years for offences punishable with imprisonment exceeding one year but not exceeding three years.
simpleExplanation: Courts cannot ordinarily take cognizance of certain minor offences after the prescribed limitation period expires.
punishment: Bar on cognizance after expiry of limitation.
examples: A fine-only offence cannot ordinarily be prosecuted after six months.
A one-year limitation applies to certain minor imprisonment offences.

faqs.question: What is the limitation period for a fine-only offence?
faqs.answer: Six months.

title: Commencement of Period of Limitation
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: The period of limitation commences on the date of the offence, or where the offence was not known, on the first day it becomes known to the aggrieved person or police officer, whichever is earlier. Where the offender's identity is unknown, limitation begins on the first day the identity becomes known. The day from which the period is computed shall be excluded.
simpleExplanation: This section explains when the limitation period starts running for criminal offences.
examples: Limitation begins when a concealed offence is discovered.
The calculation starts when the offender's identity becomes known.

faqs.question: When does the limitation period begin?
faqs.answer: Usually on the date of the offence, subject to exceptions for delayed discovery.

title: Exclusion of Time in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: In computing the period of limitation, the time during which a person has been prosecuting with due diligence another prosecution relating to the same facts in good faith before a Court which is unable to entertain it due to lack of jurisdiction or similar cause shall be excluded. The period during which prosecution is stayed by injunction or order shall also be excluded. Where prior notice, consent or sanction is required for prosecution, the time required for obtaining such notice, consent or sanction shall be excluded. The period during which the offender is absent from India or is absconding or concealing himself to avoid arrest shall likewise be excluded.
simpleExplanation: Certain periods are not counted while calculating limitation, such as time spent obtaining sanction, time during a court stay, or when the accused is absconding.
examples: The limitation clock pauses while government sanction is being obtained.
Time during which the accused absconds is excluded from limitation.

faqs.question: Does time spent obtaining government sanction count toward limitation?
faqs.answer: No. Such time is excluded from the limitation period.

title: Exclusion of Date on Which Court Is Closed
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: Where the period of limitation expires on a day when the Court is closed, the Court may take cognizance on the day on which the Court reopens. A Court shall be deemed closed if it remains closed during its normal working hours.
simpleExplanation: If the limitation period ends on a court holiday, filing can be done on the next working day.
examples: A complaint due on a public holiday is filed on the next working day.

faqs.question: What happens if the last day of limitation is a holiday?
faqs.answer: The matter may be filed on the next day the Court reopens.

title: Continuing Offence
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: In the case of a continuing offence, a fresh period of limitation shall begin to run at every moment during which the offence continues.
simpleExplanation: For offences that continue over time, limitation keeps restarting as long as the unlawful situation continues.
examples: An ongoing unlawful obstruction constitutes a continuing offence and limitation renews daily.

faqs.question: What is a continuing offence?
faqs.answer: An offence that continues over a period of time rather than being completed instantly.

title: Extension of Period of Limitation in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXVIII - Limitation for Taking Cognizance of Certain Offences
officialText: Notwithstanding anything contained in the preceding provisions of this Chapter, any Court may take cognizance of an offence after the expiry of the limitation period if it is satisfied that the delay has been properly explained or that taking cognizance is necessary in the interests of justice.
simpleExplanation: Courts can condone delay and allow prosecution even after limitation has expired if justice requires it.
examples: A delayed complaint is accepted because the complainant was prevented by sufficient cause.

faqs.question: Can a Court extend limitation?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the delay is properly explained or justice requires it.

title: Trials Before High Courts
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: When an offence is tried by the High Court otherwise than under section 447, the High Court shall follow the same procedure that a Court of Session would follow while trying the case.
simpleExplanation: The High Court generally follows Sessions Court trial procedures when conducting a criminal trial.
examples: A criminal trial before the High Court follows the procedure applicable to Sessions Courts.

faqs.question: What procedure does the High Court follow during a criminal trial?
faqs.answer: The same procedure followed by a Court of Session.

title: Delivery to Commanding Officers of Persons Liable to Be Tried by Court-Martial
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: The Central Government may make rules determining whether persons subject to Army, Navy, Air Force or other Armed Forces laws shall be tried by ordinary criminal courts or by Court-martial. Magistrates shall act in accordance with such rules and may deliver the accused to the appropriate commanding officer for Court-martial. Magistrates shall also assist commanding officers in apprehending accused personnel. High Courts may direct production of prisoners before Court-martial proceedings.
simpleExplanation: This section governs the relationship between criminal courts and military courts when dealing with members of the Armed Forces.
examples: A soldier accused of an offence is handed over for Court-martial proceedings.
A Magistrate assists military authorities in securing an accused serviceman.

faqs.question: Can a member of the Armed Forces be tried by Court-martial instead of a criminal court?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on applicable laws and rules.

title: Forms
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: Subject to Article 227 of the Constitution, the forms contained in the Second Schedule, with necessary modifications according to circumstances, may be used for the purposes specified therein and shall be sufficient if so used.
simpleExplanation: The standard forms provided in the Second Schedule may be used for criminal proceedings.
examples: A prescribed warrant form from the Second Schedule is used with minor modifications.

faqs.question: Are the forms in the Second Schedule mandatory?
faqs.answer: They may be used and are considered legally sufficient when used appropriately.

title: Power of High Court to Make Rules
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: Every High Court may, with the previous approval of the State Government, make rules regulating petition writers, issuance of licences, conduct of their business, fees chargeable, penalties for contraventions, and other matters required to be provided by rules. All such rules shall be published in the Official Gazette.
simpleExplanation: High Courts can make procedural rules, especially regarding petition writers and related court practices.
punishment: Penalties may be prescribed by rules for violations.
examples: A High Court prescribes licensing requirements for petition writers.

faqs.question: Can a High Court regulate petition writers?
faqs.answer: Yes, through rules made under this section.

title: Power to Alter Functions Allocated to Executive Magistrates in Certain Cases
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: If the Legislative Assembly of a State permits by resolution, the State Government may, after consultation with the High Court, direct that references in specified provisions to an Executive Magistrate shall be construed as references to a Judicial Magistrate of the First Class.
simpleExplanation: Certain powers normally exercised by Executive Magistrates may be transferred to Judicial Magistrates in a State.
examples: A State Government transfers specified Executive Magistrate functions to Judicial Magistrates.

faqs.question: Can Judicial Magistrates perform certain Executive Magistrate functions?
faqs.answer: Yes, if authorized under this section.

title: Cases in Which Judge or Magistrate Is Personally Interested
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: No Judge or Magistrate shall, except with permission of the appellate court, try or commit for trial any case in which he is a party or personally interested. No Judge or Magistrate shall hear an appeal from a judgment or order passed by himself. Mere involvement in a public capacity or inspection of a place connected with the case does not constitute personal interest.
simpleExplanation: Judges and Magistrates must avoid hearing cases where they have a personal interest or conflict of interest.
examples: A Magistrate recuses himself from a case involving a close family member.
A Judge cannot hear an appeal against his own decision.

faqs.question: Can a Judge hear a case in which he has a personal interest?
faqs.answer: No, except in limited circumstances with proper permission.

title: Repeal and Savings
chapter: Chapter XXXIX - Miscellaneous
officialText: (1) The Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 is hereby repealed. (2) Notwithstanding such repeal— (a) if, immediately before the date on which this Sanhita comes into force, there is any appeal, application, trial, inquiry or investigation pending, such appeal, application, trial, inquiry or investigation shall be disposed of, continued, held or made in accordance with the provisions of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 as if this Sanhita had not come into force; (b) all notifications published, proclamations issued, powers conferred, forms prescribed, local jurisdictions defined, sentences passed, orders made, rules framed and appointments made under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 and in force immediately before commencement of this Sanhita shall be deemed to have been made under the corresponding provisions of this Sanhita; (c) any sanction accorded or consent given under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 under which no proceeding had commenced shall be deemed to have been accorded or given under the corresponding provisions of this Sanhita and proceedings may be commenced accordingly. (3) Where the period specified for an application or proceeding under the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 had expired before the commencement of this Sanhita, nothing in this Sanhita shall revive such right merely because a longer limitation period or extension provision is available under this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: This section formally repeals the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973 (CrPC) and replaces it with BNSS. However, pending cases, investigations, appeals, orders, notifications, sanctions and other actions taken under CrPC continue to remain valid and are protected during the transition. Expired rights or limitation periods under the old law are not revived merely because BNSS provides a longer period.
examples: A criminal trial that was pending before BNSS came into force continues under the provisions of the CrPC, 1973.
A notification issued under CrPC before BNSS commenced is treated as valid under the corresponding BNSS provision.
A prosecution sanction granted under CrPC before BNSS remains valid for initiating proceedings under BNSS.
A limitation period that expired under CrPC cannot be revived merely because BNSS provides a longer limitation period.

faqs.question: Does BNSS completely replace the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973?
faqs.answer: Yes. BNSS repeals and replaces the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973.

faqs.question: What happens to cases pending when BNSS came into force?
faqs.answer: They continue to be governed and completed under the provisions of the CrPC, 1973.

faqs.question: Are orders and notifications issued under CrPC still valid?
faqs.answer: Yes. Existing orders, notifications, powers, rules and appointments continue to be valid under corresponding BNSS provisions.

faqs.question: Can an expired limitation period under CrPC be revived because BNSS allows more time?
faqs.answer: No. BNSS does not revive rights or proceedings that had already become time-barred under CrPC.

correspondingSection: Not Applicable (This is the repealing and saving provision of BNSS)
